subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the rate of change of the area of a circle per second with respect to its radius r when r = 5 cm.
question_html: Find the rate of change of the area of a circle per second with respect to its radius r when r = 5 cm.
solution_text: The area A of a circle with radius r is given by A = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 . Therefore, the rate of change of the area A with respect to its radius r is given by {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{A}}{d r}=\frac{d}{d r}\left(\pi r^{2}\right)=2 \pi r{/tex} . When r = 5 cm, {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{A}}{d r}{/tex} = 10 {tex}\pi{/tex} . Thus, the area of the circle is changing at the rate of 10 {tex}\pi{/tex} cm 2 /s.
solution_html:
The area A of a circle with radius r is given by A = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2. Therefore, the rate of change of the area A with respect to its radius r is given by {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{A}}{d r}=\frac{d}{d r}\left(\pi r^{2}\right)=2 \pi r{/tex}.
When r = 5 cm, {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{A}}{d r}{/tex}= 10{tex}\pi{/tex}. 
Thus, the area of the circle is changing at the rate of 10{tex}\pi{/tex} cm2/s.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The volume of a cube is increasing at a rate of 9 cubic centimetres per second. How fast is the surface area increasing when the length of an edge is 10 centimetres?
question_html: The volume of a cube is increasing at a rate of 9 cubic centimetres per second. How fast is the surface area increasing when the length of an edge is 10 centimetres?
solution_text: Let x be the side, V be the volume and S be the surface area of cube {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 9c{m^3}/s{/tex} v = x 3 {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 9 = 3{x^{2}}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{3}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} Now, s = 6 x 2 {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = 12x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}= 12x \times \frac{3}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = \frac{{36}}{x}{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{ds}}{{dt}}} \right]_{x = 10}} = \frac{{36}}{{10}}{/tex} = 3.6 cm 2 /sec
solution_html:
Let x be the side, V be the volume and S be the surface area of cube
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 9c{m^3}/s{/tex}
v = x3
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 9 = 3{x^{2}}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{3}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}
Now, s = 6 x2
{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = 12x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}= 12x \times \frac{3}{{{x^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = \frac{{36}}{x}{/tex}
{tex}{\left. {\frac{{ds}}{{dt}}} \right]_{x = 10}} = \frac{{36}}{{10}}{/tex}
= 3.6 cm2 /sec

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A stone is dropped into a quiet lake and waves move in circles at a speed of 4 cm/s. At the instant, when the radius of the circular wave is 10 cm, how fast is the enclosed area increasing?
question_html: A stone is dropped into a quiet lake and waves move in circles at a speed of 4 cm/s. At the instant, when the radius of the circular wave is 10 cm, how fast is the enclosed area increasing?
solution_text: Let A be the area of the circle of radius r. Then, A = {tex}\pi r^2{/tex} Therefore, the rate of change of area A with respect to time 't' is {tex}\frac{d \mathbf{A}}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(\pi r^{2}\right)=\frac{d}{d r}\left(\pi r^{2}\right) \cdot \frac{d r}{d t}=2 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} ...(By Chain Rule) Given that {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} = 4cm/s Therefore, when r = 10, {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{A}}{d t} {/tex} = {tex}2\pi \times10\times4{/tex} = {tex}80\pi{/tex} Thus, the enclosed area is increasing at a rate of {tex}80\pi{/tex} {tex}cm^2/s{/tex} , when r = 10 cm.
solution_html:
Let A be the area of the circle of radius r.
Then,  A = {tex}\pi r^2{/tex} 
Therefore, the rate of change of area A with respect to time 't' is
{tex}\frac{d \mathbf{A}}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(\pi r^{2}\right)=\frac{d}{d r}\left(\pi r^{2}\right) \cdot \frac{d r}{d t}=2 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex}    ...(By Chain Rule)
Given that {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} = 4cm/s
Therefore, when r = 10, {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{A}}{d t} {/tex} = {tex}2\pi \times10\times4{/tex} = {tex}80\pi{/tex}
Thus, the enclosed area is increasing at a rate of {tex}80\pi{/tex} {tex}cm^2/s{/tex} , when r = 10 cm.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The length x of a rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 3 cm/minute and the width y is increasing at the rate of 2cm/minute. when x = 10cm and y = 6cm, find the rates of change of (a) the perimeter (b) the area of the rectangle.
question_html: The length x of a rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 3 cm/minute and the width y is increasing at the rate of 2cm/minute. when x = 10cm and y = 6cm, find the rates of change of (a) the perimeter (b) the area of the rectangle.
solution_text: Given {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = - 3cm/{\text{min, }}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 2cm/{\text{min}}{/tex} Let P be the perimeter {tex}P = 2\left( {x + y} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dp}}{{dx}} = 2\left( {\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex} {tex}= 2\left( { - 3 + 2} \right){/tex} {tex}= - 2cm/{\text{min}}{/tex} (i.e perimeter is decreasing) Now area of rectangle A = xy {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + y.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}= 10\left( 2 \right) + 6\left( { - 3} \right){/tex} {tex}= 20 - 18{/tex} {tex}= 2c{m^2}/{\text{min}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = - 3cm/{\text{min, }}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 2cm/{\text{min}}{/tex}

  1. Let P be the perimeter
    {tex}P = 2\left( {x + y} \right){/tex}
    {tex}\frac{{dp}}{{dx}} = 2\left( {\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex}
    {tex}= 2\left( { - 3 + 2} \right){/tex}
    {tex}= - 2cm/{\text{min}}{/tex} (i.e perimeter is decreasing)
  2. Now area of rectangle A = xy
    {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + y.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}
    {tex}= 10\left( 2 \right) + 6\left( { - 3} \right){/tex}
    {tex}= 20 - 18{/tex}
    {tex}= 2c{m^2}/{\text{min}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The total cost C(x) in Rupees, associated with the production of x units of an item is given by C(x) = 0.005x 3 - 0.02x 2 + 30x + 5000. Find the marginal cost when 3 units are produced, where by marginal cost we mean the instantaneous rate of change of total cost at any level of output.
question_html:
The total cost C(x) in Rupees, associated with the production of x units of an item is given by C(x) = 0.005x3 - 0.02x2 + 30x + 5000.
Find the marginal cost when 3 units are produced, where by marginal cost we mean the instantaneous rate of change of total cost at any level of output.
solution_text: We have, C(x) = 0.005x 3 - 0.02x 2 + 30x + 5000 The marginal cost, MC(x) = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} C(x) Now, {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (0.005x 3 - 0.02x 2 + 30x + 5000) = 0.005 {tex}\times{/tex} 3x 2 - 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 2x + 30 + 0 = 0.015 x 2 - 0.04x + 30 Marginal cost when 3 units are produced is MC(3) {tex}=0.015(9) - 0.04(3) + 30{/tex} {tex}= 0.135 - 0.12 + 30 \\ = 30.015{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, C(x) = 0.005x3 - 0.02x2 + 30x + 5000
The marginal cost, MC(x) = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex}C(x)
Now, {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex}(0.005x3 - 0.02x2 + 30x + 5000)
= 0.005 {tex}\times{/tex}3x2 - 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex}2x + 30 + 0
= 0.015 x2 - 0.04x + 30
Marginal cost when 3 units are produced is 
MC(3) {tex}=0.015(9) - 0.04(3) + 30{/tex}
{tex}= 0.135 - 0.12 + 30 \\ = 30.015{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The total revenue in Rupees received from the sale of x units of a product is given by R(x) = 3x 2 + 36x + 5. Find the marginal revenue, when x = 5, where by marginal revenue we mean the rate of change of total revenue with respect to the number of items sold at any instant.
question_html: The total revenue in Rupees received from the sale of x units of a product is given by R(x) = 3x2 + 36x + 5. Find the marginal revenue, when x = 5, where by marginal revenue we mean the rate of change of total revenue with respect to the number of items sold at any instant.
solution_text: Since Marginal Revenue is the rate of change of total revenue with respect to the number of units sold, we have Marginal Revenue (MR) = {tex}\frac{d {R}}{d x}{/tex} = 6x + 36 When x = 5, MR = 6(5) + 36 = 66 Hence, the required marginal revenue is ₹ 66.
solution_html:
Since Marginal Revenue is the rate of change of total revenue with respect to the number of units sold, we have
Marginal Revenue (MR) = {tex}\frac{d {R}}{d x}{/tex} = 6x + 36
When x = 5, MR = 6(5) + 36 = 66
Hence, the required marginal revenue is ₹ 66.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function given by f(x) = 7x – 3 is increasing on R.
question_html: Show that the function given by f(x) = 7x – 3 is increasing on R.
solution_text: Let x 1 and x 2 be any two numbers in R. Then x 1 < x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7x 1 < 7x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7x 1 – 3 < 7x 2 – 3 ​ {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x 1 ) < f(x 2 ) Thus, f is strictly increasing on R.
solution_html:
Let x1 and x2 be any two numbers in R. Then
x1 < x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7x1 < 7x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7x1 – 3 < 7x2 – 3
​{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x1) < f(x2)
Thus, f is strictly increasing on R.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function f given by f(x) = x 3 – 3x 2 + 4x, x {tex}\in{/tex} R is increasing on R.
question_html: Show that the function f given by f(x) = x3 – 3x2 + 4x, x {tex}\in{/tex} R is increasing on R.
solution_text: Not that f ′(x) = 3x 2 – 6x + 4 = 3(x 2 – 2x + 1) + 1 = 3(x – 1) 2 + 1 > 0, in every interval of R Therefore, the function f is increasing on R.
solution_html:
Not that
f ′(x) = 3x2 – 6x + 4
= 3(x2 – 2x + 1) + 1
= 3(x – 1)2 + 1 > 0, in every interval of R
Therefore, the function f is increasing on R.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function given by f(x) = cos x is decreasing in (0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ).
question_html: Prove that the function given by f(x) = cos x is decreasing in (0, {tex}\pi{/tex}).
solution_text: Note that f ′(x) = – sin x Since for each x ∈ (0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ), sin x > 0, we have f ′(x) < 0 and so f is decreasing in (0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ).
solution_html:
Note that f ′(x) = – sin x
Since for each x ∈ (0, {tex}\pi{/tex}), sin x > 0, we have f ′(x) < 0 and so f is decreasing in (0, {tex}\pi{/tex}).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function given by f(x) = cos x is increasing in ( {tex}\pi{/tex} , 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} )
question_html: Prove that the function given by f(x) = cos x is increasing in ({tex}\pi{/tex}, 2{tex}\pi{/tex})
solution_text: Note that f ′(x) = – sin x Since for each x {tex}\in{/tex} ( {tex}\pi{/tex} , 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ), sin x < 0, we have f ′(x) > 0 Therefore, f is increasing in ( {tex}\pi{/tex} , 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ).
solution_html:
Note that f ′(x) = – sin x
Since for each x {tex}\in{/tex} ({tex}\pi{/tex}, 2{tex}\pi{/tex}), sin x < 0,
we have f ′(x) > 0
Therefore, f is increasing in ({tex}\pi{/tex}, 2{tex}\pi{/tex}).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function given by f(x) = cos x is neither increasing nor decreasing in (0, 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ).
question_html: Prove that the function given by f(x) = cos x is neither increasing nor decreasing in (0, 2{tex}\pi{/tex}).
solution_text: Note that {tex}f ′(x) = – sin x{/tex} Clearly, for {tex}{/tex} {tex}(0, \pi){/tex} , we have {tex}f ′(x) = – sin x<0{/tex} So, the function is decreasing in {tex}(0, \pi){/tex} . Now, for {tex}x \in (\pi, 2\pi){/tex} we have {tex}f ′(x) = – sin x>0{/tex} Therefore, {tex}f(x){/tex} is increasing in {tex}(\pi, 2\pi){/tex} Hence, f is neither increasing nor decreasing in (0, 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ).
solution_html:
Note that {tex}f ′(x) = – sin x{/tex}
Clearly, for  {tex}{/tex}{tex}(0, \pi){/tex}, we have
{tex}f ′(x) = – sin x<0{/tex} 
So, the function is decreasing in   {tex}(0, \pi){/tex}.
Now, for {tex}x \in (\pi, 2\pi){/tex} we have
{tex}f ′(x) = – sin x>0{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}f(x){/tex} is increasing in {tex}(\pi, 2\pi){/tex}
Hence, f is neither increasing nor decreasing in (0, 2{tex}\pi{/tex}).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = x 2 – 4x + 6 is increasing decreasing
question_html:
Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = x2 – 4x + 6 is

  1. increasing
  2. decreasing
solution_text: We have f (x) = x 2 – 4x + 6 or f ′(x) = 2x – 4 Therefore, f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 2. Now the point x = 2 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals namely, (– {tex}\infty{/tex} , 2) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). In the interval (– {tex}\infty{/tex} , 2), f ′(x) = 2x – 4 < 0. And in interval (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ), {tex}f^\prime(x)=2x-4>0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (i) f is increasing in (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) and (ii) f is decreasing in (– {tex}\infty{/tex} , 2)
solution_html:
We have
f (x) = x2 – 4x + 6
or f ′(x) = 2x – 4
Therefore, f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 2.
Now the point x = 2 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals namely, (– {tex}\infty{/tex}, 2) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex}).

In the interval (– {tex}\infty{/tex}, 2), f ′(x) = 2x – 4 < 0.
And in interval (2, {tex}\infty{/tex}), {tex}f^\prime(x)=2x-4>0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}   (i) f is increasing in (2, {tex}\infty{/tex})
and (ii) f is decreasing in (– {tex}\infty{/tex}, 2)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by {tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 6{x^2} - 72x + 30{/tex} is increasing decreasing.
question_html:
Find the intervals  in which the function f given by {tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 6{x^2} - 72x + 30{/tex} is

  1. increasing
  2. decreasing.
solution_text: {tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 6{x^2} - 72x + 30{/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 12{x^2} - 12x - 72{/tex} {tex} = 12({x^2} - x - 6){/tex} {tex} = 12({x^2} - 3x +2x - 6){/tex} {tex} = 12\left[ {x\left( {x - 3} \right) + 2\left( {x - 3} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}= 12\left( {x - 3} \right)\left( {x + 2} \right){/tex} Put f ' (x) = 0 {tex}x = - 2,3{/tex} int Sign of f’(x) Result {tex}\left( { - \infty , - 2} \right){/tex} + tive Increase {tex}\left( { - 2,3} \right){/tex} + tive Decrease {tex}\left( {3,\infty } \right){/tex} + tive increase Hence function is increasing in {tex}\left( { - \infty , - 2} \right){/tex} {tex}\left( {3,\infty } \right){/tex} decreasing in {tex}\left( { - 2,3} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 6{x^2} - 72x + 30{/tex} 
{tex}f'\left( x \right) = 12{x^2} - 12x - 72{/tex} 
{tex} = 12({x^2} - x - 6){/tex} 
{tex} = 12({x^2} - 3x +2x - 6){/tex} 
{tex} = 12\left[ {x\left( {x - 3} \right) + 2\left( {x - 3} \right)} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= 12\left( {x - 3} \right)\left( {x + 2} \right){/tex} 
Put f ' (x) = 0

{tex}x = - 2,3{/tex}

int

Sign of f’(x)

Result

{tex}\left( { - \infty , - 2} \right){/tex}

+ tive

Increase

{tex}\left( { - 2,3} \right){/tex}

+ tive

Decrease

{tex}\left( {3,\infty } \right){/tex}

+ tive

increase

Hence function is

  1. increasing in {tex}\left( { - \infty , - 2} \right){/tex} 
  2. {tex}\left( {3,\infty } \right){/tex} decreasing in {tex}\left( { - 2,3} \right){/tex}

question_text: Find intervals in which the function given by f (x) = sin 3x, x {tex}\in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} is (a) increasing (b) decreasing.
question_html: Find intervals in which the function given by f (x) = sin 3x, x {tex}\in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} is (a) increasing (b) decreasing.
solution_text: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \sin 3x{/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 3\cos 3x{/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\cos 3x = 0{/tex} {tex}3x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} S A T C int. Sign of f’(x) Result {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{6}} \right){/tex} +tive increase {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{6},\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex} -tive Decrease Hence, f(x) is increasing on {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{6}} \right){/tex} and decreasing on {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{6},\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f\left( x \right) = \sin 3x{/tex} 
{tex}f'\left( x \right) = 3\cos 3x{/tex} 
{tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\cos 3x = 0{/tex} 
{tex}3x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} 
{tex}x = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} 

S A
T C

int.

Sign of f’(x)

Result

{tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{6}} \right){/tex}

+tive

increase

{tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{6},\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}

-tive

Decrease

Hence, f(x) is increasing on{tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{6}} \right){/tex}and decreasing on {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{6},\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by {tex}f(x) = \sin x + \cos x,0 \leq x \leq 2\pi {/tex} is increasing or decreasing.
question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f given by {tex}f(x) = \sin x + \cos x,0 \leq x \leq 2\pi {/tex} is increasing or decreasing.
solution_text: The given function is: {tex}f(x) = \sin x + \cos x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f'(x) = -\sin x + \cos x{/tex} Now, let f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos x - \sin x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\sin x}}{{\cos x}} = \frac{{\cos x}}{{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\tan x = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{4},\frac{{5\pi }}{4}{/tex} {tex}0 \leqslant x \leqslant 2\pi {/tex} int Sign of f’(x) Result {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} +ve Increase {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{4},\frac{{5\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex} -ve Decrease {tex}\left( {\frac{{5\pi }}{4},2\pi } \right){/tex} +ve increase
solution_html:
The given function is: {tex}f(x) = \sin x + \cos x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f'(x) = -\sin x + \cos x{/tex}
Now, let f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos x - \sin x = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\sin x}}{{\cos x}} = \frac{{\cos x}}{{\cos x}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\tan x = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{4},\frac{{5\pi }}{4}{/tex}
{tex}0 \leqslant x \leqslant 2\pi {/tex}

int Sign of f’(x) Result
{tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} +ve Increase
{tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{4},\frac{{5\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex} -ve Decrease
{tex}\left( {\frac{{5\pi }}{4},2\pi } \right){/tex} +ve increase

question_text: Find the maximum and the minimum values, if any, of the function f given by f(x) = x 2 , x {tex}\in{/tex} R.
question_html:
Find the maximum and the minimum values, if any, of the function f given by
f(x) = x2 , x {tex}\in{/tex} R.
solution_text: From the graph of the given function we have f(x) = 0 if x = 0. Also f(x) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, for all x {tex}\in{/tex} R. Therefore, the minimum value of f is 0 and the point of minimum value of f is x = 0. Further, it may be observed from the graph of the function that f has no maximum value and hence no point of a maximum value of f in R. Note: If we restrict the domain of f to [– 2, 1] only, then f will have maximum value at {tex}x=-2,{/tex} and equal to {tex}f(-2)=(-2)^2=4.{/tex}
solution_html:
From the graph of the given function

we have f(x) = 0 if x = 0. Also
f(x) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, for all x {tex}\in{/tex} R. Therefore, the minimum value of f is 0 and the point of minimum value of f is x = 0. Further, it may be observed from the graph of the function that f has no maximum value and hence no point of a maximum value of f in R.
Note: If we restrict the domain of f to [– 2, 1] only, then f will have maximum value at {tex}x=-2,{/tex} and equal to {tex}f(-2)=(-2)^2=4.{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the maximum and the minimum values, if any, of the function given by f(x) = x, x {tex}\in{/tex} (0, 1).
question_html: Find the maximum and the minimum values, if any, of the function given by f(x) = x, x {tex}\in{/tex} (0, 1).
solution_text: The given function is an increasing (strictly) function in the given interval (0, 1). From the graph of the function f, it seems that it should have the minimum value at a point closest to 0 on its right and the maximum value at a point closest to 1 on its left. It is not possible to locate such points. In fact, if a point x 0 is closest to 0, then we find {tex}\frac{x_{0}}{2}{/tex} < x for all x 0 {tex}\in{/tex} (0,1) . Also, if x 1 is closest to 1, then {tex}\frac{x_{1}+1}{2}{/tex} > x 1 for all x 1 {tex}\in{/tex} (0,1) Therefore, the given function has neither the maximum value nor the minimum value in the interval (0,1).
solution_html:
The given function is an increasing (strictly) function in the given interval (0, 1). From the graph

of the function f, it seems that it should have the minimum value at a point closest to 0 on its right and the maximum value at a point closest to 1 on its left. It is not possible to locate such points. In fact, if a point x0 is closest to 0, then we find {tex}\frac{x_{0}}{2}{/tex} < x for all x0 {tex}\in{/tex}(0,1) . Also, if x1 is closest to 1, then {tex}\frac{x_{1}+1}{2}{/tex} > x1 for all x1 {tex}\in{/tex}(0,1)
Therefore, the given function has neither the maximum value nor the minimum value in the interval (0,1).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find all points of local maxima and local minima of the function f given by f(x) = x 3 – 3x + 3.
question_html: Find all points of local maxima and local minima of the function f given by f(x) = x3 – 3x + 3.
solution_text: We have, f(x) = x 3 – 3x + 3 or f ′(x) = 3x 2 – 3 = 3(x – 1) (x + 1) or f ′(x) = 0 at x = 1 and x = – 1 Thus, x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 1 are the only critical points which could possibly be the points of local maxima and/or local minima of f . Let us first examine the point x = 1. Note that for values close to 1 and to the right of 1, f ′(x) > 0 and for values close to 1 and to the left of 1, f ′(x) < 0. Therefore, by first derivative test, x = 1 is a point of local minima and local minimum value is f (1) = 1. In the case of x = –1, note that f ′(x) > 0, for values close to and to the left of –1 and f ′(x) < 0, for values close to and to the right of – 1. Therefore, by first derivative test, x = – 1 is a point of local maxima and local maximum value is f(–1) = 5. Values of x Sign of f′(x) = 3(x – 1) (x + 1) Close to 1 to the right (say 1.1 etc.) > 0 to the left (say 0.9 etc.) < 0 Close to –1 to the right (say 0.9 etc.) < 0 to the left (say 1.1 etc.) > 0
solution_html:
We have,
f(x) = x3 – 3x + 3
or f ′(x) = 3x2 – 3 = 3(x – 1) (x + 1)
or f ′(x) = 0 at x = 1 and x = – 1
Thus, x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 1 are the only critical points which could possibly be the points of local maxima and/or local minima of f . Let us first examine the point x = 1.
Note that for values close to 1 and to the right of 1, f ′(x) > 0 and for values close to 1 and to the left of 1, f ′(x) < 0. Therefore, by first derivative test, x = 1 is a point of local minima and local minimum value is f (1) = 1.
In the case of x = –1, note that f ′(x) > 0, for values close to and to the left of –1 and f ′(x) < 0, for values close to and to the right of – 1. Therefore, by first derivative test, x = – 1 is a point of local maxima and local maximum value is f(–1) = 5.

Values of x Sign of f′(x) = 3(x – 1) (x + 1)
Close to 1 to the right (say 1.1 etc.) > 0
to the left (say 0.9 etc.) < 0
Close to –1 to the right (say 0.9 etc.) < 0
to the left (say 1.1 etc.) > 0

question_text: Find all the points of local maxima and local minima of the function f given by f(x) = 2x 3 – 6x 2 + 6x +5.
question_html: Find all the points of local maxima and local minima of the function f given by f(x) = 2x3 – 6x2 + 6x +5.
solution_text: We have f (x) = 2x 3 – 6x 2 + 6x + 5 or f ′(x) = 6x 2 – 12x + 6 = 6 (x – 1) 2 Now, f ′(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 Thus, x = 1 is the only critical point of f . We shall now examine this point for local maxima and/or local minima of f. Observe that f ′(x) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, for all x {tex}\in{/tex} R and in particular f ′(x) > 0, for values close to 1, to the left and to the right of 1. Therefore, by first derivative test, the point x = 1 is neither a point of local maxima nor a point of local minima. Hence x = 1 is a point of inflexion.
solution_html:
We have
f (x) = 2x3 – 6x2 + 6x + 5
or f ′(x) = 6x2 – 12x + 6 = 6 (x – 1)2
Now, f ′(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1
Thus, x = 1 is the only critical point of f . We shall now examine this point for local maxima and/or local minima of f. Observe that f ′(x) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, for all x {tex}\in{/tex} R and in particular f ′(x) > 0, for values close to 1, to the left and to the right of 1. Therefore, by first derivative test, the point x = 1 is neither a point of local maxima nor a point of local minima. Hence x = 1 is a point of inflexion.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find local minimum value of the function f given by f (x) = 3 + |x|, x {tex}\in{/tex} R.
question_html: Find local minimum value of the function f given by f (x) = 3 + |x|, x {tex}\in{/tex} R.
solution_text: Note that the given function is not differentiable at x = 0. So, second derivative test fails. Function can be written as: f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{lc}3-x;&x\;\leq\;0\\3+x;&x\;\geq\;0\end{array}\right.{/tex} Clearly, {tex}x=0, {/tex} is the critical point. {tex}f^\prime(x){/tex} = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{lc}-1;&x\;\leq\;0\\1;&x\;\geq\;0\end{array}\right.{/tex} So, f is decreasing on the left of '0' and increasing on the right of '0'. Hence {tex}x=0{/tex} is the point of minimum and the minimum value of 'f' is f(0)= 3.
solution_html:
Note that the given function is not differentiable at x = 0. So, second derivative test fails.
Function can be written as: 
f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{lc}3-x;&x\;\leq\;0\\3+x;&x\;\geq\;0\end{array}\right.{/tex}
Clearly, {tex}x=0, {/tex}  is the critical point. 
  {tex}f^\prime(x){/tex} = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{lc}-1;&x\;\leq\;0\\1;&x\;\geq\;0\end{array}\right.{/tex}
 So, f is decreasing on the left of '0' and increasing on the right of '0'. Hence {tex}x=0{/tex} is the point of minimum and the minimum value of 'f' is f(0)= 3.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find local maximum and local minimum values of the function f given by f (x) = 3x 4 + 4x 3 – 12x 2 + 12
question_html: Find local maximum and local minimum values of the function f given by f (x) = 3x4 + 4x3 – 12x2 + 12
solution_text: We have f (x) = 3x 4 + 4x 3 – 12x 2 + 12 or f ′(x) = 12x 3 + 12x 2 – 24x = 12x (x – 1) (x + 2) or f ′(x) = 0 at x = 0, x = 1 and x = – 2. Now f ″(x) = 36x 2 + 24x – 24 = 12 (3x 2 + 2x – 2) or {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {f^{\prime \prime}(0)=-24<0} \\ {f^{\prime \prime}(1)=36>0} \\ {f^{\prime \prime}(-2)=72>0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Therefore, by second derivative test, x = 0 is a point of local maxima and local maximum value of f at x = 0 is f (0) = 12 While x = 1 and x = – 2 are the points of local minima and local minimum values of f at x = 1 and – 2 are f (1) = 7 and f (–2) = –20, respectively.
solution_html:
We have
f (x) = 3x4 + 4x3 – 12x2 + 12
or f ′(x) = 12x3 + 12x2 – 24x = 12x (x – 1) (x + 2)
or f ′(x) = 0 at x = 0, x = 1 and x = – 2.
Now f ″(x) = 36x2 + 24x – 24 = 12 (3x2 + 2x – 2)
or {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {f^{\prime \prime}(0)=-24<0} \\ {f^{\prime \prime}(1)=36>0} \\ {f^{\prime \prime}(-2)=72>0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Therefore, by second derivative test, x = 0 is a point of local maxima and local maximum value of f at x = 0 is
f (0) = 12
While x = 1 and x = – 2 are the points of local minima and local minimum values of f at x = 1 and – 2 are
f (1) = 7 and f (–2) = –20, respectively.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find two positive numbers whose sum is 15 and the sum of whose squares is minimum.
question_html: Find two positive numbers whose sum is 15 and the sum of whose squares is minimum.
solution_text: Let one of the numbers be x. Then the other number is (15 – x). Let S(x) denote the sum of the squares of these numbers. Then S(x) = x 2 + (15 – x) 2 = 2x 2 – 30x + 225 or {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} {S^{\prime}(x)=4 x-30} \\ {S^{\prime \prime}(x)=4} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Now S'(x) = 0, gives, x = {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex} . Also S'' {tex}\left(\frac{15}{2}\right){/tex} = 4 > 0. Therefore, by second derivative test, x = {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex} is the point of local minima of S. Hence the sum of squares of numbers is minimum when the numbers are {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex} and 15 - {tex}\frac{15}{2}=\frac{15}{2}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let one of the numbers be x. Then the other number is (15 – x).
Let S(x) denote the sum of the squares of these numbers. Then
S(x) = x2 + (15 – x)2 = 2x2 – 30x + 225
or {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} {S^{\prime}(x)=4 x-30} \\ {S^{\prime \prime}(x)=4} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Now S'(x) = 0, gives, x = {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex}.
Also S''{tex}\left(\frac{15}{2}\right){/tex} = 4 > 0. 
Therefore, by second derivative test, x = {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex} is the point of local minima of S. Hence the sum of squares of numbers is minimum when the numbers are {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex} and 15 -{tex}\frac{15}{2}=\frac{15}{2}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the shortest distance of the point (0, c) from the parabola y = x 2 , where {tex}\frac{1}{2} \leq c \leq 5{/tex} .
question_html: Find the shortest distance of the point (0, c) from the parabola y = x2, where {tex}\frac{1}{2} \leq c \leq 5{/tex}.
solution_text: Let (h, k) be any point on the parabola y = x 2 , and D be the required distance between (h, k) and (0, c). Then {tex}\mathrm{D}=\sqrt{(h-0)^{2}+(k-c)^{2}}=\sqrt{h^{2}+(k-c)^{2}}{/tex} ....(1) Since (h, k) lies on the parabola y = x 2 , we have k = h 2 . So (1) gives {tex}\mathrm{D} \ = \mathrm{D}(k)=\sqrt{k+(k-c)^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{D}^{\prime}(k)=\frac{1+2(k-c)}{2 \sqrt{k+(k-c)^{2}}}{/tex} Now D′(k) = 0 gives k = {tex}\frac{2 c-1}{2}{/tex} Observe that when k < {tex}\frac{2 c-1}{2}{/tex} , then 2( k - c) + 1 < 0, i.e., D'(k) < 0. Also when {tex}k>\frac{2 c-1}{2}{/tex} , then D'(k) > 0. So, by first derivative test, D(k) is minimum at k = {tex}\frac{2 c-1}{2}{/tex} . Hence, the required shortest distance is given by {tex}\mathrm{D}\left(\frac{2 c-1}{2}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{2 c-1}{2}+\left(\frac{2 c-1}{2}-c\right)^{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{4 c-1}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let (h, k) be any point on the parabola y = x2, and D be the required distance between (h, k) and (0, c). Then
{tex}\mathrm{D}=\sqrt{(h-0)^{2}+(k-c)^{2}}=\sqrt{h^{2}+(k-c)^{2}}{/tex} ....(1)
Since (h, k) lies on the parabola y = x2 , we have k = h2 . So (1) gives
{tex}\mathrm{D} \ = \mathrm{D}(k)=\sqrt{k+(k-c)^{2}}{/tex}
or  {tex}\mathrm{D}^{\prime}(k)=\frac{1+2(k-c)}{2 \sqrt{k+(k-c)^{2}}}{/tex}
Now D′(k) = 0 gives k = {tex}\frac{2 c-1}{2}{/tex}
Observe that when k < {tex}\frac{2 c-1}{2}{/tex} , then 2( k - c) + 1 < 0, i.e., D'(k) < 0. 
Also when {tex}k>\frac{2 c-1}{2}{/tex}, then D'(k) > 0.
So, by first derivative test, D(k) is minimum at k = {tex}\frac{2 c-1}{2}{/tex}.
Hence, the required shortest distance is given by
{tex}\mathrm{D}\left(\frac{2 c-1}{2}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{2 c-1}{2}+\left(\frac{2 c-1}{2}-c\right)^{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{4 c-1}}{2}{/tex}

question_text: Let AP and BQ be two vertical poles at points A and B, respectively. If AP = 16 m, BQ = 22 m and AB = 20 m, then find the distance of a point R on AB from the point A such that RP 2 + RQ 2 is minimum.
question_html: Let AP and BQ be two vertical poles at points A and B, respectively. If AP = 16 m, BQ = 22 m and AB = 20 m, then find the distance of a point R on AB from the point A such that RP2 + RQ2 is minimum.
solution_text: Let R be a point on AB such that AR = x m. Then RB = (20 – x) m (as AB = 20 m). From figure We have, RP 2 = AR 2 + AP 2 and RQ 2 = RB 2 + BQ 2 Therefore RP 2 + RQ 2 = AR 2 + AP 2 + RB 2 + BQ 2 = x 2 + (16) 2 + (20 – x) 2 + (22) 2 = 2x 2 – 40x + 1140 Let S ≡ S(x) = RP 2 + RQ 2 = 2x 2 – 40x + 1140. Therefore S′(x) = 4x – 40. Now S′(x) = 0 gives x = 10. Also S″(x) = 4 > 0, for all x and so S″(10) > 0. Therefore, by second derivative test, x = 10 is the point of local minima of S. Thus, the distance of R from A on AB is AR = x =10 m.
solution_html:
Let R be a point on AB such that AR = x m. Then RB = (20 – x) m (as AB = 20 m).
From figure 

We have,
RP2 = AR2 + AP2
and RQ2 = RB2 + BQ2
Therefore RP2 + RQ2 = AR2 + AP2 + RB2 + BQ2
= x2 + (16)2 + (20 – x)2 + (22)2
= 2x2 – 40x + 1140
Let S ≡ S(x) = RP2 + RQ2 = 2x2 – 40x + 1140.
Therefore S′(x) = 4x – 40.
Now S′(x) = 0 gives x = 10. Also S″(x) = 4 > 0, for all x and so S″(10) > 0. Therefore, by second derivative test, x = 10 is the point of local minima of S. Thus, the distance of R from A on AB is AR = x =10 m.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If length of three sides of a trapezium other than base are equal to 10cm, then find the area of the trapezium when it is maximum.
question_html: If length of three sides of a trapezium other than base are equal to 10cm, then find the area of the trapezium when it is maximum.
solution_text: Let ABCD be the given trapezium in which AD = BC = CD = 10 cm. Draw a perpendicular DP and CQ on AB. Let AP = x cm In {tex}\Delta{/tex} APD & {tex}\Delta{/tex} BQC, {tex}\angle APD=\angle BQC{/tex} [each =90 {tex}\circ{/tex} ] AD = BC [Both 10 cm] DP = CQ [Perpendicular between parallel lines are equal in length] {tex}\therefore \quad \Delta A P D \cong \Delta B Q C{/tex} [RHS Congruency] {tex}\therefore QB=AP{/tex} [CPCT] {tex}\Rightarrow QB=x \;cm{/tex} {tex}D P = \sqrt { 10 ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \quad{/tex} [by Pythagoras theorem] Now, area of trapezium, {tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( \text { sum of parallel sides } ) \times{height}{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( 2 x + 10 + 10 ) \times \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= ( x + 10 ) \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .....(i) We need to find the area of trapezium when it is maximum i.e. we need to maximize area. On differentiating both sides of eq(i) w.r.t.x, we get {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = ( x + 10 ) \frac { ( - 2 x ) } { 2 \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } } + \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 - x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} ....(ii) For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } } = 0{/tex} = -2 (x 2 + 5x - 50) =0 = -2 (x+10) (x - 5) =0 x = 5 or -10 Since, x represents distance, so it cannot be negative. Therefore, we take x = 5. On diffentiating both sides of eq.(ii) w.r.t.x, we get {tex}\frac{\mathrm d^2A}{\mathrm dx^2}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot \frac{d}{dx}(-2x^2-10x+100)-(-2x^2-10x+100)\frac{d}{dx}(\sqrt{100-x^2})}{(\sqrt{100-x^2})^2}{/tex} [by using the quotient rule of derivative] {tex}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot (-4x-10)-(-2x^2-10x+100)(\frac{-2x}{2\sqrt{100-x^2}})}{(\sqrt{100-x^2})^2}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot (-4x-10)+\frac{x(-2x^2-10x+100)}{\sqrt{100-x^2}})}{{100-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{({100-x^2})\cdot (-4x-10)+{x(-2x^2-10x+100})}{{(100-x^2})^\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-400x+4x^3-1000+10x^2-2x^3-10x^2+100x}{{(100-x^2})^\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm d^2A}{\mathrm dx^2}= \frac { 2 x ^ { 3 } - 300 x - 1000 } { \left( 100 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex} When x = 5, {tex} \frac {\mathrm d ^ { 2 } A } {\mathrm d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 ( 5 ) ^ { 3 } - 300 ( 5 ) - 1000 } { \left[ 100 - ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } \right] ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 250 - 1500 - 1000 } { ( 100 - 25 ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } = \frac { - 2250 } { 75 \sqrt { 75 } } < 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} It is maximum when x = 5 Thus, area of trapezium is maximum at x = 5 and maximum area is {tex}A _ { \max } = ( 5 + 10 ) \sqrt { 100 - ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } } {/tex} [put x = 5 in Eq. (i)] {tex}= 15 \sqrt { 100 - 25 } = 15 \sqrt { 75 } = 75 \sqrt { 3 }\; \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let ABCD be the given trapezium in which AD = BC = CD = 10 cm.
Draw a perpendicular DP and CQ on AB. Let AP = x cm
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}APD & {tex}\Delta{/tex}BQC, 
{tex}\angle APD=\angle BQC{/tex}  [each =90{tex}\circ{/tex}]
AD = BC [Both 10 cm]
DP = CQ [Perpendicular between parallel lines are equal in length]
{tex}\therefore \quad \Delta A P D \cong \Delta B Q C{/tex} [RHS Congruency]
{tex}\therefore QB=AP{/tex} [CPCT]
{tex}\Rightarrow QB=x \;cm{/tex}
{tex}D P = \sqrt { 10 ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \quad{/tex}[by Pythagoras theorem]
Now, area of trapezium,
{tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( \text { sum of parallel sides } ) \times{height}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( 2 x + 10 + 10 ) \times \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= ( x + 10 ) \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}.....(i)

We need to find the area of trapezium when it is maximum i.e. we need to maximize area.
On differentiating both sides of eq(i) w.r.t.x, we get
{tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = ( x + 10 ) \frac { ( - 2 x ) } { 2 \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } } + \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 - x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}....(ii)
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } } = 0{/tex}
= -2 (x2 + 5x - 50) =0
= -2 (x+10) (x - 5) =0
x = 5 or -10
Since, x represents distance, so it cannot be negative.
Therefore, we take x = 5.
On diffentiating both sides of eq.(ii) w.r.t.x, we get
{tex}\frac{\mathrm d^2A}{\mathrm dx^2}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot \frac{d}{dx}(-2x^2-10x+100)-(-2x^2-10x+100)\frac{d}{dx}(\sqrt{100-x^2})}{(\sqrt{100-x^2})^2}{/tex} [by using the quotient rule of derivative]

{tex}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot (-4x-10)-(-2x^2-10x+100)(\frac{-2x}{2\sqrt{100-x^2}})}{(\sqrt{100-x^2})^2}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot (-4x-10)+\frac{x(-2x^2-10x+100)}{\sqrt{100-x^2}})}{{100-x^2}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{({100-x^2})\cdot (-4x-10)+{x(-2x^2-10x+100})}{{(100-x^2})^\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}

{tex}=\frac{-400x+4x^3-1000+10x^2-2x^3-10x^2+100x}{{(100-x^2})^\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm d^2A}{\mathrm dx^2}= \frac { 2 x ^ { 3 } - 300 x - 1000 } { \left( 100 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex}

When x = 5,

 {tex} \frac {\mathrm d ^ { 2 } A } {\mathrm d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 ( 5 ) ^ { 3 } - 300 ( 5 ) - 1000 } { \left[ 100 - ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } \right] ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 250 - 1500 - 1000 } { ( 100 - 25 ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } = \frac { - 2250 } { 75 \sqrt { 75 } } < 0{/tex}

{tex}\therefore {/tex} It is maximum when x = 5
Thus, area of trapezium is maximum at x = 5 and maximum area is
{tex}A _ { \max } = ( 5 + 10 ) \sqrt { 100 - ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } } {/tex} [put x = 5 in Eq. (i)]
{tex}= 15 \sqrt { 100 - 25 } = 15 \sqrt { 75 } = 75 \sqrt { 3 }\; \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 }{/tex}

question_text: Prove that the radius of the right circular cylinder of greatest curved surface area, which can be inscribed in a given cone, is half of that of the cone.
question_html: Prove that the radius of the right circular cylinder of greatest curved surface area, which can be inscribed in a given cone, is half of that of the cone.
solution_text: Let OC = r be the radius of the cone and OA = h be its height. Let a cylinder with radius OE = x inscribed in the given cone The height QE of the cylinder is given by {tex}\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{\mathrm{O} \mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{EC}}{\mathrm{OC}}{/tex} (since {tex}\Delta{/tex} QEC {tex} \sim {/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} AOC) or {tex}\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{h}=\frac{r-x}{r}{/tex} or QE = {tex}\frac{h(r-x)}{r}{/tex} Let S be the curved surface area of the given cylinder. Then S ≡ S( {tex}x{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{2 \pi x h(r-x)}{r}=\frac{2 \pi h}{r}\left(r x-x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow~~ {S^{\prime}(x)=\frac{2 \pi h}{r}(r-2 x)} \\ \Rightarrow~~{S^{{\prime}{\prime}}(x)=\frac{-4 \pi h}{r}}{/tex} Now S' ( {tex}x{/tex} ) = 0 gives x = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex} . Since S″( {tex}x{/tex} ) < 0 for all x, S'' {tex}\left(\frac{r}{2}\right){/tex} < 0. So, {tex}x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex} is a point of maxima of S. Hence, the radius of the cylinder of greatest curved surface area which can be inscribed in a given cone is half of that of the cone.
solution_html:
Let OC = r be the radius of the cone and OA = h be its height. Let a cylinder with radius OE = x inscribed in the given cone

The height QE of the cylinder is given by {tex}\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{\mathrm{O} \mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{EC}}{\mathrm{OC}}{/tex} (since {tex}\Delta{/tex}QEC {tex} \sim {/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}AOC)
or {tex}\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{h}=\frac{r-x}{r}{/tex}
or QE = {tex}\frac{h(r-x)}{r}{/tex}
Let S be the curved surface area of the given cylinder. Then
S ≡ S({tex}x{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{2 \pi x h(r-x)}{r}=\frac{2 \pi h}{r}\left(r x-x^{2}\right){/tex}
 {tex}\Rightarrow~~ {S^{\prime}(x)=\frac{2 \pi h}{r}(r-2 x)} \\ \Rightarrow~~{S^{{\prime}{\prime}}(x)=\frac{-4 \pi h}{r}}{/tex}
Now S' ({tex}x{/tex}) = 0 gives x = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex}. 
Since S″({tex}x{/tex}) < 0 for all x, S''{tex}\left(\frac{r}{2}\right){/tex} < 0.
So, {tex}x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex} is a point of maxima of S. Hence, the radius of the cylinder of greatest curved surface area which can be inscribed in a given cone is half of that of the cone.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the absolute maximum and minimum values of a function f given by f (x) = 2x 3 – 15x 2 + 36x +1 on the interval [1, 5].
question_html:
Find the absolute maximum and minimum values of a function f given by
f (x) = 2x3 – 15x2 + 36x +1 on the interval [1, 5].
solution_text: We have f(x) = 2x 3 – 15x 2 + 36x + 1 or f ′(x) = 6x 2 – 30x + 36 = 6 (x – 3) (x – 2) Note that f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 2 and x = 3. We shall now evaluate the value of f at these points and at the end points of the interval [1, 5], i.e., at x = 1, x = 2, x = 3 and at x = 5. So f(1) = 2(1 3 ) – 15(1 2 ) + 36 (1) + 1 = 24 f(2) = 2(2 3 ) – 15(2 2 ) + 36 (2) + 1 = 29 f(3) = 2(3 3 ) – 15(3 2 ) + 36 (3) + 1 = 28 f(5) = 2(5 3 ) – 15(5 2 ) + 36 (5) + 1 = 56 Thus, we conclude that absolute maximum value of f on [1, 5] is 56, occurring at x =5, and absolute minimum value of f on [1, 5] is 24 which occurs at x = 1.
solution_html:
We have
f(x) = 2x3 – 15x2 + 36x + 1
or f ′(x) = 6x2 – 30x + 36 = 6 (x – 3) (x – 2)
Note that f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 2 and x = 3.
We shall now evaluate the value of f at these points and at the end points of the interval [1, 5], i.e., at x = 1, x = 2, x = 3 and at x = 5. So
f(1) = 2(13) – 15(12) + 36 (1) + 1 = 24
f(2) = 2(23) – 15(22) + 36 (2) + 1 = 29
f(3) = 2(33) – 15(32) + 36 (3) + 1 = 28
f(5) = 2(53) – 15(52) + 36 (5) + 1 = 56
Thus, we conclude that absolute maximum value of f on [1, 5] is 56, occurring at x =5, and absolute minimum value of f on [1, 5] is 24 which occurs at x = 1.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find absolute maximum and minimum values of a function f given by {tex}f(x)=12 x^{\frac{4}{3}}-6 x^{\frac{1}{3}}, x \in[-1,1]{/tex}
question_html: Find absolute maximum and minimum values of a function f given by {tex}f(x)=12 x^{\frac{4}{3}}-6 x^{\frac{1}{3}}, x \in[-1,1]{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}f(x)=12 x^{\frac{4}{3}}-6 x^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} or f'( {tex}x{/tex} ) = {tex}16 x^{\frac{1}{3}}-\frac{2}{x^{\frac{2}{3}}}=\frac{2(8 x-1)}{x^{\frac{2}{3}}}{/tex} Thus, f ′( {tex}x{/tex} ) = 0 gives x = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} . Further note that f ′(x) is not defined at x = 0. So the critical points are x = 0 and x = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} . Now evaluating the value of f at critical points x = 0, {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} and at end points of the interval x = -1 and x = 1, we have {tex}f (–1) = 12(-1)^\frac43 -6(-1)^\frac13 = 18{/tex} {tex}f (0) = 12 (0) – 6(0) = 0{/tex} {tex}f\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)=12\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{\frac{4}{3}}-6\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{-9}{4}{/tex} {tex}f(1)=12(1)^{\frac{4}{3}}-6(1)^{\frac{1}{3}}=6{/tex} Hence, we conclude that absolute maximum value of f is 18 that occurs at x = –1 and absolute minimum value of f is {tex}\frac{-9}{4}{/tex} that occurs at x = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} .
solution_html:
We have
{tex}f(x)=12 x^{\frac{4}{3}}-6 x^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
or f'({tex}x{/tex}) = {tex}16 x^{\frac{1}{3}}-\frac{2}{x^{\frac{2}{3}}}=\frac{2(8 x-1)}{x^{\frac{2}{3}}}{/tex}
Thus, f ′({tex}x{/tex}) = 0 gives x = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}. Further note that f ′(x) is not defined at x = 0. So the critical points are x = 0 and x = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}. 
Now evaluating the value of f at critical points x = 0, {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} and at end points of the interval x = -1 and x = 1, we have
{tex}f (–1) = 12(-1)^\frac43 -6(-1)^\frac13  = 18{/tex}
{tex}f (0) = 12 (0) – 6(0) = 0{/tex}
{tex}f\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)=12\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{\frac{4}{3}}-6\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{-9}{4}{/tex}
{tex}f(1)=12(1)^{\frac{4}{3}}-6(1)^{\frac{1}{3}}=6{/tex}
Hence, we conclude that absolute maximum value of f is 18 that occurs at x = –1 and absolute minimum value of f is {tex}\frac{-9}{4}{/tex} that occurs at x = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: An Apache helicopter of enemy is flying along the curve given by y = x 2 + 7. A soldier, placed at (3, 7), wants to shoot down the helicopter when it is nearest to him. Find the nearest distance.
question_html: An Apache helicopter of enemy is flying along the curve given by y = x2 + 7. A soldier, placed at (3, 7), wants to shoot down the helicopter when it is nearest to him. Find the nearest distance.
solution_text: For each value of x, the position of the Helicopter is at point {tex}(x, x^2 + 7){/tex} . Therefore, the distance between the helicopter and the soldier placed at (3,7) is given by {tex}|(x, x^2+7)-(3, 7)|=\sqrt{(x-3)^{2}+\left(x^{2}+7-7\right)^{2}}= \sqrt{(x-3)^{2}+x^{4}}{/tex} Let {tex}f (x) = (x – 3)^2 + x^4{/tex} or {tex} f ′(x) = 2(x – 3) + 4x^3 =2(x-1)(2x^2+2x+3){/tex} Thus, f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 1 or 2x 2 + 2x + 3 = 0 for which there are no real roots. Also, there are no end points of the interval to be added to the set for which f ′ is zero, i.e., there is only one point, namely, x = 1. The value of f at this point is given by f (1) = (1 – 3) 2 + (1) 4 = 5. Thus, the distance between the solider and the helicopter is {tex}\sqrt{f(1)}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} . Note that {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is either a maximum value or a minimim value. Since {tex}\sqrt{f(0)}=\sqrt{(0-3)^{2}+(0)^{4}}=3>\sqrt{5}{/tex} , it follows that {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is the minimim value of {tex}\sqrt{f(x)}{/tex} . Hence, {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is the minimum distance between the soldier and the helicopter.
solution_html:
For each value of x, the position of the Helicopter is at point {tex}(x, x^2 + 7){/tex}. Therefore, the distance between the helicopter and the soldier placed at (3,7) is given by
{tex}|(x, x^2+7)-(3, 7)|=\sqrt{(x-3)^{2}+\left(x^{2}+7-7\right)^{2}}= \sqrt{(x-3)^{2}+x^{4}}{/tex}
Let   {tex}f (x) = (x – 3)^2 + x^4{/tex}
or    {tex} f ′(x) = 2(x – 3) + 4x^3 =2(x-1)(2x^2+2x+3){/tex}
Thus, f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 1 or 2x2 + 2x + 3 = 0 for which there are no real roots. Also, there are no end points of the interval to be added to the set for which f ′ is zero, i.e., there is only one point, namely, x = 1. The value of f at this point is given by
f (1) = (1 – 3)2 + (1)4 = 5.
Thus, the distance between the solider and the helicopter is {tex}\sqrt{f(1)}=\sqrt{5}{/tex}.
Note that {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is either a maximum value or a minimim value.
Since
{tex}\sqrt{f(0)}=\sqrt{(0-3)^{2}+(0)^{4}}=3>\sqrt{5}{/tex},
it follows that {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is the minimim value of {tex}\sqrt{f(x)}{/tex}. Hence, {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is the minimum distance between the soldier and the helicopter.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A car starts from a point P at time t = 0 seconds and stops at point Q. The distance x, in metres, covered by it, in t seconds is given by {tex}x=t^{2}\left(2-\frac{t}{3}\right){/tex} Find the time taken by it to reach Q and also find distance between P and Q.
question_html:
A car starts from a point P at time t = 0 seconds and stops at point Q. The distance x, in metres, covered by it, in t seconds is given by
{tex}x=t^{2}\left(2-\frac{t}{3}\right){/tex}
Find the time taken by it to reach Q and also find distance between P and Q.
solution_text: Let v be the velocity of the car at t seconds. Now {tex}x=t^{2}\left(2-\frac{t}{3}\right){/tex} Therefore {tex}v=\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = 4t – t 2 = t(4 – t) Clearly, v = 0 gives t = 0 and t = 4 Now v = 0 at P as well as at Q and at P, t = 0. So, at Q, t = 4. Thus, the car will reach the point Q after 4 seconds. Also the distance travelled in 4 seconds is given by {tex}x]_{t=4}=4^{2}\left(2-\frac{4}{3}\right)=16\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)=\frac{32}{3}{/tex} m
solution_html:
Let v be the velocity of the car at t seconds.
Now {tex}x=t^{2}\left(2-\frac{t}{3}\right){/tex}
Therefore {tex}v=\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = 4t – t2 = t(4 – t)
Clearly, v = 0 gives t = 0 and t = 4
Now v = 0 at P as well as at Q and at P, t = 0. So, at Q, t = 4.
Thus, the car will reach the point Q after 4 seconds. Also the distance travelled in 4 seconds is given by
{tex}x]_{t=4}=4^{2}\left(2-\frac{4}{3}\right)=16\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)=\frac{32}{3}{/tex}m

question_text: A water tank has the shape of an inverted right circular cone with its axis vertical and vertex lower most. Its semi vertical angle is tan -1 (0.5) water is poured into it at a constant rate of 5 cubic metre per hour. Find the rate at which the level of the water is rising at the instant when the depth of water in the tank is 4 m.
question_html: A water tank has the shape of an inverted right circular cone with its axis vertical and vertex lower most. Its semi vertical angle is tan-1(0.5) water is poured into it at a constant rate of 5 cubic metre per hour. Find the rate at which the level of the water is rising at the instant when the depth of water in the tank is 4 m.
solution_text: Clearly, {tex}\tan \alpha = \frac{r}{h}{/tex} Given that {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}(0.5) = \alpha {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan \alpha = 0.5{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{r}{h} = \frac{5}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 2r Now, {tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi .{\left( {\frac{h}{2}} \right)^2}.h{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi {\frac{h}{4}^3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{12}}\pi .3{h^2}\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}5 = \frac{1}{{12}}\pi .3{(4)^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{{5}}{{4 {tex}\pi{/tex} }} m/hr{/tex}
solution_html:
Clearly, {tex}\tan \alpha = \frac{r}{h}{/tex} 
Given that {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}(0.5) = \alpha {/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan \alpha = 0.5{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{r}{h} = \frac{5}{{10}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 2r
Now, {tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi .{\left( {\frac{h}{2}} \right)^2}.h{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi {\frac{h}{4}^3}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{12}}\pi .3{h^2}\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}5 = \frac{1}{{12}}\pi .3{(4)^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{{5}}{{4{tex}\pi{/tex}}} m/hr{/tex}

question_text: A man of height 2 metres walks at a uniform speed of 5 km/h away from a lamp post which is 6 metres high. Find the rate at which the length of his shadow increases.
question_html: A man of height 2 metres walks at a uniform speed of 5 km/h away from a lamp post which is 6 metres high. Find the rate at which the length of his shadow increases.
solution_text: AB is lamp post DC is man {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 5km/h,\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = ?{/tex} {tex}\triangle DEC \sim \triangle BEA{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{6} = \frac{y}{{x + y}}{/tex} x + y = 3y x = 2y {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}5 = 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} km\h
solution_html:
AB is lamp post DC is man
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 5km/h,\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = ?{/tex} 
{tex}\triangle DEC \sim \triangle BEA{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{2}{6} = \frac{y}{{x + y}}{/tex} 
x + y = 3y
x = 2y
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}5 = 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} km\h

question_text: Find intervals in which the function given by {tex}f(x) = \frac{3}{{10}}{x^4} - \frac{4}{5}{x^3} - 3{x^2} + \frac{{36}}{5}x + 11{/tex} is increasing or decreasing.
question_html: Find intervals in which the function given by {tex}f(x) = \frac{3}{{10}}{x^4} - \frac{4}{5}{x^3} - 3{x^2} + \frac{{36}}{5}x + 11{/tex} is increasing or decreasing.
solution_text: {tex}f(x) = \frac{3}{{10}}{x^4} - \frac{4}{5}{x^3} - 3{x^2} + \frac{{36}}{5}x + 11{/tex} {tex}f'(x) = \frac{3}{{10}}.4{x^3} - \frac{4}{5}3{x^2} - 6x + \frac{{36}}{5}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{6}{5}{x^3} - \frac{{12}}{5}{x^2} - \frac{{6x}}{1} + \frac{{36}}{5}{/tex} {tex}f'(x) = \frac{{6{x^3} - 12{x^2} - 30x + 36}}{5}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{6}{5}\left[ {{x^3} - 2{x^2} - 5x + 6} \right]{/tex} let f'(x) = 0 {tex}\frac{6}{5}({x^3} - 2{x^2} - 5x + 6) = 0{/tex} {tex}{x^3} - 2{x^2} - 5x + 6 = 0{/tex} Put x = 1 f'(1) = 1 - 2 - 5 + 6 = 0 x-1 is the factor of f'(x) {tex}f'(x) = \frac{6}{5}(x - 1)({x^2} - x - 6){/tex} {tex} = \frac{6}{5}(x - 1)\left[ {{x^2} - 3x + 2x - 6} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{6}{5}(x - 1)\left[ {x(x - 3) + 2(x - 2)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{6}{5}(x - 1)(x - 3)(x + 2){/tex} put f'(x) = 0 x = 1, x = 3, x = -2 int Sign of f’(x) Result {tex}( - \infty , - 2){/tex} -ve Decrease (-2, 1) +ve Increase (1,3) -ve Decrease {tex}(3,\infty ){/tex} +tve increase
solution_html:
{tex}f(x) = \frac{3}{{10}}{x^4} - \frac{4}{5}{x^3} - 3{x^2} + \frac{{36}}{5}x + 11{/tex} 
{tex}f'(x) = \frac{3}{{10}}.4{x^3} - \frac{4}{5}3{x^2} - 6x + \frac{{36}}{5}{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{6}{5}{x^3} - \frac{{12}}{5}{x^2} - \frac{{6x}}{1} + \frac{{36}}{5}{/tex} 
{tex}f'(x) = \frac{{6{x^3} - 12{x^2} - 30x + 36}}{5}{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{6}{5}\left[ {{x^3} - 2{x^2} - 5x + 6} \right]{/tex} 
let f'(x) = 0
{tex}\frac{6}{5}({x^3} - 2{x^2} - 5x + 6) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}{x^3} - 2{x^2} - 5x + 6 = 0{/tex} 
Put x = 1
f'(1) = 1 - 2 - 5 + 6 = 0
x-1 is the factor of f'(x)
{tex}f'(x) = \frac{6}{5}(x - 1)({x^2} - x - 6){/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{6}{5}(x - 1)\left[ {{x^2} - 3x + 2x - 6} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{6}{5}(x - 1)\left[ {x(x - 3) + 2(x - 2)} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{6}{5}(x - 1)(x - 3)(x + 2){/tex} 
put f'(x) = 0
x = 1, x = 3, x = -2

int

Sign of f’(x)

Result

{tex}( - \infty , - 2){/tex}

-ve

Decrease

(-2, 1)

+ve

Increase

(1,3)

-ve

Decrease

{tex}(3,\infty ){/tex}

+tve

question_text: Show that the function f given by f(x) = tan –1 (sin x + cos x), x > 0 is always an increasing function in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex}
question_html: Show that the function f given by f(x) = tan–1 (sin x + cos x), x > 0 is always an increasing function in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}f\left( x \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) = \frac{1}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}.\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + {{\sin }^2}x + {{\cos }^2}x + 2\sin x.\cos x}}\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{\left( {2 + \sin 2x} \right)}}\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \sin 2x = 2\sin x\cos x\,\,and\,\,{{\sin }^2}x + {{\cos }^2}x = 1} \right]{/tex} For {tex}f'\left( x \right) \geqslant 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{\left( {2 + \sin 2x} \right)}}.\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right) \geqslant 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x - \sin x \geqslant 0\,\,\left[ {\because \left( {2 + \sin 2x} \right) \geqslant 0\,\,in\,\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x \geqslant \sin x{/tex} Which is true, if {tex}x \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} Hence, f(x) is an increasing function in {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} .
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f\left( x \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) = \frac{1}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}.\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + {{\sin }^2}x + {{\cos }^2}x + 2\sin x.\cos x}}\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{1}{{\left( {2 + \sin 2x} \right)}}\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\left[ {\because \sin 2x = 2\sin x\cos x\,\,and\,\,{{\sin }^2}x + {{\cos }^2}x = 1} \right]{/tex}

For {tex}f'\left( x \right) \geqslant 0{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{{\left( {2 + \sin 2x} \right)}}.\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right) \geqslant 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \cos x - \sin x \geqslant 0\,\,\left[ {\because \left( {2 + \sin 2x} \right) \geqslant 0\,\,in\,\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \cos x \geqslant \sin x{/tex}

Which is true, if {tex}x \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}

Hence, f(x) is an increasing function in {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}.

question_text: A circular disc of radius 3 cm is being heated. Due to expansion, its radius increases at the rate of 0.05 cm/s. Find the rate at which its area is increasing when radius is 3.2 cm.
question_html: A circular disc of radius 3 cm is being heated. Due to expansion, its radius increases at the rate of 0.05 cm/s. Find the rate at which its area is increasing when radius is 3.2 cm.
solution_text: Given , {tex}\frac{dr}{dt}=0.05cm/sec{/tex} {tex}A = \pi {r^2}{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = 2\pi r.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2\pi \left( {3.2} \right) \times 0.05{/tex} (given r=3.2cm) {tex}= 0.320\pi c{m^2}/s{/tex}
solution_html:
Given , {tex}\frac{dr}{dt}=0.05cm/sec{/tex}

{tex}A = \pi {r^2}{/tex}

Now {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = 2\pi r.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex}

{tex} = 2\pi \left( {3.2} \right) \times 0.05{/tex}(given r=3.2cm)

{tex}= 0.320\pi c{m^2}/s{/tex}
