question_text: An open topped box is to be constructed by removing equal squares from each corner of a 3 metre by 8 metre rectangular sheet of aluminium and folding up the sides. Find the volume of the largest such box
question_html: An open topped box is to be constructed by removing equal squares from each corner of a 3 metre by 8 metre rectangular sheet of aluminium and folding up the sides. Find the volume of the largest such box
solution_text: Let x be the length of side of each square to be removed. Then, the height of the box is x, length is 8 – 2x and breadth is 3 – 2x . If V(x) is the volume of the box, then V(x) = x (3 – 2x) (8 – 2x) = 4x 3 – 22x 2 + 24x {tex}\Rightarrow{\mathrm{V}^{\prime}(x)=12 x^{2}-44 x+24=4(x-3)(3 x-2)} \\ \Rightarrow{\mathrm{V}^{\prime \prime}(x)=24 x-44} {/tex} Now V′(x) = 0 gives x = 3, {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} . But x {tex}\neq{/tex} 3, as we cannot cut a square of length 6m from a sheet of breadth 3m. Thus, we have x = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} . Now V'' {tex}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)=24\left(\frac{2}{3}\right){/tex} -44 = - 28 < 0. Therefore, x = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} is the point of maxima, i.e., if we remove a square of side {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} mtere from each corner of the sheet and make a box from the remaining sheet, then the volume of the box so obtained will be the largest and it is given by {tex}V\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)=4\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{3}-22\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}+24\left(\frac{2}{3}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{200}{27}{/tex} {tex}m^3{/tex}
solution_html:
Let x be the length of side of each square to be removed. Then, the height of the box is x, length is 8 – 2x and breadth is 3 – 2x
.
If V(x) is the volume of the box, then
 V(x) = x (3 – 2x) (8 – 2x)
 = 4x3 – 22x2 + 24x
 {tex}\Rightarrow{\mathrm{V}^{\prime}(x)=12 x^{2}-44 x+24=4(x-3)(3 x-2)} \\ \Rightarrow{\mathrm{V}^{\prime \prime}(x)=24 x-44} {/tex}
Now V′(x) = 0 gives x = 3, {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}. But x {tex}\neq{/tex} 3, as we cannot cut a square of length 6m from a sheet of breadth 3m.
Thus, we have x = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}.
Now V''{tex}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)=24\left(\frac{2}{3}\right){/tex}-44 = - 28 < 0.
Therefore, x = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} is the point of maxima, i.e., if we remove a square of side {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} mtere from each corner of the sheet and make a box from the remaining sheet, then the volume of the box so obtained will be the largest and it is given by
{tex}V\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)=4\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{3}-22\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}+24\left(\frac{2}{3}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{200}{27}{/tex}  {tex}m^3{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Manufacturer can sell x items at a price of rupees {tex} \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right){/tex} each. The cost price of x items is Rs {tex} \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex} . Find the number of items he should sell to earn maximum profit.
question_html: Manufacturer can sell x items at a price of rupees {tex} \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right){/tex} each. The cost price of x items is Rs {tex} \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex}. Find the number of items he should sell to earn maximum profit.
solution_text: Given manufacturer sells x items at a price of Rs. {tex} \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right){/tex} each. {tex} \therefore{/tex} Total revenue obtained {tex} = Rs. \left[ x \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right) \right] = Rs. \left( 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } \right){/tex} Also, cost price of x item {tex} = Rs. \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex} Let P(x) be the profit function. We know that, Profit = Revenue - Cost {tex} \therefore \quad P = \left( 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } \right) - \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad P = \frac { - x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } + \frac { 24 x } { 5 } - 500{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = \frac { - 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 } = 0 \Rightarrow x = 240{/tex} Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d P } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( - \frac { 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 } \right){/tex} {tex} = - \frac { 2 } { 100 } = - \frac { 1 } { 50 } < 0{/tex} Thus, at x = 240, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } < 0 \Rightarrow P{/tex} is maximum. Hence, number of items sold to have maximum profit is 240.
solution_html:
Given manufacturer sells x items at a price of Rs. {tex} \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right){/tex} each.
{tex} \therefore{/tex} Total revenue obtained {tex} = Rs. \left[ x \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right) \right] = Rs. \left( 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } \right){/tex}
Also, cost price of x item {tex} = Rs. \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex}
Let P(x) be the profit function.
We know that,
Profit = Revenue - Cost
{tex} \therefore \quad P = \left( 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } \right) - \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad P = \frac { - x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } + \frac { 24 x } { 5 } - 500{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = \frac { - 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 }{/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 } = 0 \Rightarrow x = 240{/tex}
Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d P } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( - \frac { 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 } \right){/tex}
{tex} = - \frac { 2 } { 100 } = - \frac { 1 } { 50 } < 0{/tex}
Thus, at x = 240, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } < 0 \Rightarrow P{/tex} is maximum.
Hence, number of items sold to have maximum profit is 240.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the rate of change of the area of a circle with respect to its radius r when r = 3 cm
question_html: Find the rate of change of the area of a circle with respect to its radius r when r = 3 cm
solution_text: We know that area of a circle (A) is A = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 Then, Rate of change of the area with respect to its radius is given by, {tex}\frac{d A}{d r}=\frac{d\left(\pi r^{2}\right)}{d r}=2 \pi r{/tex} When r = 3cm Then {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{dr}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} (3) = 6 {tex}\pi{/tex} Therefore, the area of the circle is changing at the rate of 6 {tex}\pi{/tex} cm 2 /s when its radius is 3cm.
solution_html:
We know that area of a circle (A) is A = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2
Then, Rate of change of the area with respect to its radius is given by,
{tex}\frac{d A}{d r}=\frac{d\left(\pi r^{2}\right)}{d r}=2 \pi r{/tex}
When r = 3cm
Then {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{dr}}{/tex} = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}(3) = 6{tex}\pi{/tex}
Therefore, the area of the circle is changing at the rate of 6{tex}\pi{/tex}cm2/s when its radius is 3cm.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the rate of change of the area of a circle with respect to its radius r when r = 4 cm.
question_html: Find the rate of change of the area of a circle with respect to its radius r when r = 4 cm.
solution_text: We know that area A of a circle is, A = {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}r^2{/tex} Then, Rate of change of the area with respect to its radius is given by, {tex}\frac{d }{d r}(A)=\frac{d}{d r}\left(\pi r^{2}\right)=2 \pi r{/tex} When {tex}r = 4~~cm{/tex} Then {tex}\frac{d }{d r}(A)=2 \pi(4)=8 \pi{/tex} Therefore, the area of the circle is changing at the rate of {tex}8\pi~~cm^2/s{/tex} when its radius is {tex} 4~~cm{/tex} .
solution_html:
We know that area A of a circle is,  A = {tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}r^2{/tex}
Then, Rate of change of the area with respect to its radius is given by,
 {tex}\frac{d }{d r}(A)=\frac{d}{d r}\left(\pi r^{2}\right)=2 \pi r{/tex}
When     {tex}r = 4~~cm{/tex}
Then {tex}\frac{d }{d r}(A)=2 \pi(4)=8 \pi{/tex}
Therefore, the area of the circle is changing at the rate of {tex}8\pi~~cm^2/s{/tex} when its radius is {tex} 4~~cm{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The volume of a cube is increasing at the rate of 8 cm 3 /sec. How fast is the surface area increasing when the length of an edge is 12 cm?
question_html: The volume of a cube is increasing at the rate of 8 cm3/sec. How fast is the surface area increasing when the length of an edge is 12 cm?
solution_text: Let x cm be the edge of the cube and y be the surface area of the cube at any time t Given: Rate of increase of volume of cube {tex} = 8c{m^3}/\sec {/tex} {tex} \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {{x^3}} \right){/tex} = 8 {tex} \Rightarrow 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 8{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}y = 6{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of change of surface area of the cube {tex}= \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 6\frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2}{/tex} {tex}= 6\left( {2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 12x\left( {\frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} [from (1)] {tex}= 4\left( {\frac{8}{x}} \right) = \frac{{32}}{x}c{m^2}/\sec{/tex} Putting x = 12 cm (given), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{32}}{{12}} = \frac{8}{3}c{m^2}\sec {/tex} Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive, therefore surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{8}{3}c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}
solution_html:
Let x cm be the edge of the cube and y be the surface area of the cube at any time t

Given: Rate of increase of volume of cube {tex} = 8c{m^3}/\sec {/tex}

{tex} \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {{x^3}} \right){/tex} = 8             

{tex} \Rightarrow 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 8{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i)

{tex}y = 6{x^2}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex}  Rate of change of surface area of the cube {tex}= \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 6\frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2}{/tex} 

{tex}= 6\left( {2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex}

{tex} = 12x\left( {\frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} [from (1)]

{tex}= 4\left( {\frac{8}{x}} \right) = \frac{{32}}{x}c{m^2}/\sec{/tex}

Putting x = 12 cm (given),

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{32}}{{12}} = \frac{8}{3}c{m^2}\sec {/tex}

Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex}  is positive, therefore surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{8}{3}c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The radius of a circle is increasing uniformly at the rate of 3 cm/s. Find the rate at which the area of the circle is increasing when the radius is 10 cm.
question_html: The radius of a circle is increasing uniformly at the rate of 3 cm/s. Find the rate at which the area of the circle is increasing when the radius is 10 cm.
solution_text: Suppose r cm be the radius and A be the area of the circle at any time t. Rate of increase of radius of circle = 3 cm/sec {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive and = 3 cm/sec {tex}\frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = 3{/tex} ….(i) Now, {tex}A = \pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Rate of change of area of circle {tex}= \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \pi 2r\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \pi 2r\left( 3 \right) = 6\pi rc{m^2}/\sec {/tex} (from (i)) Substituting r = 10 cm (given),we get {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = 6\pi \left( {10} \right) = 60\pi c{m^2}/\sec {/tex} Since {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive, thus surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}60\pi c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose r cm be the radius and A be the area of the circle at any time t.
Rate of increase of radius of circle = 3 cm/sec
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive and = 3 cm/sec
{tex}\frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = 3{/tex}….(i)
Now, {tex}A = \pi {r^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Rate of change of area of circle {tex}= \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \pi 2r\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \pi 2r\left( 3 \right) = 6\pi rc{m^2}/\sec {/tex} (from (i))
Substituting r = 10 cm (given),we get
{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = 6\pi \left( {10} \right) = 60\pi c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}
Since {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive, thus surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}60\pi c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: An edge of a variable cube is increasing at the rate of 3 cm per second. How fast is the volume of the cube increasing when the edge is 10 cm long?
question_html: An edge of a variable cube is increasing at the rate of 3 cm per second. How fast is the volume of the cube increasing when the edge is 10 cm long?
solution_text: Let x cm be the edge and y be the volume of the variable cube at any time t. Rate of increase of edge = 3 cm/sec {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive and = 3 cm/sec {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3cm/\sec {/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow y = {x^3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of change of volume of cube {tex}= \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}{x^3}{/tex} {tex}= 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3{x^2}\left( 3 \right){/tex} [from (1)] {tex}= 9{x^2}c{m^3}/\sec{/tex} Putting x = 10cm (given), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 9{\left( {10} \right)^2} = 900c{m^3}/\sec {/tex} Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive, therefore volume of cube is increasing at the rate of 900 cm 3 /sec.
solution_html:
Let x cm be the edge and y be the volume of the variable cube at any  time t. 

Rate of increase of edge = 3 cm/sec

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive and = 3 cm/sec

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3cm/\sec {/tex}...(i)

{tex}\Rightarrow y = {x^3}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of change of volume of cube {tex}= \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}{x^3}{/tex} 

{tex}= 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3{x^2}\left( 3 \right){/tex} [from (1)]

{tex}= 9{x^2}c{m^3}/\sec{/tex}

Putting x = 10cm (given),

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 9{\left( {10} \right)^2} = 900c{m^3}/\sec {/tex}

Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive, therefore volume of cube is increasing at the rate of 900 cm3/sec.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A stone is dropped into a quiet lake and waves move in circles at the speed of 5 cm/s. At the instant when the radius of the circular wave is 8 cm, how fast is the enclosed area increasing?
question_html: A stone is dropped into a quiet lake and waves move in circles at the speed of 5 cm/s. At the instant when the radius of the circular wave is 8 cm, how fast is the enclosed area increasing?
solution_text: Let x cm be the radius and y be the enclosed area of the circular wave at any time t. Rate of increase of radius of circular wave = 5 cm/sec {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive and = 5 cm/sec {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 5cm/\sec{/tex} ...(i) {tex}y = \pi {x^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Rate of change of area {tex} = \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \pi \frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2}{/tex} {tex} = \pi .2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2\pi x\left( 5 \right){/tex} (from (i) {tex}= 10\pi xc{m^2}/\sec{/tex} Putting {tex}x = 8cm{/tex} (given), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 10\pi \left( 8 \right) = 80\pi c{m^2}/\sec{/tex} Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive, therefore area of circular wave is increasing at the rate of {tex}80\pi c{m^2}/\sec {/tex} .
solution_html:
Let x cm be the radius and y be the enclosed area of the circular wave at any time t.
Rate of increase of radius of circular wave = 5 cm/sec
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive and = 5 cm/sec
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 5cm/\sec{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}y = \pi {x^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Rate of change of area {tex} = \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \pi \frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2}{/tex} 

{tex} = \pi .2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2\pi x\left( 5 \right){/tex}(from (i)

{tex}= 10\pi xc{m^2}/\sec{/tex}

Putting {tex}x = 8cm{/tex}(given),

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 10\pi \left( 8 \right) = 80\pi c{m^2}/\sec{/tex}

Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive, therefore area of circular wave is increasing at the rate of {tex}80\pi c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The radius of a circle is increasing at the rate of 0.7 cm/s. What is the rate of increase of its circumference?
question_html: The radius of a circle is increasing at the rate of 0.7 cm/s. What is the rate of increase of its circumference?
solution_text: Let x cm be the radius and y be the circumference of the circle at any time t. Rate of increase of radius of circle = 0.7 cm/sec {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive and = 0.7 cm/sec {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} = 0.7 cm / sec ...(i) {tex} \Rightarrow y = 2\pi x{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of change of circumference of circle {tex} = \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2\pi \frac{d}{{dt}}x = 2\pi \left( {0.7} \right){/tex} (from (i)) = 1.4 {tex}\pi{/tex} cm/sec
solution_html:
Let x cm be the radius and y be the circumference of the circle at any time t. 
Rate of increase of radius of circle = 0.7 cm/sec
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive and = 0.7 cm/sec
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} = 0.7 cm / sec ...(i)
{tex} \Rightarrow y = 2\pi x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of change of circumference of circle {tex} = \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2\pi \frac{d}{{dt}}x = 2\pi \left( {0.7} \right){/tex} (from (i))
= 1.4{tex}\pi{/tex}cm/sec

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The length x of a rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 5 cm/minute and the width y is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/minute. When x = 8cm and y = 6cm, find the rates of change of (a) the perimeter, and (b) the area of the rectangle.
question_html: The length x of a rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 5 cm/minute and the width y is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/minute. When x = 8cm and y = 6cm, find the rates of change of (a) the perimeter, and (b) the area of the rectangle.
solution_text: Given: Rate of decrease of length x of rectangle is 5 cm/minute. {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is negative = –5 cm/minute {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} = – 5 cm/minute ….(i) Also, Rate of increase of width y of rectangle is 4 cm/minute {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive= 4 cm/minute {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 4{/tex} cm/minute ….(ii) let z denotes the perimeter of rectangle at any time t {tex}\therefore x = 2x + 2y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} (from (i) and (ii) ) {tex}= 2\left( { - 5} \right) + 2\left( 4 \right) = - 2{/tex} which is negative. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 2 cm/min. (b) Let z denotes the area of rectangle at any time t {tex}\Rightarrow z = xy{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = x\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} + y\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}= 8\left( 4 \right) + 6\left( { - 5} \right) = 2{/tex} which is positive. (from (i) and (ii)) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the rectangle is increasing at the rate of 2 cm 2 /min
solution_html:
Given: Rate of decrease of length x of rectangle is 5 cm/minute.

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is negative = –5 cm/minute

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} = – 5 cm/minute  ….(i)

Also, Rate of increase of width y of rectangle is 4 cm/minute

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive= 4 cm/minute

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 4{/tex} cm/minute   ….(ii)

let z denotes the perimeter of rectangle at any time t

{tex}\therefore x = 2x + 2y{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} (from (i) and (ii) )   

{tex}= 2\left( { - 5} \right) + 2\left( 4 \right) = - 2{/tex} which is negative.             

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 2 cm/min.

(b) Let z denotes the area of rectangle at any time t

{tex}\Rightarrow z = xy{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = x\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} + y\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}

{tex}= 8\left( 4 \right) + 6\left( { - 5} \right) = 2{/tex} which is positive. (from (i) and (ii))

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the rectangle is increasing at the rate of 2 cm2/min

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A balloon, which always remains spherical on inflation, is being inflated by pumping in 900 cubic centimeters of gas per second. Find the rate at which the radius of the balloon increases when the radius is 15 cm.
question_html: A balloon, which always remains spherical on inflation, is being inflated by pumping in 900 cubic centimeters of gas per second. Find the rate at which the radius of the balloon increases when the radius is 15 cm.
solution_text: Let V be the volume of sphere {tex}V = \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 900c{m^3}/s{/tex} (given) Now , {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{4}{3}.\pi .3{r^2}.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\implies900 = 4\pi {r^2}.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\implies900 = 4\pi \times {\left( {15} \right)^2}.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} [r = 15 cm] {tex}\implies\frac{{900}}{{4\pi \times 225}} = \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{\pi }cm/s = \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let V be the volume of sphere
{tex}V = \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 900c{m^3}/s{/tex} (given)
Now , {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{4}{3}.\pi .3{r^2}.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\implies900 = 4\pi {r^2}.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\implies900 = 4\pi \times {\left( {15} \right)^2}.\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex} [r = 15 cm]
{tex}\implies\frac{{900}}{{4\pi \times 225}} = \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{\pi }cm/s = \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A balloon, which always remains spherical has a variable radius. Find the rate at which its volume is increasing with the radius when the later is 10 cm.
question_html: A balloon, which always remains spherical has a variable radius. Find the rate at which its volume is increasing with the radius when the later is 10 cm.
solution_text: Since, {tex}V = \frac{4}{3}\pi {x^3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{4}{3}\pi .3{r^2} = 4\pi {r^2}{/tex} At x = 10 cm {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = 4\pi {\left( {10} \right)^2} = 400\pi {/tex} Therefore, the volume is increasing at the rate of 400 {tex}\pi{/tex} cm 3 /sec.
solution_html:
Since, {tex}V = \frac{4}{3}\pi {x^3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}} \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac{4}{3}\pi .3{r^2} = 4\pi {r^2}{/tex}
At x = 10 cm
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = 4\pi {\left( {10} \right)^2} = 400\pi {/tex}
Therefore, the volume is increasing at the rate of 400{tex}\pi{/tex} cm3/sec.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A ladder 5 m long is leaning against a wall. The bottom of the ladder is pulled along the ground, away from the wall, at the rate of 2cm/s. How fast is its height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 4 m away from the wall?
question_html: A ladder 5 m long is leaning against a wall. The bottom of the ladder is pulled along the ground, away from the wall, at the rate of 2cm/s. How fast is its height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 4 m away from the wall?
solution_text: {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2cm/s{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 0.02m/\sec {/tex} {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = {5^2}{/tex} {tex}2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} When x = 4 {tex}y = \sqrt {{5^2} - {4^2}} {/tex} = 3 {tex}2 \times 4\left( {0.02} \right) + 2 \times 3\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{2 \times 4 \times 0.02}}{{2 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}= - \frac{{0.08}}{3} \times {100}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{8}{3}cm/\sec {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2cm/s{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 0.02m/\sec {/tex} 
{tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = {5^2}{/tex} 
{tex}2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} 
When x = 4
{tex}y = \sqrt {{5^2} - {4^2}} {/tex} = 3
{tex}2 \times 4\left( {0.02} \right) + 2 \times 3\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{2 \times 4 \times 0.02}}{{2 \times 3}}{/tex} 
{tex}= - \frac{{0.08}}{3} \times {100}{/tex} 
{tex} = - \frac{8}{3}cm/\sec {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A particle moves along the curve 6y = x 3 + 2. Find the points on the curve at which the y-coordinate is changing 8 times as fast as the x – coordinate.
question_html: A particle moves along the curve 6y = x3 + 2. Find the points on the curve at which the y-coordinate is changing 8 times as fast as the x – coordinate.
solution_text: Given curve is 6y = x 3 + 2 ...(i) so, {tex}6\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow6 \times 8\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}\,\,\left[ {\because \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 8\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow16 = {x^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = \pm 4{/tex} Put the value of x in equation (1) When x = 4 6y = ( 4 ) 3 + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6y = 64 + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6y = 66 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}y = \frac{{66}}{6} = 11{/tex} So, point is (4, 11) Now, When {tex}{/tex} x = - 4 6y = ( - 4} 3 + 2 = - 64 + 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}y = \frac{{ - 62}}{6}= \frac{-31}{3}{/tex} So the point is {tex}\left( { - 4,\frac{{ - 31}}{3}} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Given curve is 6y = x3 + 2 ...(i)
so, {tex}6\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow6 \times 8\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}\,\,\left[ {\because \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 8\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow16 = {x^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x = \pm 4{/tex}
Put the value of x in equation (1)
When x = 4
6y = ( 4 )3 + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6y = 64 + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6y = 66
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}y = \frac{{66}}{6} = 11{/tex}
So, point is (4, 11)
Now, When {tex}{/tex}x = - 4
6y = ( - 4}3 + 2
= - 64 + 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}y = \frac{{ - 62}}{6}= \frac{-31}{3}{/tex}
So the point is {tex}\left( { - 4,\frac{{ - 31}}{3}} \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The radius of an air bubble is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} cm/s. At what rate is the volume of the bubble increasing when the radius is 1 cm?
question_html: The radius of an air bubble is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} cm/s. At what rate is the volume of the bubble increasing when the radius is 1 cm?
solution_text: Let x cm be the radius of the air bubble at time t. According to question, {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive {tex}= \frac{1}{2}{/tex} cm/sec ……….(i) Volume of air bubble {tex}\left( z \right) = \frac{{4\pi }}{3}{x^3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = \frac{{4\pi }}{3}\frac{d}{{dt}}{x^3}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{4\pi }}{3}.3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}= 4\pi {x^2}\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = 2\pi {x^2}{/tex} {tex}= 2\pi {\left( 1 \right)^2} = 2\pi{/tex} Therefore, required rate of increase of volume of air bubble is {tex}2\pi c{m^3}/\sec{/tex}
solution_html:
Let x cm be the radius of the air bubble at time t. 

According to question, {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive {tex}= \frac{1}{2}{/tex} cm/sec  ……….(i)

Volume of air bubble {tex}\left( z \right) = \frac{{4\pi }}{3}{x^3}{/tex} 

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = \frac{{4\pi }}{3}\frac{d}{{dt}}{x^3}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{4\pi }}{3}.3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}

{tex}= 4\pi {x^2}\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = 2\pi {x^2}{/tex}

{tex}= 2\pi {\left( 1 \right)^2} = 2\pi{/tex}

Therefore, required rate of increase of volume of air bubble is {tex}2\pi c{m^3}/\sec{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A balloon which always remains spherical, has a variable diameter {tex}\frac{3}{2}\left( {2x + 1} \right){/tex} Find the rate of change of its volume with respect to x.
question_html: A balloon which always remains spherical, has a variable diameter {tex}\frac{3}{2}\left( {2x + 1} \right){/tex} Find the rate of change of its volume with respect to x.
solution_text: Given: Diameter of the balloon {tex}= \frac{3}{2}\left( {2x + 1} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of the balloon {tex}= \frac{3}{4}\left( {2x + 1} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of the balloon {tex}= \frac{4}{3}\pi {\left( {\frac{3}{4}\left( {2x + 1} \right)} \right)^3}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{9\pi }}{{16}}{\left( {2x + 1} \right)^3}{/tex} cube units {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of change of volume w.r.t. {tex}x = \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{9\pi }}{{16}}.3{\left( {2x + 1} \right)^2}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {2x + 1} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{27\pi }}{16}{\left( {2x + 1} \right)^2}.2{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{27\pi }}{8}{\left( {2x + 1} \right)^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Diameter of the balloon {tex}= \frac{3}{2}\left( {2x + 1} \right){/tex} 

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of the balloon {tex}= \frac{3}{4}\left( {2x + 1} \right){/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of the balloon {tex}= \frac{4}{3}\pi {\left( {\frac{3}{4}\left( {2x + 1} \right)} \right)^3}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{9\pi }}{{16}}{\left( {2x + 1} \right)^3}{/tex} cube units

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of change of volume w.r.t. {tex}x = \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{9\pi }}{{16}}.3{\left( {2x + 1} \right)^2}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {2x + 1} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{27\pi }}{16}{\left( {2x + 1} \right)^2}.2{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{27\pi }}{8}{\left( {2x + 1} \right)^2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Sand is pouring from a pipe at the rate of 12cm 3 /s. the falling sand forms a cone on the ground in such a way that the height of the cone is always one-sixth of the radius of the base. How fast is the height of the sand cone increasing when the height is 4 cm?
question_html: Sand is pouring from a pipe at the rate of 12cm3/s. the falling sand forms a cone on the ground in such a way that the height of the cone is always one-sixth of the radius of the base. How fast is the height of the sand cone increasing when the height is 4 cm?
solution_text: Let radius of the cone = r Height of the cone = h Volume of the cone {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12c{m^3}/s{/tex} (Given) {tex}h = \frac{1}{6}r{/tex} (Given) {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .{\left( {6h} \right)^2}.h\,\,\left[ {\because h = \frac{1}{6}r} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .36.{h^3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = 12\pi {h^3}{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12\pi .3{h^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}12 = 12\pi .3{\left( 4 \right)^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} [h = 4cm] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{12}}{{12\pi .3.16}} = \frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{48\pi }}cm/s{/tex}
solution_html:
Let radius of the cone = r
Height of the cone = h
Volume of the cone {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12c{m^3}/s{/tex} (Given)
{tex}h = \frac{1}{6}r{/tex} (Given)
{tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
{tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .{\left( {6h} \right)^2}.h\,\,\left[ {\because h = \frac{1}{6}r} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .36.{h^3}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow v = 12\pi {h^3}{/tex} 
Now {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12\pi .3{h^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}12 = 12\pi .3{\left( 4 \right)^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} [h = 4cm]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{12}}{{12\pi .3.16}} = \frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{48\pi }}cm/s{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The total cost C(x) in rupees associated with the production of x units of an item given by c(x) = 0.007x 3 - 0.003x 2 + 15x + 4000. Find the marginal cost when 17 units are produced.
question_html: The total cost C(x) in rupees associated with the production of x units of an item given by c(x) = 0.007x3 - 0.003x2 + 15x + 4000. Find the marginal cost when 17 units are produced.
solution_text: Marginal cost {tex}= \frac{{dC}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {0.007{x^3} - 0.003{x^2} + 15x + 4000} \right){/tex} = 0.021x 2 - 0.006x + 15 Now, when x = 17, MC = 0.021(17) 2 - 0.006 {tex}\times{/tex} 17 + 15 = 0.069 - 0.102 + 15 = 20.967 Therefore, required Marginal cost is Rs 20.97
solution_html:
Marginal cost {tex}= \frac{{dC}}{{dx}}{/tex} 

{tex}= \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {0.007{x^3} - 0.003{x^2} + 15x + 4000} \right){/tex}

= 0.021x2 - 0.006x + 15

Now, when x = 17, MC

= 0.021(17)2 - 0.006 {tex}\times{/tex}17 + 15

= 0.069 - 0.102 + 15 = 20.967

Therefore, required Marginal cost is Rs 20.97

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The total revenue in rupees received from the sale of x units of a product is given by R(x) = 13x 2 + 26x + 15. Find the marginal revenue when x = 7.
question_html: The total revenue in rupees received from the sale of x units of a product is given by R(x) = 13x2 + 26x + 15. Find the marginal revenue when x = 7.
solution_text: Marginal Revenue (MR) {tex}= \frac{{dR}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{d}{{dx}}{/tex} (13x 2 + 26x + 15) = 26x + 26 Now, when x = 7, MR = 26 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 + 26 = 208 Thus, the required marginal revenue is Rs 208.
solution_html:
Marginal Revenue (MR) {tex}= \frac{{dR}}{{dx}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{d}{{dx}}{/tex} (13x2 + 26x + 15)
= 26x + 26
Now, when x = 7, MR = 26 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 + 26 = 208
Thus, the required marginal revenue is Rs 208.

question_text: The rate of change of the area of a circle with respect to its radius r at r = 6 cm is
question_html: The rate of change of the area of a circle with respect to its radius r at r = 6 cm is

options.explanation: We know that area of a circle (A) is A = {tex}\pi r^2{/tex} Then, Rate of change of the area with respect to its radius is given by {tex}\frac{d}{dr} A = \frac{d}{dr}\pi {r}^{2}=2 \pi \mathrm{r}{/tex} When r = 6 cm Then {tex}\frac{d }{d r}A=2 \pi(6)=12 \pi{/tex} Therefore, the area of the circle is changing at the rate of 12 {tex}\pi{/tex} cm 2 /s when its radius is 6 cm

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The total revenue in Rupees received from the sale of x units of a product is given by R(x) = 3x 2 + 36x + 5. The marginal revenue, when x = 15 is
question_html:
The total revenue in Rupees received from the sale of x units of a product is given by
R(x) = 3x2 + 36x + 5. The marginal revenue, when x = 15 is

options.explanation: Marginal revenue (MR) is the rate of change of total revenue with respect to the number of units sold. So, MR = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} = 6x + 36 = 6x + 36 {tex}\therefore{/tex} when x = 15, then MR = 6(15) + 36 = 126 Therefore, the marginal revenue when x = 15 is 126.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function given by f(x) = 3x + 17 is increasing on R.
question_html: Show that the function given by f(x) = 3x + 17 is increasing on R.
solution_text: Let {tex}x_1{/tex} and {tex}x_2{/tex} be any two numbers in R such that {tex}x_1{/tex} < {tex}x_2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3x_1 < 3x_2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3x_1 + 17 < 3x_2 + 17{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f(x_1) < f(x_2){/tex} Therefore, f is strictly increasing on R.
solution_html:
Let {tex}x_1{/tex} and {tex}x_2{/tex} be any two numbers in R such that
      {tex}x_1{/tex} < {tex}x_2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3x_1 < 3x_2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3x_1 + 17 < 3x_2 + 17{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f(x_1) < f(x_2){/tex}
Therefore, f is strictly increasing on R.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function given by {tex}f\left( x \right) = {e^{2x}}{/tex} is increasing on R.
question_html: Show that the function given by {tex}f\left( x \right) = {e^{2x}}{/tex} is increasing on R.
solution_text: Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = {e^{2x}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) = {e^{2x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}(2x) = {e^{2x}}\left( 2 \right) = 2{e^{2x}} > 0{/tex} i.e., positive for all {tex}x \in R{/tex} Therefore, f(x) is strictly increasing on R.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = {e^{2x}}{/tex} 

{tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) = {e^{2x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}(2x) = {e^{2x}}\left( 2 \right) = 2{e^{2x}} > 0{/tex} i.e., positive for all {tex}x \in R{/tex}

Therefore, f(x) is strictly increasing on R.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function given by f (x) = sin x is increasing in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
question_html: Show that the function given by f (x) = sin x is increasing in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
solution_text: The function is f (x) = sin x Then, f’(x) = cos x Since for each x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} , cos x > 0, we have f’(x) > 0 Therefore, f’ is strictly increasing in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} .
solution_html:
The function is f (x) = sin x
Then, f’(x) = cos x
Since for each x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}, cos x > 0, we have f’(x) > 0
Therefore, f’ is strictly increasing in{tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}.

question_text: Show that the function given by f (x) = sin x is decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex}
question_html: Show that the function given by f (x) = sin x is decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex}
solution_text: The function is f (x) = sin x Then, {tex}f^\prime(x)=cosx{/tex} Since for each x {tex}\epsilon\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex} , cos x < 0, we have {tex}f^\prime(x)<0{/tex} Therefore, {tex}f{/tex} is strictly decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex} .
solution_html:
The function is f (x) = sin x
Then, {tex}f^\prime(x)=cosx{/tex} 
Since for each x {tex}\epsilon\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex}, cos x < 0, we have {tex}f^\prime(x)<0{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}f{/tex} is strictly decreasing in{tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex}.

question_text: Show that the function given by f (x) = sin x is neither increasing nor decreasing in (0, {tex}\pi{/tex} )
question_html: Show that the function given by f (x) = sin x is neither increasing nor decreasing in (0, {tex}\pi{/tex})
solution_text: The function is f (x) = sin x Then, {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) = cos x Since for each x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} , cos x > 0, we have {tex}f^\prime(x){/tex} >0 Therefore, {tex}f{/tex} is strictly increasing in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} ……(1) Now, The function is f (x) = sin x Then, {tex}f^\prime(x)=cosx{/tex} Since, for each {tex}x\in\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex} , cos x < 0, we have {tex}f^\prime(x){/tex} < 0 Therefore, {tex}f{/tex} is strictly decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex} ……(2) From (1) and (2), It is clear that f is neither increasing nor decreasing in (0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ).
solution_html:
The function is f (x) = sin x
Then, {tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) = cos x
Since for each x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}, cos x > 0, we have {tex}f^\prime(x){/tex} >0
Therefore, {tex}f{/tex} is strictly increasing in{tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}……(1)
Now, The function is f (x) = sin x
Then, {tex}f^\prime(x)=cosx{/tex}
Since, for each  {tex}x\in\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex}, cos x < 0, we have {tex}f^\prime(x){/tex} < 0
Therefore, {tex}f{/tex} is strictly decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex}……(2)
From (1) and (2),
It is clear that f is neither increasing nor decreasing in (0, {tex}\pi{/tex}).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 2x 2 – 3x is increasing.
question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 2x2 – 3x is increasing.
solution_text: It is given that function, f(x) = 2x 2 - 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 4x - 3 If f'(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\mathrm{x}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} So, the point x = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} , divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{3}{4}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{3}{4}, \infty\right){/tex} So, in interval {tex}\left(\frac{3}{4}, \infty\right){/tex} , f'(x) = 4x -3 > 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing in interval {tex}\left(\frac{3}{4}, \infty\right){/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that function, f(x) = 2x2 - 3x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 4x - 3
If f'(x) = 0, then we get,
{tex}\mathrm{x}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} 
So, the point x = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}, divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{3}{4}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{3}{4}, \infty\right){/tex}

So, in interval {tex}\left(\frac{3}{4}, \infty\right){/tex}, f'(x) = 4x -3 > 0
Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing in interval {tex}\left(\frac{3}{4}, \infty\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 2x 2 – 3x is decreasing.
question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 2x2 – 3x is decreasing.
solution_text: It is given that function f(x) = 2x 2 - 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 4x - 3 If f'(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}x=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} So, the point x = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} , divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{3}{4}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{3}{4}, \infty\right){/tex} Now, in interval {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{3}{4}\right){/tex} , f'(x) = 4x - 3 < 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{3}{4}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that function f(x) = 2x2 - 3x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 4x - 3
If f'(x) = 0, then we get,
      {tex}x=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} 
So, the point x = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}, divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{3}{4}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{3}{4}, \infty\right){/tex} 

Now, in interval {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{3}{4}\right){/tex},  f'(x) = 4x - 3 < 0
Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{3}{4}\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 2x 3 – 3x 2 – 36x + 7 is increasing.
question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 2x3 – 3x2 – 36x + 7 is increasing.
solution_text: It is given that function f(x) = 2x 3 - 3x 2 - 36x + 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6x 2 - 6x - 36 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x 2 - x - 6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2) (x - 3) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) If f'(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2, 3 So, the points x = -2 and x = 3 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty, -2),(-2,3) {/tex} and {tex}(3, \infty){/tex} So, in interval {tex}(-\infty, -2) and (3, \infty){/tex} {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) > 0 But, in {tex}(-2, 3){/tex} , {tex}f^\prime(x)<0{/tex} Therefore, the given function 'f' is strictly increasing in interval {tex}(-\infty, 2)and(3, \infty){/tex} while as strictly decreasing in {tex}(-2, 3){/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that function f(x) = 2x3 - 3x2 - 36x + 7
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6x2 - 6x - 36
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x2 - x - 6)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2) (x - 3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3)
If f'(x) = 0, then we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2, 3
So, the points x = -2 and x = 3 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty, -2),(-2,3) {/tex} and {tex}(3, \infty){/tex} 





So, in interval {tex}(-\infty, -2) and (3, \infty){/tex}
{tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) > 0
But, in {tex}(-2, 3){/tex}, {tex}f^\prime(x)<0{/tex}
Therefore, the given function 'f' is strictly increasing in interval {tex}(-\infty, 2)and(3, \infty){/tex} while as strictly decreasing in {tex}(-2, 3){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 2x 3 – 3x 2 – 36x + 7 is decreasing.
question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 2x3 – 3x2 – 36x + 7 is decreasing.
solution_text: It is given that function f(x) = 2x 3 - 3x 2 - 36x + 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6x 2 - 6x + 36 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x 2 - x + 6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) If f'(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2, 3 So, the point x = -2 and x = 3 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty, 2),(-2,3){/tex} and {tex}(3, \infty){/tex} So, in interval (-2, 3) f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) < 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly decreasing in interval (-2, 3).
solution_html:
It is given that function f(x) = 2x3 - 3x2 - 36x + 7
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6x2 - 6x + 36
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x2 - x + 6)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3)
If f'(x) = 0, then we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2, 3
So, the point x = -2 and x = 3 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty, 2),(-2,3){/tex} and {tex}(3, \infty){/tex} 

So, in interval (-2, 3)
f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) < 0
Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly decreasing in interval (-2, 3).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the interval in which the function {tex}f(x) = x^2 + 2x – 5{/tex} is strictly increasing or decreasing.
question_html:
Find the interval in which the function
{tex}f(x) =  x^2 + 2x – 5{/tex}
 is strictly increasing or decreasing.
solution_text: The given function is, f(x) = x 2 + 2x - 5 Derivative, f'(x) = 2x + 2 If f'(x) = 0, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 So, the point x = -1 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals {tex}(-\infty,-1){/tex} and {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex} So, in interval {tex}(-\infty,-1){/tex} f'(x) = 2x + 2 < 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}(-\infty,-1){/tex} Now, in interval {tex}(1, \infty){/tex} , we have f'(x) = 2x + 2 > 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing in interval {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex} Thus, f is strictly increasing for x > -1
solution_html:
The given function is,  f(x) = x2 + 2x - 5
Derivative, f'(x) = 2x + 2
If f'(x) = 0,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1
So, the point x = -1 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals {tex}(-\infty,-1){/tex} and {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex} 
So, in interval {tex}(-\infty,-1){/tex} 
f'(x) = 2x + 2 < 0
Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}(-\infty,-1){/tex}
Now, in interval {tex}(1, \infty){/tex}, we have
f'(x) = 2x + 2 > 0
Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing in interval {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex}
Thus, f is strictly increasing for x > -1

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the interval of the function that is strictly increasing or decreasing: 10 - 6x - 2x 2
question_html: Find the interval of the function that is strictly increasing or decreasing: 10 - 6x - 2x2
solution_text: It is given that function f(x) = 10 - 6x - 2x 2 f'(x) = -6 - 4x If f'(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{-3}{2}{/tex} So, the point {tex}x=\frac{-3}{2}{/tex} divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-3}{2}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{-3}{2}, \infty\right){/tex} So, in interval {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-3}{2}\right){/tex} x < {tex}\frac{-3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -4x > 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -6 - 4x > 0 i.e, f'(x) > 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing in interval {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-3}{2}\right){/tex} and in interval {tex}\left(\frac{-3}{2}, \infty\right){/tex} f'(x) = -6 - 4x < 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(\frac{-3}{2}, \infty\right){/tex} Thus, function is strictly increasing in {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-3}{2}\right){/tex} and strictly decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{-3}{2}, \infty\right){/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that function f(x) = 10 - 6x - 2x2
f'(x) = -6 - 4x
If f'(x) = 0, then we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{-3}{2}{/tex} 
So, the point {tex}x=\frac{-3}{2}{/tex} divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-3}{2}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{-3}{2}, \infty\right){/tex} 
So, in interval {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-3}{2}\right){/tex} 
x < {tex}\frac{-3}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -4x > 6 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}-6 - 4x > 0
i.e,    f'(x) > 0
Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing in interval {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-3}{2}\right){/tex} and in interval {tex}\left(\frac{-3}{2}, \infty\right){/tex} 
f'(x) = -6 - 4x < 0
Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(\frac{-3}{2}, \infty\right){/tex}
Thus, function is strictly increasing in {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-3}{2}\right){/tex}and strictly decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{-3}{2}, \infty\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the interval in function -2x 3 - 9x 2 - 12x + 1 is increasing or decreasing:
question_html: Find the interval in function -2x3 - 9x2 - 12x + 1 is increasing or decreasing:
solution_text: It is given that function f(x) = -2x 3 - 9x 2 - 12x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6x 2 - 18x - 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x 2 + 3x + 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) If f '(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 and -2 So, the points x = -1 and x = -2 divide the real line into three disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-2,-1){/tex} and {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex} So, in intervals {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-1, \infty){/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) < 0 Therefore, the given function 'f ' is strictly decreasing for x < -2 and x > -1 Further, in interval (-2, -1) f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) > 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing for -2 < x < -1
solution_html:
It is given that function f(x) = -2x3 - 9x2 - 12x + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6x2 - 18x - 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x2 + 3x + 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2)
If f '(x) = 0, then we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 and -2
So, the points x = -1 and x = -2 divide the real line into three disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-2,-1){/tex} and {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex} 
So, in intervals {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-1, \infty){/tex}
f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) < 0
Therefore, the given function 'f ' is strictly decreasing for x < -2 and x > -1
Further, in interval (-2, -1)
f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) > 0
Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing for -2 < x < -1

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the interval in function 6 - 9x - x 2 is increasing or decreasing.
question_html: Find the interval in function 6 - 9x - x2 is increasing or decreasing.
solution_text: It is given that function f(x) = 6 - 9x - x 2 f'(x) = -9 - 2x If f'(x) = 0, {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{-9}{2}{/tex} So, the point x = {tex}\frac{-9}{2}{/tex} divides the real line two disjoint intervals, {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-9}{2}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{-9}{2}, \infty\right){/tex} So, in interval {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-9}{2}\right){/tex} f'(x) = -9 - 2x > 0 Therefore, the given function 'f' is strictly increasing for x < {tex}\frac{-9}{2}{/tex} . And in interval {tex}\left(\frac{-9}{2}, \infty\right){/tex} f'(x) = -9 - 2x < 0 Therefore, the given function 'f' is strictly decreasing for {tex}x>\frac{-9}{2}{/tex} Thus, f is strictly decreasing for {tex}x>\frac{-9}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that function f(x) = 6 - 9x - x2
f'(x) = -9 - 2x
If f'(x) = 0, 
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{-9}{2}{/tex} 
So, the point x = {tex}\frac{-9}{2}{/tex} divides the real line two disjoint intervals, {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-9}{2}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{-9}{2}, \infty\right){/tex} 
So, in interval {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{-9}{2}\right){/tex} 
f'(x) = -9 - 2x > 0
Therefore, the given function 'f' is strictly increasing for x < {tex}\frac{-9}{2}{/tex}.
And in interval {tex}\left(\frac{-9}{2}, \infty\right){/tex} 
f'(x) = -9 - 2x < 0
Therefore, the given function 'f' is strictly decreasing for {tex}x>\frac{-9}{2}{/tex} 
Thus, f is strictly decreasing for {tex}x>\frac{-9}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the interval of the function that is strictly increasing or decreasing: (x + 1) 3 (x - 3) 3
question_html: Find the interval of the function that is strictly increasing or decreasing: (x + 1)3 (x - 3)3
solution_text: {tex}f(x) = {(x + 1)^3}{(x - 3)^3}{/tex} {tex}f'(x) = {(x + 1)^3}.3{(x - 3)^2} + {(x - 3)^3}.3{(x + 1)^2}{/tex} {tex} = 3{(x + 1)^2}{(x - 3)^2}[x + 1 + x - 3]{/tex} {tex} = 3{(x + 1)^2}{(x - 3)^2}[2x - 2]{/tex} {tex} = 6{(x + 1)^2}{(x - 3)^2}(x - 1){/tex} Put f'(x) = 0 x = -1, 3, 1 int Sign of f ’(x) Result {tex}( - \infty , - 1){/tex} -ve Decrease (-1, 1) -ve Decrease (1, 3) +ve Increase {tex}(3,\infty ){/tex} +ve Increase
solution_html:
{tex}f(x) = {(x + 1)^3}{(x - 3)^3}{/tex}
{tex}f'(x) = {(x + 1)^3}.3{(x - 3)^2} + {(x - 3)^3}.3{(x + 1)^2}{/tex}
{tex} = 3{(x + 1)^2}{(x - 3)^2}[x + 1 + x - 3]{/tex}
{tex} = 3{(x + 1)^2}{(x - 3)^2}[2x - 2]{/tex}
{tex} = 6{(x + 1)^2}{(x - 3)^2}(x - 1){/tex}
Put f'(x) = 0
x = -1, 3, 1

int Sign of f’(x) Result
{tex}( - \infty , - 1){/tex} -ve Decrease
(-1, 1) -ve Decrease
(1, 3) +ve Increase
{tex}(3,\infty ){/tex} +ve Increase

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that {tex}y = \log \left( {1 + x} \right) - \frac{{2x}}{{2 + x}},x > - 1{/tex} is an increasing function of x throughout its domain.
question_html: Show that {tex}y = \log \left( {1 + x} \right) - \frac{{2x}}{{2 + x}},x > - 1{/tex} is an increasing function of x throughout its domain.
solution_text: Given: {tex}y = \log \left( {1 + x} \right) - \frac{{2x}}{{2+x}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{1 + x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 + x} \right) - \left[ {\frac{{\left( {2 + x} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {2x} \right) - 2x\frac{d}{{dx}} \left( {2 + x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + x}} - \frac{{\left( {4 + 2x - 2x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + x}} - \frac{4}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\left( {2 + {x}} \right)^2 - 4\left( {1 + x} \right)}}{{\left( {1 + x} \right){{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{{x^2}}}{{\left( {1 + x} \right)\left( {2 + {x}} \right)^2}}{/tex} ...(i) Domain of the given function is given to be {tex}x > - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x + 1 > 0{/tex} Also {tex}{\left( {2 + x} \right)^2} > 0{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} \geqslant 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for all x in domain {tex}x > - 1{/tex} and f is an increasing function.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}y = \log \left( {1 + x} \right) - \frac{{2x}}{{2+x}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{1 + x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 + x} \right) - \left[ {\frac{{\left( {2 + x} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {2x} \right) - 2x\frac{d}{{dx}} \left( {2 + x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + x}} - \frac{{\left( {4 + 2x - 2x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + x}} - \frac{4}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\left( {2 + {x}} \right)^2 - 4\left( {1 + x} \right)}}{{\left( {1 + x} \right){{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{{{x^2}}}{{\left( {1 + x} \right)\left( {2 + {x}} \right)^2}}{/tex}       ...(i)
Domain of the given function is given to be {tex}x > - 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x + 1 > 0{/tex}
Also {tex}{\left( {2 + x} \right)^2} > 0{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} \geqslant 0{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for all x in domain {tex}x > - 1{/tex} and f is an increasing function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the value(s) of x for which y = [x(x - 2)] 2 is an increasing function.
question_html: Find the value(s) of x for which y = [x(x - 2)]2 is an increasing function.
solution_text: Given function is y = [x(x - 2)] 2 = [x 2 - 2x] 2 . Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 2 \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) {/tex} = 2(x 2 - 2x) (2x - 2) = 4x(x - 2) (x - 1) Therefore,on putting {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = 0, we get, 4x(x - 2)(x - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, 1 and 2. Now, we find interval in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) = 12x(x + 1)(x - 2) Sign of f'(x) (- {tex}\infty{/tex} ,0) (-)(-)(-) -ve (0, 1) (+)(-)(-) +ve (1, 2) (+)(-)(+) -ve (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) (+)(+)(+) +ve Therefore, y is strictly increasing in ( 0, 1 ) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). Also, y is a polynomial function, so it continuous at x = 0, 1 and 2. Hence, y is increasing in [ 0, 1] {tex} \cup {/tex} [2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ).
solution_html:
Given function is y = [x(x - 2)]2= [x2 - 2x]2.
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 2 \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) {/tex}
= 2(x2 - 2x) (2x - 2)
= 4x(x - 2) (x - 1)
Therefore,on putting {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}= 0, we get,
4x(x - 2)(x - 1) = 0{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}x = 0, 1 and 2.
Now, we find interval in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.

Interval f'(x) = 12x(x + 1)(x - 2) Sign of f'(x)
(-{tex}\infty{/tex},0) (-)(-)(-) -ve
(0, 1) (+)(-)(-) +ve
(1, 2) (+)(-)(+) -ve
(2,{tex}\infty{/tex}) (+)(+)(+) +ve

Therefore, y is strictly increasing in ( 0, 1 ) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex}).
Also, y is a polynomial function, so it continuous at x = 0, 1 and 2.
 Hence, y is increasing in [ 0, 1] {tex} \cup {/tex} [2, {tex}\infty{/tex}).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} is an increasing function of {tex}\theta{/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}
question_html: Prove that {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} is an increasing function of {tex}\theta{/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right).4\cos \theta - 4\sin \theta \left( { - \sin \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4{{\cos }^2}\theta + 4{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4\left( {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } \right) - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4 - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {8\cos \theta + 4} \right) - \left( {4 + 4\cos \theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{4\cos \theta - {{\cos }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\cos \theta \left( {4 - \cos \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} Since {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} and we have {tex}0 \leqslant \cos \theta \leqslant 1{/tex} , therefore {tex}4 - \cos \theta > 0{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} Hence, y is an increasing function of {tex}\theta {/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} 

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right).4\cos \theta - 4\sin \theta \left( { - \sin \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4{{\cos }^2}\theta + 4{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4\left( {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } \right) - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4 - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {8\cos \theta + 4} \right) - \left( {4 + 4\cos \theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{4\cos \theta - {{\cos }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\cos \theta \left( {4 - \cos \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

Since {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} and we have {tex}0 \leqslant \cos \theta \leqslant 1{/tex}, therefore {tex}4 - \cos \theta > 0{/tex}. 

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

Hence, y is an increasing function of {tex}\theta {/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the logarithmic function is increasing on {tex}(0, \infty){/tex} .
question_html: Prove that the logarithmic function is increasing on {tex}(0, \infty){/tex}.
solution_text: The given function is f(x) = log x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} ,. Clearly, for x > 0, f'(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} > 0 Therefore, f(x) = log x is strictly increasing in interval {tex}(0, \infty){/tex}
solution_html:
The given function is f(x) = log x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex},.
Clearly, for x > 0,
     f'(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} > 0
Therefore, f(x) = log x is strictly increasing in interval {tex}(0, \infty){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function f given by f(x) = x 2 - x + 1 is neither strictly increasing nor decreasing on (-1, 1).
question_html: Prove that the function f given by f(x) = x2 - x + 1 is neither strictly increasing nor decreasing on (-1, 1).
solution_text: It is given that function f(x) = x 2 - x + 1 {tex}f'(x) = 2x - 1{/tex} If {tex}f'(x) = 0{/tex} , then we get {tex}~~~ ~~~x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} So, the point x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} divides the interval (-1, 1) into two disjoint intervals, {tex}\left(-1, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 1\right){/tex} In interval {tex}\left(-1, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} , we have {tex}f'(x) = 2x - 1 < 0{/tex} Therefore, the given function 'f 'is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(-1, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} Also, in interval {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 1\right){/tex} {tex}f'(x) = 2x - 1 > 0{/tex} Hence, the given function 'f ' is strictly increasing in interval for {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 1\right){/tex} . Therefore, f is neither strictly increasing nor decreasing in interval (-1, 1).
solution_html:
It is given that function f(x) = x2 - x + 1
{tex}f'(x) = 2x - 1{/tex}
If {tex}f'(x) = 0{/tex}, then we get
{tex}~~~ ~~~x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
So, the point x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} divides the interval (-1, 1) into two disjoint intervals, {tex}\left(-1, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 1\right){/tex} 
In interval {tex}\left(-1, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}, we have
    {tex}f'(x) = 2x - 1 < 0{/tex}
Therefore, the given function 'f 'is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(-1, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} 
Also, in interval {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 1\right){/tex} 
    {tex}f'(x) = 2x - 1 > 0{/tex}
Hence, the given function 'f ' is strictly increasing in interval for {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 1\right){/tex}.
Therefore, f is neither strictly increasing nor decreasing in interval (-1, 1).

question_text: Is function cos x decreasing on ( {tex}0, \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} )?
question_html: Is function cos x decreasing on ({tex}0, \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex})?
solution_text: Let f 1 (x) = cos x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}_{1}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} = -sin x In interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} , {tex}\mathrm{f}_{1}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} = -sin x < 0. Therefore, f 1 (x) = cos x is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} .
solution_html:
Let f1(x) = cos x 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}_{1}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} = -sin x
In interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}, {tex}\mathrm{f}_{1}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} = -sin x < 0.
Therefore, f1(x) = cos x is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Is the function {tex}cos2x{/tex} decreasing on ( {tex}0, \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} )?
question_html: Is the function {tex}cos2x{/tex} decreasing on ({tex}0, \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex})?
solution_text: Let f(x) = cos 2x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} = -2 sin 2x Now, {tex}0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}0<2 x<\pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin 2x > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 sin 2x < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} = -2 sin 2x < 0 on {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} Therefore, f(x) = cos 2x is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} .
solution_html:
Let f(x) = cos 2x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} = -2 sin 2x
Now, {tex}0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}0<2 x<\pi{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin 2x > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 sin 2x < 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} = -2 sin 2x < 0 on {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} 
Therefore, f(x) = cos 2x is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Is the function {tex}cos3x{/tex} decreasing on {tex}(0, \frac{\pi}{2}){/tex} ?
question_html: Is the function {tex}cos3x{/tex} decreasing on {tex}(0, \frac{\pi}{2}){/tex}?
solution_text: Let f(x) = cos 3x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} = -3 sin 3x Now, {tex}f^\prime(x){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin 3x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = {tex}\pi{/tex} , as {tex}x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} The point {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} divides the interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} into two distinct intervals. i.e. {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} Now, in the interval, {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-3 \sin 3 x<0 \text { as }\left(0<x<\frac{\pi}{3} \Rightarrow 0<3 x<\pi\right){/tex} Therefore, 'f ' is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} Now, in the interval {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-3 \sin 3 x>0{/tex} as {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}<\mathrm{x}<\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \pi<3 \mathrm{x}<\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex} Therefore, 'f ' is strictly increasing in the interval {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} .
solution_html:
Let f(x) = cos 3x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} = -3 sin 3x
Now, {tex}f^\prime(x){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin 3x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = {tex}\pi{/tex}, as {tex}x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} 
The point {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} divides the interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} into two distinct intervals.
i.e. {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} 
Now, in the interval, {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} 
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-3 \sin 3 x<0 \text { as }\left(0<x<\frac{\pi}{3} \Rightarrow 0<3 x<\pi\right){/tex} 
Therefore, 'f ' is strictly decreasing in interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} 
Now, in the interval {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} 
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-3 \sin 3 x>0{/tex} as {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}<\mathrm{x}<\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \pi<3 \mathrm{x}<\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex} 
Therefore, 'f ' is strictly increasing in the interval {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}.

question_text: On which of the following intervals is the function f given by {tex}f(x) = x^{100} + sinx – 1{/tex} , decreasing?
question_html: On which of the following intervals is the function f given by {tex}f(x) = x^{100} + sinx – 1{/tex}, decreasing?

options.explanation: It is given that f (x) = x 100 + sin x –1 Then, f’(x) = 100x 99 + cosx In interval (0,1), cos x >0 and 100x 99 > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f’(x)>0 Therefore, function f is strictly increasing in interval (0,1). In interval {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex} , cos x < 0 and 100x 99 > 0. Also, 100x 99 > cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f’(x) > 0 in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex} Therefore, function f is strictly increasing in interval {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex} . In interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} , cos x > 0 and 100x 99 > 0. Also, 100x 99 > cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f’(x) > 0 on {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} Therefore, function f is strictly increasing in interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} . Hence, function f is strictly decreasing on none of the intervals.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: For what values of a the function f given by f(x) = x 2 + ax + 1 is increasing on [1, 2]?
question_html: For what values of a the function f given by f(x) = x2 + ax + 1 is increasing on [1, 2]?
solution_text: f(x) = x 2 + ax + 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f ' (x) = 2x + a Now, function f will be increasing in (1, 2), if f '(x) > 0 in (1, 2). {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + a > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x > -a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x > {tex}\frac {-a}{2}{/tex} Therefore, we have to find the least value of a such that {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x > {tex}\frac {-a}{2}{/tex} , when x {tex}\in{/tex} (1, 2). Thus, the least value of a for f to be increasing on (1, 2) is given by, {tex}\frac {-a}{2}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -2 Hence, the required value of a is -2.
solution_html:
f(x) = x2 + ax + 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) = 2x + a
Now, function f will be increasing in (1, 2), if f'(x) > 0 in (1, 2).
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + a > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x > -a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x > {tex}\frac {-a}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, we have to find the least value of a such that
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x > {tex}\frac {-a}{2}{/tex}, when x {tex}\in{/tex} (1, 2).
Thus, the least value of a for f to be increasing on (1, 2) is given by,
{tex}\frac {-a}{2}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -2
Hence, the required value of a is -2.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Let I be any interval disjoint from [-1, 1]. Prove that the function f given by {tex}f\left( x \right) = x + \frac{1}{x}{/tex} is increasing on I.
question_html: Let I be any interval disjoint from [-1, 1]. Prove that the function f given by {tex}f\left( x \right) = x + \frac{1}{x}{/tex} is increasing on I.
solution_text: Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = x + \frac{1}{x} = x + {x^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 1 + \left( { - 1} \right){x^{ - 2}} = 1 - \frac{1}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{{x^2} - 1}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = \frac{{\left( {x - 1} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right)}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i) Here for every x either {tex}x < - 1{/tex} or x > 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} for x< -1, x = -2 (say) {tex}f'\left( x \right)\frac{{\left( - \right)\left( - \right)}}{{\left( + \right)}} = \left( + \right) > 0{/tex} And for x > 1, x = 2 (say), {tex}f'\left( x \right)\frac{{\left( + \right)\left( + \right)}}{{\left( + \right)}} = \left( + \right) > 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) > 0{/tex} for all {tex}x \in I{/tex} , hence f(x) is strictly increasing on I.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = x + \frac{1}{x} = x + {x^{ - 1}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 1 + \left( { - 1} \right){x^{ - 2}} = 1 - \frac{1}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{{x^2} - 1}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = \frac{{\left( {x - 1} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right)}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i)

Here for every x either {tex}x < - 1{/tex} or x > 1

{tex}\therefore{/tex}  for x< -1, x = -2 (say)

{tex}f'\left( x \right)\frac{{\left( - \right)\left( - \right)}}{{\left( + \right)}} = \left( + \right) > 0{/tex}

And for x > 1, x = 2 (say),

{tex}f'\left( x \right)\frac{{\left( + \right)\left( + \right)}}{{\left( + \right)}} = \left( + \right) > 0{/tex}

{tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) > 0{/tex} for all {tex}x \in I{/tex}, hence f(x) is strictly increasing on I.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function f given by f(x) = log sinx is increasing on {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} and decreasing on {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},\pi } \right){/tex} .
question_html: Prove that the function f given by f(x) = log sinx is increasing on {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} and decreasing on {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},\pi } \right){/tex}.
solution_text: Given: f(x) = log sin x {tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = \frac{1}{{\sin x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}(\sin x) = \frac{1}{{\sin x}}\cos x = \cot x{/tex} On the interval {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} i.e., in first quadrant, f'(x) = cotx > 0 Therefore, f(x) is strictly increasing on {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} . On the interval {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},\pi } \right){/tex} i.e., in second quadrant, f'(x) = cot x < 0 Therefore, f(x) is strictly decreasing on {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},\pi } \right){/tex} .
solution_html:
Given: f(x) = log sin x
{tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = \frac{1}{{\sin x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}(\sin x) = \frac{1}{{\sin x}}\cos x = \cot x{/tex}
On the interval {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} i.e., in first quadrant,
f'(x) = cotx > 0
Therefore, f(x) is strictly increasing on {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}.
On the interval {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},\pi } \right){/tex} i.e., in second quadrant,
f'(x) = cot x < 0
Therefore, f(x) is strictly decreasing on {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},\pi } \right){/tex}.

question_text: Prove that the function f given by f (x) = log |cos x| is decreasing on {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} and increasing on {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}, 2 \pi\right){/tex} .
question_html: Prove that the function f given by f (x) = log |cos x| is decreasing on {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} and increasing on {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}, 2 \pi\right){/tex}.
solution_text: It is given that f(x) = log |cos x| {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{1}{\cos \mathrm{x}}(-\sin \mathrm{x})=-\tan \mathrm{x}{/tex} In interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right), f^{\prime}(x)=-\tan x<0{/tex} Therefore, f is strictly decreasing on {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} In interval {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}, 2 \pi\right), f^{\prime}(x)=-\tan x>0{/tex} Therefore, f is strictly increasing in {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}, 2 \pi\right){/tex} .
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = log |cos x|
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{1}{\cos \mathrm{x}}(-\sin \mathrm{x})=-\tan \mathrm{x}{/tex}
In interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right), f^{\prime}(x)=-\tan x<0{/tex} 
Therefore, f is strictly decreasing on {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
In interval {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}, 2 \pi\right), f^{\prime}(x)=-\tan x>0{/tex} 
Therefore, f is strictly increasing in {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}, 2 \pi\right){/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function given by {tex}f\left( x \right) = {x^3} - 3{x^2} + 3x - 100{/tex} is increasing in R.
question_html: Prove that the function given by {tex}f\left( x \right) = {x^3} - 3{x^2} + 3x - 100{/tex} is increasing in R.
solution_text: Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = {x^3} - 3{x^2} + 3x - 100{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 3{x^2} - 6x + 3 = 3\left( {{x^2} - 2x + 1} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 3{\left( {x - 1} \right)^2} \geqslant 0{/tex} for all x in R. Therefore, {tex}f\left( x \right){/tex} is increasing in R.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = {x^3} - 3{x^2} + 3x - 100{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 3{x^2} - 6x + 3 = 3\left( {{x^2} - 2x + 1} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 3{\left( {x - 1} \right)^2} \geqslant 0{/tex} for all x in R.

Therefore, {tex}f\left( x \right){/tex} is increasing in R.

question_text: The interval in which y = x 2 e –x is increasing is
question_html: The interval in which y = x2 e–x is increasing is

options.explanation: It is given that y = x 2 e –x then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2xe -x - x 2 e -x = xe -x (2-x) Now, if {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 and x =2 The points x = 0 and x= 2 divide the real line into three disjoint intervals ie, (- {tex}\infty{/tex} ,0), (0,2) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). In interval (- {tex}\infty{/tex} ,0) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ), f’(x) < 0 as e -x is always positive. Therefore, f is decreasing on (- {tex}\infty{/tex} ,0) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). In interval (0,2), f’(x) > 0 Therefore, f is strictly increasing in interval (0.2).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the maximum and minimum value, f(x) = (2x - 1) 2 + 3
question_html: Find the maximum and minimum value, f(x) = (2x - 1)2 + 3
solution_text: It is given that f(x) = (2x - 1) 2 + 3 Now, we can see that (2x - 1) 2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = (2x - 1) 2 + 3 {tex}\ge{/tex} 3 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R The minimum value of f is attained when 2x - 1 = 0 2x - 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Then, Minimum value of {tex}f=f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\left(2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}-1\right)^{2}+3=3{/tex} Now, f' (x) = 4x - 2 = 0, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac12{/tex} is the only critical point which is a minimum. Therefore, function f does not have a maximum value.
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = (2x - 1)2 + 3
Now, we can see that (2x - 1)2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = (2x - 1)2 + 3 {tex}\ge{/tex} 3 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R
The minimum value of f is attained when 2x - 1 = 0
2x - 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
Then, Minimum value of {tex}f=f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\left(2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}-1\right)^{2}+3=3{/tex} 
Now, f' (x) = 4x - 2 = 0, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  x = {tex}\frac12{/tex} is the only critical point which is a minimum.
Therefore, function f does not have a maximum value.

question_text: Find the maximum and minimum value, f(x) = 9x 2 + 12x + 2
question_html: Find the maximum and minimum value, f(x) = 9x2 + 12x + 2
solution_text: It is given that f(x) = 9x 2 + 12x + 2 = (3x + 2) 2 - 2 Now, we can see that (3x + 2) 2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = (3x + 2) 2 - 2 {tex}\ge{/tex} -2 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R The minimum value of f is attained when 3x + 2 = 0 3x + 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=-\frac{2}{3}{/tex} Then, Minimum value of {tex}f=f\left(-\frac{2}{3}\right)=\left(3\left(-\frac{2}{3}\right)+2\right)^{2}-2=-2{/tex} Also, since x = - {tex}\frac23{/tex} , is the only critical point which is a minimum, Therefore, function f does not have a maximum value.
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = 9x2 + 12x + 2 = (3x + 2)2 - 2 
Now, we can see that (3x + 2)2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = (3x + 2)2 - 2 {tex}\ge{/tex} -2 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R
The minimum value of f is attained when 3x + 2 = 0
3x + 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=-\frac{2}{3}{/tex} 
Then, Minimum value of {tex}f=f\left(-\frac{2}{3}\right)=\left(3\left(-\frac{2}{3}\right)+2\right)^{2}-2=-2{/tex} 
Also, since  x = -{tex}\frac23{/tex}, is the only critical point which is a minimum,
Therefore, function f does not have a maximum value.
