question_text: Find the maximum and minimum value, f(x) = -(x - 1) 2 + 10
question_html: Find the maximum and minimum value, f(x) = -(x - 1)2 + 10
solution_text: It is given that f(x) = -(x - 1) 2 + 10 Now, we can see that (x - 1) 2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = -(x - 1) 2 + 10 {tex}\le{/tex} 10 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R The maximum value of f is attained when x - 1 = 0 i.e, x - 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 Then, Maximum value of f = f(1) = -(1 - 1) 2 + 10 = 10 Since, x = 1 is the only critical point, Therefore, function f does not have a minimum value.
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = -(x - 1)2 + 10
Now, we can see that (x - 1)2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = -(x - 1)2 + 10 {tex}\le{/tex} 10 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R
The maximum value of f is attained when x - 1 = 0
i.e,  x - 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}   x = 1
Then, Maximum value of f = f(1) = -(1 - 1)2 + 10 = 10
Since, x = 1 is the only critical point,
Therefore, function f does not have a minimum value.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the maximum and minimum value, g(x) = x 3 + 1
question_html: Find the maximum and minimum value, g(x) = x3 + 1

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the maximum and minimum value, f(x) = |x + 2| - 1
question_html: Find the maximum and minimum value, f(x) = |x + 2| - 1
solution_text: It is given that f(x) = |x + 2| - 1 Now, we can see that |x + 2| {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=|x+2|-1 \geq-1{/tex} for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R Clearly, the minimum value of f is attained when |x + 2| = 0 i.e, |x + 2| = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2 Then, Minimum value of f = f(-2) = |-2 + 2| - 1 = -1 Therefore, function f does not have a maximum value.
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = |x + 2| - 1
Now, we can see that |x + 2| {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=|x+2|-1 \geq-1{/tex} for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R
Clearly, the minimum value of f is attained when |x + 2| = 0
i.e, |x + 2| = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2
Then, Minimum value of f = f(-2) = |-2 + 2| - 1 = -1
Therefore, function f does not have a maximum value.

question_text: Find the maximum and minimum value, g(x) = -|x + 1| + 3
question_html: Find the maximum and minimum value, g(x) = -|x + 1| + 3
solution_text: It is given that g(x) = -|x + 1| + 3 Now, we can see that -|x + 1| {tex}\le{/tex} 0 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow g(x)=-|x+1|+3 \leq 3{/tex} for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R The maximum value of f is attained when |x + 1| = 0 |x + 1| = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x=-1{/tex} Then, Maximum value of g = g(-1) = -|-1 + 1| + 3 = 3 Therefore, function f does not have a minimum value.
solution_html:
It is given that g(x) = -|x + 1| + 3
Now, we can see that -|x + 1| {tex}\le{/tex} 0 for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow g(x)=-|x+1|+3 \leq 3{/tex} for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R
The maximum value of f is attained when |x + 1| = 0
|x + 1| = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow x=-1{/tex} 
Then, Maximum value of g = g(-1) = -|-1 + 1| + 3 = 3
Therefore, function f does not have a minimum value.

question_text: Find the maximum and minimum value of, h(x) = sin(2x) + 5
question_html: Find the maximum and minimum value of, h(x) = sin(2x) + 5
solution_text: It is given that h(x) = sin(2x) + 5 Now, we can see that {tex}-1 \leq \sin 2 x \leq 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-1+5 \leq \sin 2 x+5 \leq 1+5{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \leq \sin 2 x+5 \leq 6{/tex} Therefore, the maximum and minimum value of function h are 6 and 4 respectively.
solution_html:
It is given that h(x) = sin(2x) + 5
Now, we can see that {tex}-1 \leq \sin 2 x \leq 1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow-1+5 \leq \sin 2 x+5 \leq 1+5{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 \leq \sin 2 x+5 \leq 6{/tex} 
Therefore, the maximum and minimum value of function h are 6 and 4 respectively.

question_text: Find the maximum and minimum value of, h(x) = x + 1, x {tex}\in{/tex} (-1, 1)
question_html: Find the maximum and minimum value of, h(x) = x + 1, x {tex}\in{/tex} (-1, 1)
solution_text: It is given that h(x) = x + 1, x {tex}\in{/tex} (-1, 1) Now, if a point x 0 is closest to -1, then, We find {tex}\frac{x_{0}}{2}+1<x_{0}+1{/tex} for all x 0 {tex}\in{/tex} (-1, 1) Also, if a point x 1 is closest to 1, then, We find {tex}\mathrm{x}_{1}+1<\frac{\mathrm{x}_{1}} 2+1{/tex} for all x 1 {tex}\in{/tex} (-1, 1) Therefore, the function h(x) has neither maximum nor minimum value in (-1, 1).

We find {tex}\frac{x_{0}}{2}+1<x_{0}+1{/tex} for all x0 {tex}\in{/tex} (-1, 1)

We find {tex}\mathrm{x}_{1}+1<\frac{\mathrm{x}_{1}} 2+1{/tex} for all x1 {tex}\in{/tex} (-1, 1)
Therefore, the function h(x) has neither maximum nor minimum value in (-1, 1).

question_text: Find the local maxima and local minima, of function. Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value, as the case may be: f(x) = x 2
question_html: Find the local maxima and local minima, of function. Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value, as the case may be: f(x) = x2
solution_text: f(x) = x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2x Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 is the only critical point which could possibly be the point of local maxima or local minima of f. {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(0)=2{/tex} , which is positive. Then, by second derivative test, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 is point of local minimum of f and value of 'f ' at x = 0 is f(0) = 0
solution_html:
f(x) = x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2x
Now, f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 is the only critical point which could possibly be the point of local maxima or local minima of f.
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(0)=2{/tex}, which is positive.
Then, by second derivative test,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 is point of local minimum of f and value of 'f ' at x = 0 is f(0) = 0

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the local maxima and local minima, of function. Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value, as the case may be: g(x) = x 3 - 3x
question_html: Find the local maxima and local minima, of function. Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value, as the case may be: g(x) = x3 - 3x
solution_text: Here, g(x) = x 3 - 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g'(x) = 3x 2 - 3 Now, g'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 2 - 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 2 = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, -1 are the critical points. Further, g''(x) = 6x Now, g''(1) = 6 > 0 and g''(-1) = -6 < 0 Then, by second derivative test, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 is point of local minimum of g and at x = 1, we have g(1) = 1 3 - 3 = 1 - 3 = -2 Moreover, x = -1 is point of local maximum value of g and at x = -1, we have g(-1) = (-1) 3 - 3(-1) = -1 + 3 = 2 So, the local maxima and minima are -1 and 1 respectively. The local minimum and maximum values are -2 and 2 respectively.
solution_html:
Here, g(x) = x3 - 3x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g'(x) = 3x2 - 3
Now, g'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 - 3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, -1 are the critical points.
Further, g''(x) = 6x
Now, g''(1) = 6 > 0
and g''(-1) = -6 < 0
Then, by second derivative test,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 is point of local minimum of g and at x = 1, we have
g(1) = 13 - 3 = 1 - 3 = -2
Moreover,
x = -1 is point of local maximum value of g and  at x = -1, we have
g(-1) = (-1)3 - 3(-1) = -1 + 3 = 2
So, the local maxima and minima are -1 and 1 respectively.
The local minimum and maximum values are -2 and 2 respectively.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the local maxima and local minima, of function. Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value, as the case may be: h(x) = sin x + cos x, {tex}0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
question_html:
Find the local maxima and local minima, of function. Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value, as the case may be:
h(x) = sin x + cos x, {tex}0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution_text: Here, h(x) = sin x + cos x, {tex}0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} h'(x) = cos x - sin x Now, h'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x - sin x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x = sin x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} h''(x) = -sin x - cos x = -(sin x + cos x) {tex}h^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=-\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=-\sqrt{2}<0{/tex} Then, by second derivative test, {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is point of local maxima and local maximum value of h at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is {tex}h\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{\pi}{4}+\cos \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,
h(x) = sin x + cos x, {tex}0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} 
h'(x) = cos x - sin x
Now, h'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x - sin x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x = sin x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} 
h''(x) = -sin x - cos x = -(sin x + cos x)
{tex}h^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=-\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=-\sqrt{2}<0{/tex} 
Then, by second derivative test,
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is point of local maxima and local maximum value of h at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is
{tex}h\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{\pi}{4}+\cos \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the local maxima and local minima of the function. Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value: f(x) = sinx - cosx, 0 < x < 2 {tex} \pi{/tex} .
question_html: Find the local maxima and local minima of the function. Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value: f(x) = sinx - cosx, 0 < x < 2{tex} \pi{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, {tex} f ( x ) = \sin x - \cos x , 0 < x < 2 \pi{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x ,we get, f'(x) = cosx + sinx..........(i) For local maximum and local minimum, Put f'(x) = 0, i.e. {tex} \cos x + \sin x = 0 \Rightarrow \cos x = - \sin x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan x = - 1 \Rightarrow x = \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \text { or } 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \text { or } \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} Again, on differentiating both sides of Eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get, f'' (x)=-sin x + cos x When {tex}x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } ,{/tex} then {tex}f''\left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right) = - \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = - \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) + \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex}= - \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } < 0{/tex} When {tex} x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } , \text { then } f ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right) = - \sin \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = - \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) + \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex} = \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } > 0{/tex} Thus, {tex} x = \frac { 3 { \pi } } { 4 }{/tex} is a point of local maxima and {tex} x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} is a point of local minima. Now, the local maximum value, {tex} f \left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right) = \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex} = \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} and the local minimum value, {tex} f \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right) = \sin \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex} = - \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = - \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = - \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex} f ( x ) = \sin x - \cos x , 0 < x < 2 \pi{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x ,we get,
f'(x) = cosx + sinx..........(i)
For local maximum and local minimum,
Put f'(x) = 0,
i.e. {tex} \cos x + \sin x = 0 \Rightarrow \cos x = - \sin x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan x = - 1 \Rightarrow x = \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \text { or } 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \text { or } \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
Again, on differentiating both sides of Eq. (i) 
w.r.t. x, we get,
f'' (x)=-sin x + cos x
When {tex}x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } ,{/tex}then
{tex}f''\left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right) = - \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} = - \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) + \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= - \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } < 0{/tex}
When {tex} x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } , \text { then } f ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right) = - \sin \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} = - \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) + \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}
{tex} = \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } > 0{/tex}
Thus, {tex} x = \frac { 3 { \pi } } { 4 }{/tex} is a point of local maxima and {tex} x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} is a point of local minima.
Now, the local maximum value,
{tex} f \left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right) = \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} = \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}
{tex} = \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
and the local minimum value,
{tex} f \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right) = \sin \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} = \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}
{tex} = - \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}

{tex} = - \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = - \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the local maxima and local minima of the function f(x) = x 3 - 6x 2 + 9x + 15 Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value.
question_html:
Find the local maxima and local minima of the function 
f(x) = x3 - 6x2 + 9x + 15 
Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value.
solution_text: Here, f(x) = x 3 - 6x 2 + 9x + 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 3x 2 - 12x + 9 Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 2 - 12x + 9 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3(x - 1)(x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, 3 f ''(x) = 6x - 12 = 6(x - 2) Now, f ''(1) = 6(1 - 2) = -6 < 0 and f ''(3) = 6(3 - 2) = 6 > 0 Then, by second derivative test, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 is point of local maxima and local maximum value of f at x = 1 is f(1) = 1- 6 + 9 + 15 = 19 Also, x = 3 is point of local minima and local minimum value of f at x = 3 is f(3) = 27 - 54 + 27 + 15 = 15
solution_html:
Here,
f(x) = x3 - 6x2 + 9x + 15
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 3x2 - 12x + 9
Now, f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 - 12x + 9 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3(x - 1)(x - 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, 3
f ''(x) = 6x - 12 = 6(x - 2)
Now, f ''(1) = 6(1 - 2) = -6 < 0 and f ''(3) = 6(3 - 2) = 6 > 0
Then, by second derivative test,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 is point of local maxima and local maximum value of f at x = 1 is
f(1) = 1- 6 + 9 + 15 = 19
Also,
x = 3 is point of local minima and local minimum value of f at x = 3 is
f(3) = 27 - 54 + 27 + 15 = 15

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the local maxima and local minima, of function {tex}g(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x}, \quad x>0{/tex} Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value.
question_html:
Find the local maxima and local minima, of function
 {tex}g(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x}, \quad x>0{/tex}
Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value.
solution_text: Here, {tex}g(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x}, x>0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} Now, g'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^{2}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, -2. Since x > 0, we take x = 2 {tex}g^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{4}{x^{3}}{/tex} {tex}g^{\prime \prime}(2)=\frac{4}{2^{3}}=\frac{1}{2}>0{/tex} Then, by second derivative test, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 is point of local minima and local minimum value of g at x = 2 is {tex}g(2)=\frac{2}{2}+\frac{2}{2}=1+1=2{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,
{tex}g(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x}, x>0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} 
Now,  g'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^{2}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  x2 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  x = 2, -2.
Since x > 0, we take x = 2
{tex}g^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{4}{x^{3}}{/tex} 
{tex}g^{\prime \prime}(2)=\frac{4}{2^{3}}=\frac{1}{2}>0{/tex} 
Then, by second derivative test,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 is point of local minima and local minimum value of g at x = 2 is
{tex}g(2)=\frac{2}{2}+\frac{2}{2}=1+1=2{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the local maxima and local minima of function {tex}g(x)=\frac{1}{x^{2}+2}{/tex} Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value.
question_html:
Find the local maxima and local minima of function 
 {tex}g(x)=\frac{1}{x^{2}+2}{/tex}
Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value.
solution_text: Here, {tex}g(x)=\frac{1}{x^{2}+2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x)=\frac{-(2 x)}{\left(x^{2}+2\right)^{2}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 Now, for values close to x = 0 and to the left of 0 g'(x) > 0 Also, for values close to x = 0 and to the right of 0, g'(x) < 0 Then, by first derivative test, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 is point of local maxima and local maximum of g at x = 0 is {tex}g(0)=\frac{1}{0+2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,
{tex}g(x)=\frac{1}{x^{2}+2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x)=\frac{-(2 x)}{\left(x^{2}+2\right)^{2}}=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0
Now, for values close to x = 0 and to the left of 0
g'(x) > 0
Also, for values close to x = 0 and to the right of 0,
g'(x) < 0
Then, by first derivative test,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 is point of local maxima and local maximum of g at x = 0 is
{tex}g(0)=\frac{1}{0+2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the local maxima and local minima of function, {tex}f(x)=x \sqrt{1-x}, \quad 0<x<1{/tex} Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value.
question_html:
Find the local maxima and local minima of function,
 {tex}f(x)=x \sqrt{1-x}, \quad 0<x<1{/tex}
Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value.
solution_text: Here, {tex}f(x)=x \sqrt{1-x}, 0<x<1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{x} \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}}(-1){/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2 \sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2(1-\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{x}}{2 \sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}}{/tex} f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2-3 x}{2 \sqrt{1-x}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 - 3x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1-x}(-3)-(2-3 x)\left(\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{1-x}}\right)}{1-x}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}(-3)+(2-3 \mathrm{x})\left(\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}}\right)}{2(1-\mathrm{x})}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{-6(1-\mathrm{x})+(2-3 \mathrm{x})}{2(1-\mathrm{x})^{\frac{3}{2}}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 x-4}{4(1-x)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} Now, {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)=-\frac{2}{4( \frac13)^ \frac32}{/tex} < 0, Therefore, the second derivative test, x = {tex}\frac23{/tex} is the point of local maxima and the local maximum value of 'f ' at x = {tex}\frac23{/tex} is f( {tex}\frac23{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{2}{3\sqrt3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,
{tex}f(x)=x \sqrt{1-x}, 0<x<1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{x} \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}}(-1){/tex} 
{tex}=\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2 \sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}}{/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{2(1-\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{x}}{2 \sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}}{/tex} 
f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2-3 x}{2 \sqrt{1-x}}=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 - 3x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1-x}(-3)-(2-3 x)\left(\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{1-x}}\right)}{1-x}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}=\left[\frac{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}(-3)+(2-3 \mathrm{x})\left(\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}}}\right)}{2(1-\mathrm{x})}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}=\left[\frac{-6(1-\mathrm{x})+(2-3 \mathrm{x})}{2(1-\mathrm{x})^{\frac{3}{2}}}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{3 x-4}{4(1-x)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} 
Now,
{tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)=-\frac{2}{4( \frac13)^ \frac32}{/tex}   < 0,
Therefore, the second derivative test,  x = {tex}\frac23{/tex} is the point of local maxima and the local maximum value
of 'f ' at  x = {tex}\frac23{/tex}     is
f({tex}\frac23{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{2}{3\sqrt3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function does not have maxima or minima: f(x) = e x
question_html: Prove that the function does not have maxima or minima: f(x) = ex
solution_text: f(x) = e x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = e x Now, if f'(x) = 0, then e x = 0 But, the exponential function can never assume 0 for any value of x. Therefore, there does not exist c {tex}\in{/tex} R such that f'(c) = 0 Hence, function f does not have maxima or minima.
solution_html:
f(x) = ex 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = ex 
Now, if f'(x) = 0, then ex = 0 
But, the exponential function can never assume 0 for any value of x.
Therefore, there does not exist c {tex}\in{/tex} R such that f'(c) = 0
Hence, function f does not have maxima or minima.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function does not have maxima or minima: g(x) = log x
question_html: Prove that the function does not have maxima or minima: g(x) = log x
solution_text: g(x) = log x {tex}\Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} Since, log x is defined for a positive number x, g'(x) > 0 for any x. Therefore, there does not exist c {tex}\in{/tex} R such that f'(c) = 0 Hence, function f does not have maxima or minima.
solution_html:
g(x) = log x
{tex}\Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} 
Since, log x is defined for a positive number x,
g'(x) > 0 for any x.
Therefore, there does not exist c {tex}\in{/tex} R such that f'(c) = 0
Hence, function f does not have maxima or minima.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function does not have maxima or minima: h(x) = x 3 + x 2 + x +1
question_html: Prove that the function does not have maxima or minima: h(x) = x3 + x2 + x +1
solution_text: h(x) = x 3 + x 2 + x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h'(x) = 3x 2 + 2x + 1 h(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 2 + 2x + 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{-2 \pm 2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{i}}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{2} i}{3}, \notin R{/tex} Therefore, there does not exist c {tex}\in{/tex} R such that h'(c) = 0, i.e, there are no real critical points. Hence, function h does not have maxima or minima.
solution_html:
h(x) = x3 + x2 + x + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h'(x) = 3x2 + 2x + 1 
h(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 + 2x + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{-2 \pm 2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{i}}{6}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{2} i}{3}, \notin R{/tex} 
Therefore, there does not exist c {tex}\in{/tex} R such that h'(c) = 0, i.e, there are no  real critical points.
Hence, function h does not have maxima or minima.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the absolute maximum value and the absolute minimum value of the function: f(x) = x 3 , x {tex}\in{/tex} [-2, 2]
question_html:
Find the absolute maximum value and the absolute minimum value of the function:
f(x) = x3, x {tex}\in{/tex} [-2, 2]
solution_text: It is given that f(x) = x 3 , x {tex}\in{/tex} [-2, 2] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 3x 2 Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 Further, we evaluate the value of f at critical point x = 0 and at end points of the interval [-2, 2]. f(0) = 0 f(-2) = (-2) 3 = -8 f(2) = (2) 3 = 8 Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f on [-2, 2] is 8 occurring at x = 2 And, the absolute minimum value of f on [-2, 2] is -8 occurring at x = -2
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = x3, x {tex}\in{/tex} [-2, 2]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 3x2
Now, f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0
Further, we evaluate the value of f at critical point x = 0 and at end points of the interval [-2, 2].
f(0) = 0
f(-2) = (-2)3 = -8 
f(2) = (2)3 = 8
Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f on [-2, 2] is 8 occurring at x = 2
And, the absolute minimum value of f on [-2, 2] is -8 occurring at x = -2

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the absolute maximum value and the absolute minimum value of the function: f(x) = sin x + cos x , x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ]
question_html:
Find the absolute maximum value and the absolute minimum value of the function:
f(x) = sin x + cos x , x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex}]
solution_text: It is given that f(x) = sin x + cos x, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} f'(x) = cos x - sin x Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x - sin x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x = sin x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Now, we evaluate the value of 'f ' at critical point {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and at end points of the interval [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ] {tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{\pi}{4}+ \cos \frac{\pi}{4}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} f(0) = sin 0 + cos 0 = 0 + 1 = 1 {tex}f(\pi)=\sin \pi+\cos \pi=0-1=-1{/tex} Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f on {tex}[0, \pi]{/tex} is {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} occuring at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} And, the absolute minimum value of f on {tex}[0, \pi]{/tex} is -1 occurring at x = {tex}\pi{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = sin x + cos x, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} 
f'(x) = cos x - sin x
Now, f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x - sin x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x = sin x 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} 
Now, we evaluate the value of 'f ' at critical point {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and at end points of the interval [0, {tex}\pi{/tex}]
{tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{\pi}{4}+ \cos \frac{\pi}{4}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
f(0) = sin 0 + cos 0 = 0 + 1 = 1
{tex}f(\pi)=\sin \pi+\cos \pi=0-1=-1{/tex} 
Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f on {tex}[0, \pi]{/tex} is {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} occuring at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} 
And, the absolute minimum value of f on {tex}[0, \pi]{/tex} is -1 occurring at x = {tex}\pi{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the absolute maximum value and the absolute minimum value of the function: {tex}f(x)=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^{2}, x \in\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
Find the absolute maximum value and the absolute minimum value of the function:
{tex}f(x)=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^{2}, x \in\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: Given that {tex}f(x)=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^{2}, x \in\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=4-\frac{1}{2}(2 x)=4-x{/tex} Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4 Now, we evaluate the value of f at critical point x = 0 and at end points of the interval {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}f(4)=16-\frac{1}{2}(16)=16-8=8{/tex} {tex}f(-2)=-8-\frac{1}{2}(4)=-8-2=-10{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)=4\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)^{2}=18-\frac{81}{8}=18-10.125=7.875{/tex} Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f on {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} is 8 occurring at x = 4 And, the absolute minimum value of f on {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} is -10 occurring at x = -2
solution_html:
Given that {tex}f(x)=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^{2}, x \in\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=4-\frac{1}{2}(2 x)=4-x{/tex} 
Now, f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4
Now, we evaluate the value of f at critical point x = 0 and at end points of the interval {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}f(4)=16-\frac{1}{2}(16)=16-8=8{/tex} 
{tex}f(-2)=-8-\frac{1}{2}(4)=-8-2=-10{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)=4\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)^{2}=18-\frac{81}{8}=18-10.125=7.875{/tex} 
Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f on {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} is 8 occurring at x = 4
And, the absolute minimum value of f on {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} is -10 occurring at x = -2

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the absolute maximum value and the absolute minimum value of the function: {tex}f(x)=(x-1)^{2}+3, x \in[-3,1]{/tex}
question_html:
Find the absolute maximum value and the absolute minimum value of the function:
{tex}f(x)=(x-1)^{2}+3, x \in[-3,1]{/tex}
solution_text: Given that {tex}f(x)=(x-1)^{2}+3, x \in[-3,1]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2(x - 1) Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(x - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 Now, we evaluate the value of 'f ' at critical point x = 1, and at end points of the interval [-3, 1]. f(1) = (1 - 1) 2 + 3 = 0 + 3 = 3 f(-3) = (-3 - 1) 2 + 3 = 16 + 3 = 19 Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f on [-3, 1] is 19 occuring at x = -3. And the absolute minimum value of f on [-3, 1] is 3 occurring at x = 1.
solution_html:
Given that {tex}f(x)=(x-1)^{2}+3, x \in[-3,1]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2(x - 1)
Now, f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(x - 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1
Now, we evaluate the value of 'f ' at critical point x = 1, and at end points of the interval [-3, 1].
f(1) = (1 - 1)2 + 3 = 0 + 3 = 3
f(-3) = (-3 - 1)2 + 3 = 16 + 3 = 19
Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f on [-3, 1] is 19 occuring at x = -3.
And  the absolute minimum value of f on [-3, 1] is 3 occurring at x = 1.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the maximum profit that a company can make, if the profit function is given by p(x) = 41 – 72x – 18x 2
question_html:
Find the maximum profit that a company can make, if the profit function is given by
p(x) = 41 – 72x – 18x2
solution_text: The profit function is, p(x) = 41 - 72x - 18x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p'(x) = -72 - 36x and p''(x) = -36 Now, p'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 72 = -36x {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{-72}{36}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2 p''(-2) = -36 < 0 Then, by second derivative test, x = -2 is point of local maxima of p. So, the local maximum value is p(-2) = 41 - 72(-2) - 18(-2) 2 = 41 + 144 - 72 = 113 Therefore, the maximum profit that the company can make is 113 units.
solution_html:
The profit function is, p(x) = 41 - 72x - 18x2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p'(x) = -72 - 36x and p''(x) = -36
Now, p'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 72 = -36x
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{-72}{36}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2
p''(-2) = -36 < 0
Then, by second derivative test, x = -2 is point of local maxima of p.
So, the local maximum value is
p(-2) = 41 - 72(-2) - 18(-2)2 
= 41 + 144 - 72
= 113
Therefore, the maximum profit that the company can make is 113 units.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find both the maximum value and minimum value of {tex}3{x^4} - 8{x^3} + 12{x^2} - 48x + 25{/tex} on the interval [0, 3].
question_html: Find both the maximum value and minimum value of {tex}3{x^4} - 8{x^3} + 12{x^2} - 48x + 25{/tex} on the interval [0, 3].
solution_text: Let {tex}f(x) = 3{x^4} - 8{x^3} + 12{x^2} - 48x + 25{/tex} on [0, 3] {tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) = 12{x^3} - 24{x^2} + 24x - 48{/tex} Now {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 12{x^3} - 24{x^2} + 24x - 48 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x^3} - 2{x^2} + 2x - 4 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {x - 2} \right)\left( {{x^2} + 2} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = 2{/tex} or {tex}x = \pm \sqrt 2 {/tex} Since {tex}x = \pm \sqrt 2 {/tex} is irrational, therefore it is rejected. {tex}\therefore x = 2{/tex} is turning point. {tex}\therefore{/tex} At {tex}x = 2,f\left( 2 \right) = 3\left( {16} \right) - 8\left( 8 \right) + 12\left( 4 \right) - 48\left( 2 \right) + 25 = - 39{/tex} At x = 0 f(0) = 25 At x = 3, {tex}f\left( 3 \right) = 3\left( {81} \right) - 8\left( {27} \right) + 12\left( 9 \right) - 48\left( 3 \right) + 25 = 16{/tex} Therefore, absolute minimum value is - 39 and absolute maximum value is 25.
solution_html:
Let {tex}f(x) = 3{x^4} - 8{x^3} + 12{x^2} - 48x + 25{/tex} on [0, 3]
{tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) = 12{x^3} - 24{x^2} + 24x - 48{/tex}
Now {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 12{x^3} - 24{x^2} + 24x - 48 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {x^3} - 2{x^2} + 2x - 4 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {x - 2} \right)\left( {{x^2} + 2} \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x = 2{/tex} or {tex}x = \pm \sqrt 2 {/tex} 
Since {tex}x = \pm \sqrt 2 {/tex} is irrational, therefore it is rejected.
{tex}\therefore x = 2{/tex} is turning point.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  At {tex}x = 2,f\left( 2 \right) = 3\left( {16} \right) - 8\left( 8 \right) + 12\left( 4 \right) - 48\left( 2 \right) + 25 = - 39{/tex} 
At x = 0 f(0) = 25 
At x = 3, {tex}f\left( 3 \right) = 3\left( {81} \right) - 8\left( {27} \right) + 12\left( 9 \right) - 48\left( 3 \right) + 25 = 16{/tex} 
Therefore, absolute minimum value is - 39 and absolute maximum value is 25.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: At what points in the interval [0, 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ], does the function sin 2x attain its maximum value?
question_html: At what points in the interval [0, 2{tex}\pi{/tex}], does the function sin 2x attain its maximum value?
solution_text: Given that f(x) = sin 2x, {tex}x \in[0,2 \pi]{/tex} {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) = 2 cos 2x Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos 2x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}, \frac{7 \pi}{4}{/tex} Now, we evaluate the value of f at critical point {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}, \frac{7 \pi}{4}{/tex} and at end points of the interval [0, 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ] {tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=1{/tex} {tex}f\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{3 \pi}{2}=-1{/tex} {tex}f\left(\frac{5 \pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{5 \pi}{2}=1{/tex} {tex}f\left(\frac{7 \pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{7 \pi}{2}=-1{/tex} f(0) = sin 0, and f(2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ) = sin 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} = 0 Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f on {tex}[0,2 \pi]{/tex} is 1 occuring at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and {tex}x=\frac{5 \pi}{4}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given that f(x) = sin 2x, {tex}x \in[0,2 \pi]{/tex} 
{tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) = 2 cos 2x
Now, f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos 2x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}, \frac{7 \pi}{4}{/tex} 
Now, we evaluate the value of f at critical point {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}, \frac{7 \pi}{4}{/tex} and at end points of the interval [0, 2{tex}\pi{/tex}]
{tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=1{/tex} 
{tex}f\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{3 \pi}{2}=-1{/tex} 
{tex}f\left(\frac{5 \pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{5 \pi}{2}=1{/tex} 
{tex}f\left(\frac{7 \pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{7 \pi}{2}=-1{/tex} 
f(0) = sin 0, and  f(2{tex}\pi{/tex}) = sin 4{tex}\pi{/tex} = 0
Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f on {tex}[0,2 \pi]{/tex} is 1 occuring at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and {tex}x=\frac{5 \pi}{4}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: What is the maximum value of the function sin x + cos x?
question_html: What is the maximum value of the function sin x + cos x?
solution_text: Let f(x) = sin x + cos x, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = cos x - sin x Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x - sin x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x = sin x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}, \ldots{/tex} Now, If f''(x) will be negative when (sin x + cos x) > 0, means both sin x and cos x are positive. And, we know that sin x and cos x both are positive in the first quadrant. Then, f''(x) will be negative when {tex}\mathrm{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} . f''(x) = -sin x - cos x = -(sin x + cos x) Now, let us take x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=-\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=-\sqrt{2}<0{/tex} Then, by second derivative test, f will be maximum at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} And, the maximum value of f is {tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{\pi}{4}+\cos \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let f(x) = sin x + cos x,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = cos x - sin x
Now, f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x - sin x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x = sin x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}, \ldots{/tex} 
Now, 
If f''(x) will be negative when (sin x + cos x) > 0, means both sin x and cos x are positive.
And, we know that sin x and cos x both are positive in the first quadrant.
Then, f''(x) will be negative when {tex}\mathrm{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}.
f''(x) = -sin x - cos x = -(sin x + cos x)
Now, let us take x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=-\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=-\sqrt{2}<0{/tex} 
Then, by second derivative test,
f will be maximum at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} 
And, the maximum value of f is
{tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sin \frac{\pi}{4}+\cos \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the maximum value of {tex}2{x^3} - 24x + 107{/tex} in the interval [1, 3]. Find the maximum value of the same function in {tex}\left[ { - 3, - 1} \right]{/tex}
question_html: Find the maximum value of {tex}2{x^3} - 24x + 107{/tex} in the interval [1, 3]. Find the maximum value of the same function in {tex}\left[ { - 3, - 1} \right]{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}f\left( x \right) = 2{x^3} - 24x + 107{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 6{x^2} - 24{/tex} Now {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 6{x^2} - 24 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x^2} = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = \pm 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = 2{/tex} or {tex}x = - 2{/tex} [Turning points] For Interval [1, 3], x = 2 is turning point. At x = 1, {tex}f\left( 1 \right) = 2\left( 1 \right) - 24\left( 1 \right) + 107 = 85{/tex} At x = 2, {tex}f\left( 2 \right) = 2\left( 8 \right) - 24\left( 2 \right) + 107 = 75{/tex} At x = 3, {tex}f\left( 3 \right) = 2\left( {27} \right) - 24\left( 3 \right) + 107 = 89{/tex} Therefore, maximum value of {tex}f\left( x \right){/tex} is 89. For Interval {tex}\left[ { - 3, - 1} \right],x = - 2{/tex} is turning point. At {tex}x = - 1,{/tex} {tex}f\left( 1 \right) = 2\left( { - 1} \right) - 24\left( { - 1} \right) + 107 = 129{/tex} At {tex}x = - 2,{/tex} {tex}f\left( 2 \right) = 2\left( { - 8} \right) - 24\left( { - 2} \right) + 107 = 139{/tex} At {tex}x = - 3,{/tex} {tex}f\left( 3 \right) = 2\left( { - 27} \right) - 24\left( { - 3} \right) + 107 = 125{/tex} Therefore, maximum value of f(x) is 139.
solution_html:
Let {tex}f\left( x \right) = 2{x^3} - 24x + 107{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 6{x^2} - 24{/tex}

Now {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 6{x^2} - 24 = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow {x^2} = 4{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x = \pm 2{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x = 2{/tex} or {tex}x = - 2{/tex} [Turning points]

For Interval [1, 3], x = 2 is turning point.

At x = 1, {tex}f\left( 1 \right) = 2\left( 1 \right) - 24\left( 1 \right) + 107 = 85{/tex} 

At x = 2, {tex}f\left( 2 \right) = 2\left( 8 \right) - 24\left( 2 \right) + 107 = 75{/tex}

At x = 3, {tex}f\left( 3 \right) = 2\left( {27} \right) - 24\left( 3 \right) + 107 = 89{/tex} 

Therefore, maximum value of {tex}f\left( x \right){/tex} is 89.

For Interval {tex}\left[ { - 3, - 1} \right],x = - 2{/tex} is turning point.

At {tex}x = - 1,{/tex} {tex}f\left( 1 \right) = 2\left( { - 1} \right) - 24\left( { - 1} \right) + 107 = 129{/tex} 

At {tex}x = - 2,{/tex} {tex}f\left( 2 \right) = 2\left( { - 8} \right) - 24\left( { - 2} \right) + 107 = 139{/tex}

At {tex}x = - 3,{/tex} {tex}f\left( 3 \right) = 2\left( { - 27} \right) - 24\left( { - 3} \right) + 107 = 125{/tex} 

Therefore, maximum value of f(x) is 139.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: It is given that at x = 1, the function {tex}{x^4} - 62{x^2} + ax + 9{/tex} attains its maximum value, on the interval [0, 2]. Find the value of a.
question_html: It is given that at x = 1, the function {tex}{x^4} - 62{x^2} + ax + 9{/tex} attains its maximum value, on the interval [0, 2]. Find the value of a.
solution_text: Let f(x) = x 4 - 62 x 2 + ax + 9 {tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 124x + a{/tex} Since, f(x) attains its maximum value at x = 1 in the interval [0, 2], therefore {tex}f'\left( 1 \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore f'\left( 1 \right) = 4 - 124 + a = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a - 120 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a = 120{/tex}
solution_html:
Let f(x) = x4- 62 x2 + ax + 9

{tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 124x + a{/tex}

Since, f(x) attains its maximum value at x = 1 in the interval [0, 2], therefore {tex}f'\left( 1 \right) = 0{/tex} 

{tex}\therefore f'\left( 1 \right) = 4 - 124 + a = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow a - 120 = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow a = 120{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the maximum and minimum values of x + sin 2x on [0, 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ].
question_html: Find the maximum and minimum values of x + sin 2x on [0, 2{tex}\pi{/tex}].
solution_text: It is given that f(x) = x + sin x, {tex}x \in[0,2 \pi]{/tex} f'(x) = 1 + 2cos 2x Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \cos 2 \mathrm{x}=-\frac{1}{2}=-\cos \frac{\pi}{3}=\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}2 x=2 \pi \pm \frac{2 \pi}{3}, n \in Z{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{3}, n \in Z{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2 \pi}{3}, \frac{4 \pi}{3}, \frac{5 \pi}{3} \in[0,2 \pi){/tex} Now, we evaluate the value of f at critical point {tex}\mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2 \pi}{3}, \frac{4 \pi}{3}, \frac{5 \pi}{3}{/tex} and at end points of the interval {tex}[0,2 \pi]{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}+\sin \frac{2 \pi}{3}=\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)=\frac{2 \pi}{3}+\sin \frac{4 \pi}{3}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} , {tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{4 \pi}{3}\right)=\frac{4 \pi}{3}+\sin \frac{8 \pi}{3}=\frac{4 \pi}{3}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{5 \pi}{4}\right)=\frac{5 \pi}{3}+\sin \frac{10 \pi}{3}=\frac{5 \pi}{3}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} f'(0) = 0 + sin 0 = 0 {tex}f^{\prime}(2 \pi)=2 \pi+\sin 4 \pi=2 \pi+0=2 \pi{/tex} Therefore, we have the absolute maximum value of f on {tex}[0,2 \pi]{/tex} is {tex}2 \pi{/tex} occurring at x = {tex}2\pi{/tex} and absolute minimum value of f(x) in the interval {tex}[0,2 \pi]{/tex} is 0 occurring at x = 0.
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = x + sin x, {tex}x \in[0,2 \pi]{/tex} 
f'(x) = 1 + 2cos 2x
Now, f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos 2 \mathrm{x}=-\frac{1}{2}=-\cos \frac{\pi}{3}=\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}2 x=2 \pi \pm \frac{2 \pi}{3}, n \in Z{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x=n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{3}, n \in Z{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2 \pi}{3}, \frac{4 \pi}{3}, \frac{5 \pi}{3} \in[0,2 \pi){/tex} 
Now, we evaluate the value of f at critical point {tex}\mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2 \pi}{3}, \frac{4 \pi}{3}, \frac{5 \pi}{3}{/tex} and at end points of the interval {tex}[0,2 \pi]{/tex} 
{tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}+\sin \frac{2 \pi}{3}=\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)=\frac{2 \pi}{3}+\sin \frac{4 \pi}{3}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex},
{tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{4 \pi}{3}\right)=\frac{4 \pi}{3}+\sin \frac{8 \pi}{3}=\frac{4 \pi}{3}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{5 \pi}{4}\right)=\frac{5 \pi}{3}+\sin \frac{10 \pi}{3}=\frac{5 \pi}{3}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} 
f'(0) = 0 + sin 0 = 0
{tex}f^{\prime}(2 \pi)=2 \pi+\sin 4 \pi=2 \pi+0=2 \pi{/tex} 
Therefore, we have the absolute maximum value of f on {tex}[0,2 \pi]{/tex} is {tex}2 \pi{/tex} occurring at x = {tex}2\pi{/tex} and absolute minimum value of f(x) in the interval {tex}[0,2 \pi]{/tex} is 0 occurring at x = 0.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find two numbers whose sum is 24 and whose product is as large as possible.
question_html: Find two numbers whose sum is 24 and whose product is as large as possible.
solution_text: Let the two numbers be x and y. According to the question, x + y = 24 {tex}\Rightarrow y = 24 - x{/tex} …(i) And let z is the product of x and y. {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} z = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = x(24 - x) [From eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} z = 24x - x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dx}} = 24 - 2x{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{x^2}}} = - 2{/tex} Now to find turning point, {tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 24 - 2x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 12{/tex} At {tex}x = 12,\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{x^2}}} = - 2{/tex} [Negative] {tex}\therefore x = 12{/tex} is a point of local maxima and z is maximum at ​​​​​​​x = 12. {tex}\therefore {/tex} From eq. (i), ​​​​​​​ y = 24 - 12 = 12 Therefore, the two required numbers are 12 and 12.
solution_html:
Let the two numbers be x and y.

According to the question, x + y = 24

{tex}\Rightarrow y = 24 - x{/tex} …(i)

And let z is the product of x and y.

{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}z = xy

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = x(24 - x) [From eq. (i)]

{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}z = 24x - x2

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dx}} = 24 - 2x{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{x^2}}} = - 2{/tex} 

Now to find turning point, {tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 24 - 2x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 12{/tex}

At {tex}x = 12,\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{x^2}}} = - 2{/tex} [Negative]

{tex}\therefore x = 12{/tex} is a point of local maxima and z is maximum at ​​​​​​​x = 12.

{tex}\therefore {/tex}  From eq. (i), ​​​​​​​ y = 24 - 12 = 12

Therefore, the two required numbers are 12 and 12.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find two positive integers x and y such that {tex}x + y = 60{/tex} and {tex}x{y^3}{/tex} is maximum.
question_html: Find two positive integers x and y such that {tex}x + y = 60{/tex} and {tex}x{y^3}{/tex} is maximum.
solution_text: Given: {tex}x + y = 60,x > 0,y > 0{/tex} …(i) Let {tex}P = x{y^3}{/tex} [To be maximized] …(ii) Putting from eq. (i), {tex}x = 60 - y{/tex} in eq. (ii), {tex}P = \left( {60 - y} \right){y^3} = 60{y^3} - {y^4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{{dy}} = 180{y^2} - 4{y^3} = 4{y^2}\left( {45 - y} \right){/tex} …(iii) Now {tex}\frac{{dP}}{{dy}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4{y^2}\left( {45 - y} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = 0,45{/tex} It is clear that {tex}\frac{{dP}}{{dy}}{/tex} changes sign from positive to negative as y increases through 45. Therefore, P is maximum when {tex}y = 45.{/tex} Hence, {tex}x{y^3}{/tex} is maximum when {tex}x = 60 - 45 = 15{/tex} and y = 45.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}x + y = 60,x > 0,y > 0{/tex}…(i)

Let  {tex}P = x{y^3}{/tex} [To be maximized] …(ii)

Putting from eq. (i), {tex}x = 60 - y{/tex} in eq. (ii),

{tex}P = \left( {60 - y} \right){y^3} = 60{y^3} - {y^4}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{{dy}} = 180{y^2} - 4{y^3} = 4{y^2}\left( {45 - y} \right){/tex} …(iii)

Now {tex}\frac{{dP}}{{dy}} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 4{y^2}\left( {45 - y} \right) = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y = 0,45{/tex}

It is clear that {tex}\frac{{dP}}{{dy}}{/tex} changes sign from positive to negative as y increases through 45.

Therefore, P is maximum when {tex}y = 45.{/tex} 

Hence, {tex}x{y^3}{/tex} is maximum when {tex}x = 60 - 45 = 15{/tex} and y = 45.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find two positive numbers x and y such that their sum is 35 and the product x 2 y 5 is a maximum.
question_html: Find two positive numbers x and y such that their sum is 35 and the product x2y5 is a maximum.
solution_text: Let one number be x. Then, the other number is y = (35 - x) Let P(x) = x 2 y 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(x) = x 2 (35 - x) 5 Then, P'(x) = 2x(35 - x) 5 - 5x 2 (35 - x) 4 = x(35 - x) 4 [2(35 - x) - 5x] = x(35 - x) 4 [70 - 7x] = 7x(35x - x) 4 (10 - x) Now, P''(x) = 7(35 - x) 4 (10 - x) + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} [-(35 - x) 4 - 4(35 - x) 3 (10 - x)] = {tex}7(35-x)^{4}(10-x)-7 x(35-x)^{4}-28 x(35-x)^{3}(10-x){/tex} = {tex}7(35-x)^{3}[(35-x)(10-x)-x(35-x)-4 x(10-x)]{/tex} = {tex}7(35-x)^{3}\left[350-45 x+x^{2}-35 x+x^{2}-40 x+4 x^{2}\right]{/tex} = 7(35 - x) 3 [6x 2 - 120x + 350] Now, P'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, 35, 10 When x = 0, 35 This will make the product x 2 y 5 equal to 0. Therefore, x = 0, 35 cannot be possible values of x. And when x = 10 Then, we have, P''(x) = 7(35 - 10)3[6(10)2 - 120(10) + 350] = 7(25)3 [-250] < 0 Then, by second derivative test, x = 10 and y = 35 - 10 = 25 is the point of local maxima of P. Therefore, the required number are 10 and 25.
solution_html:
Let one number be x. Then, the other number is y = (35 - x)
Let P(x) = x2y5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(x) = x2(35 - x)5
Then,
P'(x) = 2x(35 - x)5 - 5x2(35 - x)4
= x(35 - x)4 [2(35 - x) - 5x]
= x(35 - x)4 [70 - 7x]
= 7x(35x - x)4 (10 - x)
Now, P''(x) = 7(35 - x)4 (10 - x) + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} [-(35 - x)4 - 4(35 - x)3 (10 - x)]
= {tex}7(35-x)^{4}(10-x)-7 x(35-x)^{4}-28 x(35-x)^{3}(10-x){/tex} 
= {tex}7(35-x)^{3}[(35-x)(10-x)-x(35-x)-4 x(10-x)]{/tex} 
= {tex}7(35-x)^{3}\left[350-45 x+x^{2}-35 x+x^{2}-40 x+4 x^{2}\right]{/tex} 
= 7(35 - x)3 [6x2 - 120x + 350]
Now, P'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, 35, 10
When x = 0, 35 This will make the product x2y5 equal to 0.
Therefore, x = 0, 35 cannot be possible values of x.
And when x = 10
Then, we have,
P''(x) = 7(35 - 10)3[6(10)2 - 120(10) + 350]
= 7(25)3 [-250] < 0
Then, by second derivative test,
x = 10 and y = 35 - 10 = 25 is the point of local maxima of P.
Therefore, the required number are 10 and 25.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find two positive numbers whose sum is 16 and the sum of whose cubes is minimum.
question_html: Find two positive numbers whose sum is 16 and the sum of whose cubes is minimum.
solution_text: Let one number be x. Then, the other number is (16 - x). Let S(x) be the sum of these number. Then, S(x) = x 3 + (16 - x) 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S'(x) = 3x 2 - 3(16 - x) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S''(x) = 6x + 6(16 - x) Now, S'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 2 - 3(16 - x) 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 - (16 - x) 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 - 256 - x 2 + 32x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 8 Now, S''(8) = 6(8) + 6(16 - 8) = 48 + 48 = 96 > 0 Then, by second derivative test, x = 8 is the point of local minima of S. Therefore, the sum of the cubes of the numbers is the minimum when the numbers are 8 and 16 - 8 = 8.
solution_html:
Let one number be x. Then, the other number is (16 - x).
Let S(x) be the sum of these number. Then,
S(x) = x3 + (16 - x)3 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S'(x) = 3x2 - 3(16 - x)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S''(x) = 6x + 6(16 - x)
Now, S'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 - 3(16 - x)2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - (16 - x)2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 256 - x2 + 32x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 8
Now, S''(8) = 6(8) + 6(16 - 8)
= 48 + 48 = 96 > 0
Then, by second derivative test, x = 8 is the point of local minima of S.
Therefore, the sum of the cubes of the numbers is the minimum when the numbers are 8 and 16 - 8 = 8.
