question_text: A square piece of tin of side 18cm is to be made into a box without top, by cutting a square from each corner and folding up the flaps to form the box. What should be the side of the square to be cut off so that the volume of the box is the maximum possible.
question_html: A square piece of tin of side 18cm is to be made into a box without top, by cutting a square from each corner and folding up the flaps to form the box. What should be the side of the square to be cut off so that the volume of the box is the maximum possible.
solution_text: Let side of square piece cut from the corner is = x l = (18 - 2x)cm b = (18 - 2x)cm h = x cm {tex}v = l \times b \times h{/tex} {tex}v = \left( {18 - 2x} \right) \times \left( {18 - 2x} \right) \times x{/tex} {tex}v = {\left( {18 - 2x} \right)^2} \times x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {\left( {18 - 2x} \right)^2}.\left( 1 \right) + \left( x \right).2\left( {18 - 2x} \right)\left( { - 2} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \left( {18 - 2x} \right)\left[ {\left( {18 - 2x} \right) - 4x} \right]{/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}0 = \left( {18 - 2x} \right)\left( {18 - 6x} \right){/tex} Now 18 - 2x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 9 (neglect) Now 18 - 6x =0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \left( {18 - 2x} \right)(-6) + \left( {18 - 6x} \right)\left( { - 2} \right){/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = 3}} = 12 \times( -6){/tex} = - 72<0 , Hence maximum {tex}l = 18 - 2 \times 3 = 12cm{/tex} b = 12 cm h = 3 cm Side of square piece cut at the corner is 3cm so that the volume of the box become maximum
solution_html:
Let side of square piece cut from the corner is = x

l = (18 - 2x)cm
b = (18 - 2x)cm
h = x cm
{tex}v = l \times b \times h{/tex} 
{tex}v = \left( {18 - 2x} \right) \times \left( {18 - 2x} \right) \times x{/tex} 
{tex}v = {\left( {18 - 2x} \right)^2} \times x{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {\left( {18 - 2x} \right)^2}.\left( 1 \right) + \left( x \right).2\left( {18 - 2x} \right)\left( { - 2} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \left( {18 - 2x} \right)\left[ {\left( {18 - 2x} \right) - 4x} \right]{/tex} 
For maximum/minimum
{tex}0 = \left( {18 - 2x} \right)\left( {18 - 6x} \right){/tex} 
Now 18 - 2x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}x = 9 (neglect)
Now 18 - 6x =0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \left( {18 - 2x} \right)(-6) + \left( {18 - 6x} \right)\left( { - 2} \right){/tex} 
{tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = 3}} = 12 \times( -6){/tex} 
 = - 72<0 , Hence maximum
{tex}l = 18 - 2 \times 3 = 12cm{/tex} 
b = 12 cm
h = 3 cm
Side of square piece cut at the corner is 3cm so that the volume of the box become maximum

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A rectangular sheet of tin 45 cm by 24 cm is to be made into a box without top, by cutting off square from each corner and folding up the flaps. What should be the side of the square to be cut off so that the volume of the box is maximum?
question_html: A rectangular sheet of tin 45 cm by 24 cm is to be made into a box without top, by cutting off square from each corner and folding up the flaps. What should be the side of the square to be cut off so that the volume of the box is maximum?
solution_text: Let the side of the square to be cut off be x, then, the height of the box is x and the length is 45 - 2x and the breadth is 24 - 2x. Then, the volume V(x) of the box is given by: V(x) = x(45 - 2x)(24 - 2x) = x(1080 - 90x - 48x + 4x 2 ) = 4x 3 - 138x 2 + 1080x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}V^\prime{/tex} (x) = 12x 2 - 276x + 1080 = 12(x 2 - 23x + 90) = 12(x - 18)(x - 5) And, {tex}V^\prime{^\prime}{/tex} (x) = 24x - 276 = 12(2x - 23) Now, {tex}V^\prime{/tex} (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 18 or 5 It is not possible to cut off a square of side 18 cm from each corner of the rectangular sheet. So, x cannot be equal to 18. Therefore, x = 5 {tex}V^\prime{^\prime}{/tex} (5) = 12(10 - 23) = -156 < 0 Then, by second derivative test, x = 5 is the point of maxima of V. Therefore, the side of the square to be cut off to make the volume of the box maximum is 5 cm.
solution_html:
Let the side of the square to be cut off be x, then, the height of the box is x and the length is 45 - 2x and the breadth is 24 - 2x.
Then, the volume V(x) of the box is given by:
V(x) = x(45 - 2x)(24 - 2x)
= x(1080 - 90x - 48x + 4x2)
= 4x3 - 138x2 + 1080x
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}V^\prime{/tex}(x) = 12x2 - 276x + 1080
= 12(x2 - 23x + 90)
= 12(x - 18)(x - 5)
And, {tex}V^\prime{^\prime}{/tex}(x) = 24x - 276 = 12(2x - 23)
Now,  {tex}V^\prime{/tex}(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 18 or 5
It is not possible to cut off a square of side 18 cm from each corner of the rectangular sheet. So, x cannot be equal to 18.
Therefore, x = 5
  {tex}V^\prime{^\prime}{/tex}(5) = 12(10 - 23) = -156 < 0
Then, by second derivative test, x = 5 is the point of maxima of V.
Therefore, the side of the square to be cut off to make the volume of the box maximum is 5 cm.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that of all the rectangles inscribed in a given fixed circle, the square has the maximum area.
question_html: Show that of all the rectangles inscribed in a given fixed circle, the square has the maximum area.
solution_text: Let ABCD be the rectangle which is inscribed in a fixed circle whose centre is O and radius b. Let AB = 2x and BC = 2y. In right-angled {tex}\Delta{/tex} OPA by Pythagoras theorem, we have AP 2 + OP 2 = OA 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i) Let A be the area of the rectangle. {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = (2x) (2y) [ {tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A = 4 x y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A = 4 x \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \quad \left[ \because \quad y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 4 x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } + \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( 4 x ){/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d A } { d x } = 4 x \cdot \frac { - 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } + \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \cdot 4{/tex} {tex}= 4 \left[ \frac { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d A } { d x } = 4 \left( \frac { b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad 4 \left( \frac { b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} x cannot be negative] Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d A } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 4 \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ 4 \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 4\left[ { - 4x{{\left( {{b^2} - {x^2}} \right)}^{ - 1/2}} + \left( {{b^2} - 2{x^2}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{2}} \right){{\left( {{b^2} - {x^2}} \right)}^{ - 3/2}}( - 2x)} \right]{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 4 \left[ \frac { - 4 x } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } + \frac { x \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) } { \left( b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} On putting {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 4 \left[ \frac { \frac { - 4 b } { \sqrt { 2 } } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } } + \frac { \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } } \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) } { \left( b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= 4 \left[ \frac { \frac { - 4 b } { \sqrt { 2 } } } { \sqrt { \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } } +0\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 16 < 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } < 0{/tex} . Therefore, A is maximum at {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} Now, on putting {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } = \sqrt { \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad x = y = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } } \Rightarrow 2 x = 2 y = \sqrt { 2 } b{/tex} Thus, area of rectangle is maximum, when 2x = 2y, i.e. when rectangle is a square
solution_html:
Let ABCD be the rectangle which is inscribed in a fixed circle whose centre is O and radius b. Let AB = 2x and BC = 2y.

In right-angled {tex}\Delta{/tex}OPA by Pythagoras theorem,
we have
AP2 + OP2 = OA2
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i)
Let A be the area of the rectangle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A = (2x) (2y) [{tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A = 4 x y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A = 4 x \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \quad \left[ \because \quad y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 4 x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } + \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( 4 x ){/tex} [by using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d A } { d x } = 4 x \cdot \frac { - 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } + \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \cdot 4{/tex}
{tex}= 4 \left[ \frac { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d A } { d x } = 4 \left( \frac { b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right){/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad 4 \left( \frac { b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} x cannot be negative]
Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d A } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 4 \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ 4 \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 4\left[ { - 4x{{\left( {{b^2} - {x^2}} \right)}^{ - 1/2}} + \left( {{b^2} - 2{x^2}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{2}} \right){{\left( {{b^2} - {x^2}} \right)}^{ - 3/2}}( - 2x)} \right]{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 4 \left[ \frac { - 4 x } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } + \frac { x \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) } { \left( b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex}
On putting {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}, we get
{tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 4 \left[ \frac { \frac { - 4 b } { \sqrt { 2 } } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } } + \frac { \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } } \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) } { \left( b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= 4 \left[ \frac { \frac { - 4 b } { \sqrt { 2 } } } { \sqrt { \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } } +0\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 16 < 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } < 0{/tex}. Therefore, A is maximum at {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
Now, on putting {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } = \sqrt { \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad x = y = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } } \Rightarrow 2 x = 2 y = \sqrt { 2 } b{/tex}
Thus, area of rectangle is maximum, when 2x = 2y, i.e. when rectangle is a square

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the right circular cylinder of given surface and maximum volume is such that its height is equal to the diameter of the base.
question_html: Show that the right circular cylinder of given surface and maximum volume is such that its height is equal to the diameter of the base.
solution_text: Let radius of the cylinder = r Height of the cylinder = h| {tex}s = 2\pi rh + 2\pi {r^2}{/tex} (1) {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{s-2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}} = h{/tex} Now volume of cylinder is, {tex}v = \pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \pi .{r^2}\left( {\frac{{s - 2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {sr - 2\pi {r^3}} \right]{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {s - 6\pi {r^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi r} \right]{/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow s = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} From equ (1) {tex}\Rightarrow 2\pi rh + \ 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {r^2} = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} {tex}{\left[ {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}}} \right]_{r = \frac{h}{2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi \times \frac{h}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - 3\pi h < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s is maximum at {tex}r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} Hence h = 2r
solution_html:
Let radius of the cylinder = r
Height of the cylinder = h|

{tex}s = 2\pi rh + 2\pi {r^2}{/tex}     (1)
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{s-2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}} = h{/tex} 
Now volume of cylinder is, {tex}v = \pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \pi .{r^2}\left( {\frac{{s - 2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow v = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {sr - 2\pi {r^3}} \right]{/tex} 
Now, {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {s - 6\pi {r^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi r} \right]{/tex} 
For maximum/minimum
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow s = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} 
From equ (1)
{tex}\Rightarrow 2\pi rh + \ 2{tex}\pi{/tex} {r^2} = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}{\left[ {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}}} \right]_{r = \frac{h}{2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi \times \frac{h}{2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = - 3\pi h < 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s is maximum at {tex}r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} 
Hence h = 2r

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Of all the closed cylindrical cans (right circular) of a given volume of 100 cubic centimetres, find the dimensions of the can which has the minimum surface area.
question_html: Of all the closed cylindrical cans (right circular) of a given volume of 100 cubic centimetres, find the dimensions of the can which has the minimum surface area.
solution_text: Let r be the radius and h be the height of the required cylinder. Let V be the volume of the cylinder. Then V = {tex}\pi r^{2} h{/tex} = 100(given) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=\frac{100}{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} .......(i) Hence, the surface area S (say) of the cylinder is given by: {tex}S=2 \pi r^{2}+2 \pi r h{/tex} = {tex}2 \pi r^{2}+\frac{200}{r}{/tex} , .....(using (i) Now, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{dr}}=4 \pi \mathrm{r}-\frac{200}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}, and \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{dr}^{2}}=4 \pi+\frac{400}{\mathrm{r}^{3}}{/tex} If {tex}\frac{d S}{d r}=0 \Rightarrow 4 \pi r=\frac{200}{r^{2}} \Rightarrow r^{3}=\frac{200}{4 \pi}=\frac{50}{\pi} \Rightarrow r=\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} Further, at, {tex}r=\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} {/tex} we have, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{dr}^{2}}>0{/tex} Then, by second derivative test, the surface area is the minimum when {tex}r=\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} Now, when {tex}r=\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} then {tex}h=\frac{100}{\pi\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}}}=2\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} \mathrm{cm}{/tex} Therefore, the dimensions of the can which has the minimum surface area are {tex}r=\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} and {tex}h=2\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} cm.
solution_html:
Let r be the radius and h be the height of the required cylinder.
Let V be the volume of the cylinder. Then
V = {tex}\pi r^{2} h{/tex} = 100(given)
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=\frac{100}{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex}             .......(i)
Hence, the surface area S (say) of the cylinder is given by:
{tex}S=2 \pi r^{2}+2 \pi r h{/tex}    
= {tex}2 \pi r^{2}+\frac{200}{r}{/tex} ,      .....(using (i)
Now, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{dr}}=4 \pi \mathrm{r}-\frac{200}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}, and \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{dr}^{2}}=4 \pi+\frac{400}{\mathrm{r}^{3}}{/tex} 
If {tex}\frac{d S}{d r}=0 \Rightarrow 4 \pi r=\frac{200}{r^{2}} \Rightarrow r^{3}=\frac{200}{4 \pi}=\frac{50}{\pi} \Rightarrow r=\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} 
Further, at, {tex}r=\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} {/tex}    we have, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{dr}^{2}}>0{/tex} 
Then, by second derivative test, the surface area is the minimum when   {tex}r=\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} 
Now, when {tex}r=\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} then {tex}h=\frac{100}{\pi\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}}}=2\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} \mathrm{cm}{/tex} 
Therefore, the dimensions of the can which has the minimum surface area are {tex}r=\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} and {tex}h=2\left(\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} cm.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A wire of length 28m is to be cut into two pieces. One of the pieces is to be made into a square and the other into a circle. What should be the length of the two pieces so that the combined areas of the square and the circle is minimum?
question_html: A wire of length 28m is to be cut into two pieces. One of the pieces is to be made into a square and the other into a circle. What should be the length of the two pieces so that the combined areas of the square and the circle is minimum?
solution_text: Let 1 st length = x 2 nd length = 28 - x Now circumference of circle is 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2\pi r = x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}r = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} Now perimeter of rectangle = 4a {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}4a = 28 - x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a = 7 - \frac{x}{4}{/tex} ATQ A = area of circle + area of square {tex}\pi {\left( {\frac{x}{{2\pi }}} \right)^2} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} Now, {tex}A = \pi .\frac{{{x^2}}}{{4{\pi ^2}}} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right) = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 - \frac{x}{4} = \frac{x}{\pi }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 = \frac{x}{\pi } + \frac{x}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 = x\left( {\frac{{4 + \pi }}{{4\pi }}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }} = x{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi }} - \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{2\pi }} + \frac{1}{8}{/tex} positive, hence minimum Therefore, 1 st length {tex}= \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} 2 nd length {tex}= \frac{{28}}{1} - \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} {tex}= 28\left[ {\frac{{4 + \pi - \pi }}{{4 + \pi }}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{112}}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let 1st length = x
2nd length = 28 - x

Now circumference of circle is 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2\pi r = x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}r = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} 
Now perimeter of rectangle = 4a
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}4a = 28 - x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow a = 7 - \frac{x}{4}{/tex} 
ATQ
A = area of circle + area of square
{tex}\pi {\left( {\frac{x}{{2\pi }}} \right)^2} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
Now, {tex}A = \pi .\frac{{{x^2}}}{{4{\pi ^2}}} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
So,{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right) = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 - \frac{x}{4} = \frac{x}{\pi }{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}7 = \frac{x}{\pi } + \frac{x}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}7 = x\left( {\frac{{4 + \pi }}{{4\pi }}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }} = x{/tex} 
Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi }} - \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{4}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{{2\pi }} + \frac{1}{8}{/tex} 
positive, hence minimum
Therefore, 1st length {tex}= \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} 
2nd length {tex}= \frac{{28}}{1} - \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} 
{tex}= 28\left[ {\frac{{4 + \pi - \pi }}{{4 + \pi }}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{{112}}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the volume of the largest cone that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius R is {tex}\frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} of the volume of the sphere.
question_html: Prove that the volume of the largest cone that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius R is {tex}\frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} of the volume of the sphere.
solution_text: {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{r^2} = \sqrt {{R^2} - {x^2}} } \right]{/tex} {tex}V = \frac{1}{2}\pi .\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right).\left( {R + x} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {R + x} \right)( - 2x)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + x} \right)\left( {R - x} \right) - 2x\left( {R + x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {R + x} \right)\left[ {R - x - 2x} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {R + x} \right)(R - 3x){/tex} ....(1) Put {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} R = - x (neglecting) R = 3x {tex}\frac{R}{3} = x{/tex} On again differentiating equation (1) {tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {(R + x)( - 3) + (R - 3x)(1)} \right]{/tex} = {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{R}{3}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + \frac{R}{3}} \right)( - 3) + \left( {R - 3.\frac{R}{3}} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\frac{{4R}}{3} \times - 3 + 0} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - 1}}{3}\pi 4R{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} < 0{/tex} Hence maximum Now {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( {R + x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {x = \frac{R}{3}} \right]{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {{\left( {\frac{R}{3}} \right)}^2}} \right)\left( {R + \left( {\frac{R}{3}} \right)} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\frac{{8{R^2}}}{9} \times \frac{{4R}}{3}} \right]{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{8}{{27}}\left( {\frac{4}{3}} \right)\pi {R^3}{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} Volume of sphere Volume of cone {tex} = \frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} of volume of sphere.
solution_html:
{tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{r^2} = \sqrt {{R^2} - {x^2}} } \right]{/tex}
{tex}V = \frac{1}{2}\pi .\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right).\left( {R + x} \right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {R + x} \right)( - 2x)} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + x} \right)\left( {R - x} \right) - 2x\left( {R + x} \right)} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {R + x} \right)\left[ {R - x - 2x} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {R + x} \right)(R - 3x){/tex}....(1)
Put {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex}
R = - x (neglecting)
R = 3x
{tex}\frac{R}{3} = x{/tex}
On again differentiating equation (1)
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {(R + x)( - 3) + (R - 3x)(1)} \right]{/tex}
={tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{R}{3}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + \frac{R}{3}} \right)( - 3) + \left( {R - 3.\frac{R}{3}} \right)} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\frac{{4R}}{3} \times - 3 + 0} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{ - 1}}{3}\pi 4R{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} < 0{/tex} Hence maximum
Now {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( {R + x} \right)} \right]{/tex}{tex}\left[ {x = \frac{R}{3}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {{\left( {\frac{R}{3}} \right)}^2}} \right)\left( {R + \left( {\frac{R}{3}} \right)} \right)} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\frac{{8{R^2}}}{9} \times \frac{{4R}}{3}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}v = \frac{8}{{27}}\left( {\frac{4}{3}} \right)\pi {R^3}{/tex}
{tex}v = \frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} Volume of sphere
Volume of cone {tex} = \frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} of volume of sphere.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the right circular cone of least curved surface and given volume has an altitude equal to {tex}\sqrt 2 {/tex} times the radius of the base.
question_html: Show that the right circular cone of least curved surface and given volume has an altitude equal to {tex}\sqrt 2 {/tex} times the radius of the base.
solution_text: Volume of the cone is: {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi {r^2}h{/tex} Let {tex}{r^2}h = k{/tex} Where k is constant {tex}{r^2}h = k{/tex} {tex}h = \frac{k}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} {tex}s = \pi rl{/tex} {tex}{s^2} = {\pi ^2}{r^2}{l^2}{/tex} {tex} = {\pi ^2}.{r^2}({r^2} + {h^2}){/tex} {tex} = {\pi ^2}.{r^2}\left[ {{r^2} + \frac{{{k^2}}}{{{r^4}}}} \right]\left[ {\because h = \frac{k}{{{r^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}z = {\pi ^2}{r^4} + {\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = 4{\pi ^2}{r^3} - 2{\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 3}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{r^2}}} = 12{\pi ^2}{r^2} + 6{\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 4}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex}4{\pi ^2}{r^3} - \frac{{2{\pi ^2}{k^2}}}{{{r^3}}} = 0{/tex} {tex}4{\pi ^2}{r^6} - 2{\pi ^2}{k^2} = 0{/tex} {tex}2{\pi ^2}{k^2} = 4{\pi ^2}{r^6}{/tex} {tex}{k^2} = 2{r^6}{/tex} Put {tex}k = {r^2}h{/tex} we get {tex}{r^4}{h^2} = 2{r^6}{/tex} {tex}{h^2} = 2{r^2}{/tex} {tex}h = \sqrt 2 r{/tex} {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{r^2}}}} \right)_{h = \sqrt 2 r}}{/tex} is negative z is maximum at {tex}h = \sqrt 2 r{/tex}
solution_html:
Volume of the cone is: {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
Let {tex}{r^2}h = k{/tex} 
Where k is constant
{tex}{r^2}h = k{/tex} 
{tex}h = \frac{k}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} 
{tex}s = \pi rl{/tex} 
{tex}{s^2} = {\pi ^2}{r^2}{l^2}{/tex} 
{tex} = {\pi ^2}.{r^2}({r^2} + {h^2}){/tex} 
{tex} = {\pi ^2}.{r^2}\left[ {{r^2} + \frac{{{k^2}}}{{{r^4}}}} \right]\left[ {\because h = \frac{k}{{{r^2}}}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}z = {\pi ^2}{r^4} + {\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = 4{\pi ^2}{r^3} - 2{\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 3}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{r^2}}} = 12{\pi ^2}{r^2} + 6{\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 4}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}4{\pi ^2}{r^3} - \frac{{2{\pi ^2}{k^2}}}{{{r^3}}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}4{\pi ^2}{r^6} - 2{\pi ^2}{k^2} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}2{\pi ^2}{k^2} = 4{\pi ^2}{r^6}{/tex} 
{tex}{k^2} = 2{r^6}{/tex} 
Put {tex}k = {r^2}h{/tex} we get
{tex}{r^4}{h^2} = 2{r^6}{/tex} 
{tex}{h^2} = 2{r^2}{/tex} 
{tex}h = \sqrt 2 r{/tex} 
{tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{r^2}}}} \right)_{h = \sqrt 2 r}}{/tex} is negative
z is maximum at {tex}h = \sqrt 2 r{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the semi-vertical angle of the cone of the maximum value and of given slant height is {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt 2{/tex}
question_html: Show that the semi-vertical angle of the cone of the maximum value and of given slant height is {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt 2{/tex}
solution_text: Let x be the radius, y be the height, l be the slant height of given cone and {tex}\theta {/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of cone. {tex}\therefore {l^2} = {x^2} + {y^2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = {l^2} - {y^2}{/tex} …(i) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of the cone {tex}\left( V \right) = \frac{1}{3}\pi {x^2}y{/tex} …(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow V = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {{l^2} - {y^2}} \right)y{/tex} {tex}= \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2}y - {y^3}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dy}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2} - 3{y^2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{y^2}}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\left( { - 6y} \right) = - 2\pi y{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dy}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2} - 3{y^2}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {l^2} - 3{y^2} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3{y^2} = {l^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac{l}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} At {tex}y = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} = - 2\pi \left( {\frac{l}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - 2\pi l}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} [Negative] {tex}\therefore {/tex} V is maximum at {tex}y = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}{x^2} = {l^2} - \frac{{{l^2}}}{3} = \frac{{2{l^2}}}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = \sqrt 2 \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Semi-vertical angle, {tex}\tan \theta = \frac{x}{y}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\sqrt 2 \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}}{{\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = \sqrt 2 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt 2 {/tex}
solution_html:
Let x be the radius, y be the height, l be the slant height of given cone and {tex}\theta {/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of cone.

{tex}\therefore {l^2} = {x^2} + {y^2}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = {l^2} - {y^2}{/tex} …(i)

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of the cone {tex}\left( V \right) = \frac{1}{3}\pi {x^2}y{/tex} …(ii)

{tex} \Rightarrow V = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {{l^2} - {y^2}} \right)y{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2}y - {y^3}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dy}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2} - 3{y^2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{y^2}}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\left( { - 6y} \right) = - 2\pi y{/tex} 

Now {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dy}} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2} - 3{y^2}} \right) = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {l^2} - 3{y^2} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 3{y^2} = {l^2}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac{l}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

At {tex}y = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} = - 2\pi \left( {\frac{l}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{ - 2\pi l}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} [Negative]

{tex}\therefore {/tex}  V is maximum at {tex}y = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex}  From eq. (i), {tex}{x^2} = {l^2} - \frac{{{l^2}}}{3} = \frac{{2{l^2}}}{3}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x = \sqrt 2 \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex}  Semi-vertical angle, {tex}\tan \theta = \frac{x}{y}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\sqrt 2 \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}}{{\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = \sqrt 2 {/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt 2 {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that semi – vertical angle of right circular cone of given surface area and maximum volume is {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right){/tex}
question_html: Show that semi – vertical angle of right circular cone of given surface area and maximum volume is {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right){/tex}
solution_text: {tex}s = \pi {r^2} + \pi rl{/tex} (given) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}l = \frac{{s - \pi {r^2}}}{{\pi r}}{/tex} Let v be the volume {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{v^2} = \frac{1}{9}{\pi ^2}{r^4}{h^2}\,\,\left[ {{h^2} = {l^2} - {r^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{v^2} = \frac{1}{9}{\pi ^2}{r^4}\,\left( {{l^2} - {r^2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{v^2} = \frac{1}{9}{\pi ^2}{r^4}\left[ {\,{{\left( {\frac{{s - \pi {r^2}}}{{\pi r}}} \right)}^2} - {r^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{9}{\pi ^2}{r^4}\left[ {\frac{{\left( {s - \pi {r^2}} \right)}^2}{{{\pi ^2}{r^2}}} - \frac{{{r^2}}}{1}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{9}{r^2}\left[ {{{\left( {s - \pi {r^2}} \right)}^2} - {\pi ^2}{r^4}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{9}{r^2}\left[ {{s^2} + {\pi ^2}{r^4} - 2s\pi {r^2} - {\pi ^2}{r^4}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{9}{r^2}\left[ {{s^2} - 2s\pi {r^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}z = \frac{1}{9}\left[ {{s^2}{r^2} - 2s\pi {r^4}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {v^2} = z} \right]{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = \frac{1}{9}\,\left[ {2r{s^2} - 8s\pi {r^3}} \right]{/tex} {tex}0 = \frac{1}{9}\,\left[ {2r{s^2} - 8s\pi {r^3}} \right]{/tex} {tex}8s\pi {r^2} = 2r{s^2}{/tex} {tex}\implies{/tex} {tex}4\pi {r^2} = s{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{9}\,\left[ {2{s^2} - 24s\pi {r^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{{r^2} = \frac{s}{{4\pi }}}} = \frac{1}{9}\,\left[ {{{25}^2} - 24\pi .\frac{5}{{4\pi }}} \right]{/tex} = + ve Hence minimum Now {tex}s = 4\pi {r^2}{/tex} We have {tex}s = \pi rl + \pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex}4\pi {r^2} = \pi rl + \pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3\pi {r^2} = \pi rl{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 r = l {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{r}{l} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sin \alpha = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}s = \pi {r^2} + \pi rl{/tex} (given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}l = \frac{{s - \pi {r^2}}}{{\pi r}}{/tex} 
Let v be the volume
{tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}{v^2} = \frac{1}{9}{\pi ^2}{r^4}{h^2}\,\,\left[ {{h^2} = {l^2} - {r^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}{v^2} = \frac{1}{9}{\pi ^2}{r^4}\,\left( {{l^2} - {r^2}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}{v^2} = \frac{1}{9}{\pi ^2}{r^4}\left[ {\,{{\left( {\frac{{s - \pi {r^2}}}{{\pi r}}} \right)}^2} - {r^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{9}{\pi ^2}{r^4}\left[ {\frac{{\left( {s - \pi {r^2}} \right)}^2}{{{\pi ^2}{r^2}}} - \frac{{{r^2}}}{1}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{9}{r^2}\left[ {{{\left( {s - \pi {r^2}} \right)}^2} - {\pi ^2}{r^4}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{9}{r^2}\left[ {{s^2} + {\pi ^2}{r^4} - 2s\pi {r^2} - {\pi ^2}{r^4}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{9}{r^2}\left[ {{s^2} - 2s\pi {r^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}z = \frac{1}{9}\left[ {{s^2}{r^2} - 2s\pi {r^4}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\left[ {\because {v^2} = z} \right]{/tex} 
Now {tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = \frac{1}{9}\,\left[ {2r{s^2} - 8s\pi {r^3}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}0 = \frac{1}{9}\,\left[ {2r{s^2} - 8s\pi {r^3}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}8s\pi {r^2} = 2r{s^2}{/tex} 
 {tex}\implies{/tex} {tex}4\pi {r^2} = s{/tex} 
Now  {tex}\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{9}\,\left[ {2{s^2} - 24s\pi {r^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{{r^2} = \frac{s}{{4\pi }}}} = \frac{1}{9}\,\left[ {{{25}^2} - 24\pi .\frac{5}{{4\pi }}} \right]{/tex} 
= + ve
Hence minimum
Now {tex}s = 4\pi {r^2}{/tex} 
We have {tex}s = \pi rl + \pi {r^2}{/tex} 
{tex}4\pi {r^2} = \pi rl + \pi {r^2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3\pi {r^2} = \pi rl{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}3 r = l
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\frac{r}{l} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\sin \alpha = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\alpha = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The point on the curve x 2 = 2y which is nearest to the point (0, 5) is
question_html: The point on the curve x2 = 2y which is nearest to the point (0, 5) is

options.explanation: It is given that x 2 = 2y For each value of x, the position of the point will be {tex}\left(x, \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right){/tex} The distance d(x) between the points {tex}\left(x, \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right){/tex} and (0, 5) is given by: {tex}d(x)=\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}-5\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{x^{2}+\frac{x^{2}}{4}+25-5 x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{x^{4}}{4}-4 x^{2}+25}{/tex} {tex}\therefore~~~ \mathrm{d}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\left(\mathrm{x}^{3}-8 \mathrm{x}\right)}{2 \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{x}^{4}}{4}-4 \mathrm{x}^{2}+25}}{/tex} d'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 3 - 8x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x 2 - 8) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, {tex}\pm 2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} And, {tex}\mathrm{d}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{4}-16 \mathrm{x}^{2}+100}\left(3 \mathrm{x}^{2}-8\right)-\left(\mathrm{x}^{3}-8 \mathrm{x}\right) \cdot \frac{4 \mathrm{x}^{3}-32 \mathrm{x}}{2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{4}-16 \mathrm{x}^{2}+100}}}{\mathrm{x}^{4}-16 \mathrm{x}^{2}+100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{4}-16 x^{2}+100\right)\left(3 x^{2}-8\right)-2\left(x^{3}-8 x\right)\left(x^{3}-8 x\right)}{\left(x^{4}-16 x^{2}+100\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} So, now when x = 0, then d''(x) = {tex}\frac{36(-8)}{6^{3}}<0{/tex} And when, {tex}x=\pm 2 \sqrt{2},~~~ \mathrm{d}^{\prime \prime}(x)>0{/tex} Then, by second derivative test, d(x) is minimum at {tex}x=\pm 2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} So, when {tex}x=\pm 2 \sqrt{2},~~~ y=\frac{(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}}{2}=4{/tex} Therefore, the point on the curve {tex}x^2 = 2y{/tex} , which is nearest to the point (0, 5) is {tex}(\pm 2 \sqrt{2}, 4){/tex} .

subject: Mathematics
question_text: For all real values of x, the minimum value of {tex}\frac{1-x+x^{2}}{1+x+x^{2}}{/tex} is
question_html: For all real values of x, the minimum value of {tex}\frac{1-x+x^{2}}{1+x+x^{2}}{/tex} is

options.explanation: Let f(x) = {tex}\frac{1-x+x^{2}}{1+x+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\left(1+\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)(-1+2 \mathrm{x})-\left(1-\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)(1+2 \mathrm{x})}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1+2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}^{2}+2 \mathrm{x}^{3}-1-2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}^{2}-2 \mathrm{x}^{3}}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 x^{2}-2}{\left(1+x+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{\left(1+x+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Then, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 1 Now, {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{2\left[\left(1+\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{2}(2 \mathrm{x})-\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-1\right)(2)\left(1+\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)(1+2 \mathrm{x})\right]}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{4}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4\left(1+x+x^{2}\right)\left[\left(1+x+x^{2}\right) x-\left(x^{2}-1\right)(1+2 x)\right]}{\left(1+x+x^{2}\right)^{4}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4\left[\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{x}^{3}-\mathrm{x}^{2}-2 \mathrm{x}^{3}+1+2 \mathrm{x}\right]}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4\left[1+2 x-x^{3}\right]}{\left(1+x+x^{2}\right)^{3}}{/tex} And {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(1)=\frac{4\left[1+2(1)-(1)^{3}\right]}{\left(1+1+1^{2}\right)^{3}}=\frac{4[3]}{(3)^{3}}=\frac{4}{9}>0{/tex} Also, f''(-1) = -4 < 0 Then, by second derivative test, f is minimum at x = 1 and the minimum value is given by {tex}f(1)=\frac{1-1+1}{1+1+1}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The maximum value of {tex}[x(x-1)+1]^{\frac{1}{3}}, 0 \leq x \leq 1{/tex} is
question_html: The maximum value of {tex}[x(x-1)+1]^{\frac{1}{3}}, 0 \leq x \leq 1{/tex} is

options.explanation: Let f(x) = {tex}[x(x-1)+1]^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{2 x-1}{3[[x(x-1)+1]]^{\frac{2}{3}}}{/tex} Now, if f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Then, we evaluate the value of f at critical point x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and at the end points of the interval [0, 1]. f(0) = {tex}[0(0-1)+1]^{\frac{1}{3}}=1{/tex} f(1) = {tex}[1(1-1)+1]^{\frac{1}{3}}=1{/tex} {tex}f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}-1\right)+1\right]^{\frac{1}{3}}=\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} Therefore, we can conclude that the maximum value of f in the interval [0, 1] is 1.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function given by f(x) = {tex}\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} has maximum at x = e.
question_html: Show that the function given by f(x) = {tex}\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} has maximum at x = e.
solution_text: It is given that f(x) = {tex}\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} Then, f'(x) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)-\log \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}=\frac{1-\log \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{/tex} Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - log x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log x = log e {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = e Further, {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{x^{2}\left(-\frac{1}{x}\right)-(1-\log x)(2 x)}{x^{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-x-2 x(1-\log x)}{x^{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-3+2 \log x}{x^{3}}{/tex} Now, {tex}f^\prime{^\prime}{/tex} (e) = {tex}\frac{-3+2 \log e}{e^{3}}=\frac{-3+2}{e^{3}}=\frac{-1}{e^{3}}<0{/tex} Therefore, by second derivative test, f is the maximum at x = e.
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = {tex}\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} 
Then, f'(x) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)-\log \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}=\frac{1-\log \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{/tex} 
Now, f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - log x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log x = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log x = log e
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = e
Further, {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{x^{2}\left(-\frac{1}{x}\right)-(1-\log x)(2 x)}{x^{4}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{-x-2 x(1-\log x)}{x^{4}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{-3+2 \log x}{x^{3}}{/tex} 
Now, {tex}f^\prime{^\prime}{/tex}(e) = {tex}\frac{-3+2 \log e}{e^{3}}=\frac{-3+2}{e^{3}}=\frac{-1}{e^{3}}<0{/tex} 
Therefore, by second derivative test, f is the maximum at x = e.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The two equal sides of an isosceles {tex}\triangle{/tex} with fixed base b are decreasing at the rate of 3 cm/s. How fast is the area decreasing when the two equal sides are equal to the base?
question_html: The two equal sides of an isosceles {tex}\triangle{/tex} with fixed base b are decreasing at the rate of 3 cm/s. How fast is the area decreasing when the two equal sides are equal to the base?
solution_text: Let at any time t, the length of each equal side be x cm and area of triangle be A.Then. {tex}A = \frac{1}{2}BC \times AD = \frac{1}{2}b\sqrt {{x^2} - \frac{{{b^2}}}{4}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{b}{4}\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \frac{b}{4} \times \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }} \times 8x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{bx}}{{\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \frac{{3bx}}{{\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }}\left( {\because \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3cm/\sec } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{dA}}{{dt}}} \right)_{x = b}} = \frac{{3{b^2}}}{{\sqrt {4{b^2} - {b^2}} }} = \sqrt 3 b{/tex} cm 2 /sec
solution_html:
Let at any time t, the length of each equal side be x cm and area of triangle be A.Then.
{tex}A = \frac{1}{2}BC \times AD = \frac{1}{2}b\sqrt {{x^2} - \frac{{{b^2}}}{4}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{b}{4}\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \frac{b}{4} \times \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }} \times 8x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{bx}}{{\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \frac{{3bx}}{{\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }}\left( {\because \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3cm/\sec } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{dA}}{{dt}}} \right)_{x = b}} = \frac{{3{b^2}}}{{\sqrt {4{b^2} - {b^2}} }} = \sqrt 3 b{/tex} cm2/sec

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by {tex}f\left( x \right) = \frac{{4\sin x - 2x - x\cos x}}{{2 + \cos x}}{/tex} is increasing decreasing
question_html:
Find the intervals in which the function f given by {tex}f\left( x \right) = \frac{{4\sin x - 2x - x\cos x}}{{2 + \cos x}}{/tex} is

  1. increasing
  2. decreasing
solution_text: Given function is, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \frac{{4\sin x - 2x - x\cos x}}{{2 + \cos x}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{4\sin x - x\left( {2 + \cos x} \right)}}{{2 + \cos x}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{4\sin x}}{{2 + \cos x}} - x{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Now, {tex}f'(x)=4\left({\frac{(2+cosx)cosx-sinx(0-sinx)}{(2+cosx)^2}}\right)-1{/tex} {tex}=4\left({\frac{2cosx+cos^2x+sin^2x}{(2+cosx)^2}}\right)-1{/tex} {tex}=4\left({\frac{8cosx+4-(2+cosx)^2}{(2+cosx)^2}}\right){/tex} {tex}f'(x)=4\left({\frac{cosx(4-cosx)}{(2+cosx)^2}}\right){/tex} {tex}{since\ - 1 \leqslant \cos x \leqslant 1} {/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{{\cos x\left( {4 - \cos x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos x} \right)}^2}}} > 0\forall x \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{2},2\pi } \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{\cos x\left( {4 - \cos x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos x} \right)}^2}}} < 0\forall \in \left( {\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{{3\pi }}{2}} \right){/tex} So, f(x) is increasing in {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{2},2\pi } \right){/tex} and if f(x) is decreasing in {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{{3\pi }}{2}} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Given function is, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \frac{{4\sin x - 2x - x\cos x}}{{2 + \cos x}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{4\sin x - x\left( {2 + \cos x} \right)}}{{2 + \cos x}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{4\sin x}}{{2 + \cos x}} - x{/tex}
{tex}{/tex}Now,
{tex}f'(x)=4\left({\frac{(2+cosx)cosx-sinx(0-sinx)}{(2+cosx)^2}}\right)-1{/tex}
{tex}=4\left({\frac{2cosx+cos^2x+sin^2x}{(2+cosx)^2}}\right)-1{/tex}

{tex}=4\left({\frac{8cosx+4-(2+cosx)^2}{(2+cosx)^2}}\right){/tex}{tex}f'(x)=4\left({\frac{cosx(4-cosx)}{(2+cosx)^2}}\right){/tex}

{tex}{since\ - 1 \leqslant \cos x \leqslant 1} {/tex}
Hence
{tex}\frac{{\cos x\left( {4 - \cos x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos x} \right)}^2}}} > 0\forall x \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{2},2\pi } \right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{{\cos x\left( {4 - \cos x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos x} \right)}^2}}} < 0\forall \in \left( {\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{{3\pi }}{2}} \right){/tex}
So, f(x) is increasing in {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{2},2\pi } \right){/tex}
and if f(x) is decreasing in {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{{3\pi }}{2}} \right){/tex}

question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = {tex} x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 3 } } , x \neq 0{/tex} is x (i) increasing. (ii) decreasing.
question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = {tex} x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 3 } } , x \neq 0{/tex} is x (i) increasing. (ii) decreasing.
solution_text: f(x) = {tex} x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 3 } } , x \neq 0{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, f'(x) = {tex} 3 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} On putting f'(x) = 0, we get, {tex} 3 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { x ^ { 4 } } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 3 x ^ { 6 } - 3 } { x ^ { 4 } } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 6 - 3 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 6 = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x 6 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex} \pm{/tex} 1 Now, we find intervals in whch f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) = {tex} \frac { 3 x ^ { 6 } - 3 } { x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} Sign of f'(x) x<-1 {tex}\frac { ( + ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} +ve -1<x<1,x {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 {tex}\frac { ( - ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} -ve x > 1 {tex}\frac { ( + ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} +v So, f(x) is strictly increasing on intervals (- {tex} \infty{/tex} , -1) and (1, {tex} \infty{/tex} ) and it is strictly decreasing on (-1, 1)-{0}. Also, f(x) is continuous at x = 1 , -1. Hence, f(x) is increasig on intervals (- {tex} \infty{/tex} , -1 ] and [1, {tex} \infty{/tex} ). decreasing on interval [-1, 1]- {0}.
solution_html:
f(x) = {tex} x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 3 } } , x \neq 0{/tex} 

On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
f'(x) = {tex} 3 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { x ^ { 4 } }{/tex}
On putting f'(x) = 0, we get,
{tex} 3 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { x ^ { 4 } } = 0{/tex}{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 3 x ^ { 6 } - 3 } { x ^ { 4 } } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3x6 - 3 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}3x6 = 3
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x6 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex} \pm{/tex}1
Now, we find intervals in whch f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.

Interval f'(x) ={tex} \frac { 3 x ^ { 6 } - 3 } { x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} Sign of f'(x)
x<-1 {tex}\frac { ( + ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} +ve
-1<x<1,x{tex}\ne{/tex}0 {tex}\frac { ( - ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} -ve
x > 1 {tex}\frac { ( + ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} +v

So, f(x) is strictly increasing on intervals (-{tex} \infty{/tex}, -1) and (1, {tex} \infty{/tex}) and it is strictly decreasing on (-1, 1)-{0}.
 Also, f(x) is continuous at x = 1 , -1.
 Hence, f(x) is

  1. increasig on intervals (-{tex} \infty{/tex}, -1 ] and [1, {tex} \infty{/tex}).
  2. decreasing on interval [-1, 1]- {0}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the maximum area of an isosceles {tex}\triangle {/tex} inscribed in the ellipse {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1{/tex} with its vertex at one end of the major axis
question_html: Find the maximum area of an isosceles {tex}\triangle {/tex} inscribed in the ellipse {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1{/tex} with its vertex at one end of the major axis
solution_text: Let A be the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC {tex}A = \frac{1}{2}(2b\sin \theta ) \times (a - a\cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}A = ab(\sin \theta - \sin \theta .\cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{d\theta }}{/tex} = ab[cos {tex}\theta{/tex} the perimter(b) the area of the rectangle. cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ] {tex} = ab\left[ {\cos \theta - \cos 2\theta } \right]{/tex} For maximum / minimum {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{d\theta }} = 0{/tex} {tex}ab(\cos \theta - \cos 2\theta ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta = \cos 2\theta {/tex} {tex}\cos \theta = \cos (2\pi - 2\theta ){/tex} {tex}\theta = 2\pi - 2\theta {/tex} {tex}3\theta = 2\pi {/tex} {tex}\theta = \frac{{2\pi }}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = ab\left( { - \sin \theta + 2\sin 2\theta } \right){/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{\theta ^2}}}} \right]_{\theta = \frac{{2\pi }}{3}}} = ab\left[ { - \sin \frac{{2\pi }}{3} + 2\sin 2.\frac{{2\pi }}{3}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = ab\left[ { - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} - 2 \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right] < 0{/tex} So,area is maximum {tex}A = ab\left[ {\sin \frac{{2\pi }}{3} - \sin \frac{{2\pi }}{3}\cos \frac{{2\pi }}{3}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = ab\left[ {\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} + \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{3\sqrt 3 }}{4}ab.{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A be the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC
{tex}A = \frac{1}{2}(2b\sin \theta ) \times (a - a\cos \theta ){/tex} 
{tex}A = ab(\sin \theta - \sin \theta .\cos \theta ){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{d\theta }}{/tex} = ab[cos {tex}\theta{/tex} the perimter(b) the area of the rectangle. cos2{tex}\theta{/tex} + sin2{tex}\theta{/tex}]
{tex} = ab\left[ {\cos \theta - \cos 2\theta } \right]{/tex} 
For maximum / minimum
{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{d\theta }} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}ab(\cos \theta - \cos 2\theta ) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\cos \theta = \cos 2\theta {/tex} 
{tex}\cos \theta = \cos (2\pi - 2\theta ){/tex} 
{tex}\theta = 2\pi - 2\theta {/tex} 
{tex}3\theta = 2\pi {/tex} 
{tex}\theta = \frac{{2\pi }}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = ab\left( { - \sin \theta + 2\sin 2\theta } \right){/tex} 
{tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{\theta ^2}}}} \right]_{\theta = \frac{{2\pi }}{3}}} = ab\left[ { - \sin \frac{{2\pi }}{3} + 2\sin 2.\frac{{2\pi }}{3}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = ab\left[ { - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} - 2 \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right] < 0{/tex}  
So,area is maximum
{tex}A = ab\left[ {\sin \frac{{2\pi }}{3} - \sin \frac{{2\pi }}{3}\cos \frac{{2\pi }}{3}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = ab\left[ {\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} + \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{{3\sqrt 3 }}{4}ab.{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A tank with rectangular base and rectangular sides, open at the top is to be constructed so that its depth is 2 m and volume is 8 m 3 . If building of tank costs Rs 70 per sq.metres for the base and Rs 45 per sq. metre for sides. What is the cost of least expensive tank?
question_html: A tank with rectangular base and rectangular sides, open at the top is to be constructed so that its depth is 2 m and volume is 8 m3. If building of tank costs Rs 70 per sq.metres for the base and Rs 45 per sq. metre for sides. What is the cost of least expensive tank?
solution_text: Let x and y be the length and width of rectangular base, v be the volume. v = 8 (Given) v = 2xy 8 = 2xy {tex}y = \frac{4}{x}{/tex} {tex}s = (xy) \times 70 + 2(x + y) \times 45{/tex} {tex} = x \times \frac{4}{x} \times 70 + 90\left( {x + \frac{4}{x}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 280 + 90\left( {x + \frac{4}{x}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0 + 90\left( {1 - \frac{4}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = 90\left( {0 + \frac{8}{{{x^3}}}} \right){/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} x = 2 {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 2}}=\frac{{720}}{{{2^3}}} > 0{/tex} s is Minimum at x = 2 Minimum cost is given by {tex}s = 280 + 90\left( {2 + \frac{4}{2}} \right){/tex} = 280 + 90 (4) = 280 + 360 = 640
solution_html:
Let x and y be the length and width of rectangular base, v be the volume.
v = 8 (Given)
v = 2xy
8 = 2xy
{tex}y = \frac{4}{x}{/tex}
{tex}s = (xy) \times 70 + 2(x + y) \times 45{/tex}
{tex} = x \times \frac{4}{x} \times 70 + 90\left( {x + \frac{4}{x}} \right){/tex}
{tex} = 280 + 90\left( {x + \frac{4}{x}} \right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0 + 90\left( {1 - \frac{4}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex}
={tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = 90\left( {0 + \frac{8}{{{x^3}}}} \right){/tex}
For maximum/minimum
{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}
x = 2
{tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 2}}=\frac{{720}}{{{2^3}}} > 0{/tex}
s is Minimum at x = 2
Minimum cost is given by
{tex}s = 280 + 90\left( {2 + \frac{4}{2}} \right){/tex}
= 280 + 90 (4)
= 280 + 360
= 640

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The sum of the perimeter of a circle and square is k, where k is some constant. Prove that the sum of their area is least when the side of square is double the radius of the circle.
question_html: The sum of the perimeter of a circle and square is k, where k is some constant. Prove that the sum of their area is least when the side of square is double the radius of the circle.
solution_text: r is the radius of circle and x be side of sq. {tex}2\pi r + 4x = K{/tex} {tex}s = \pi {r^2} + {x^2}{/tex} {tex}s = \pi {r^2} + {\left( {\frac{{K - 2\pi r}}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 2\pi r + 2\left( {\frac{{K - 2\pi r}}{4}} \right).\left( {0 - \frac{{2\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 2\pi r - \frac{\pi }{4}\left( {K - 2\pi r} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{r^2}}} = 2\pi - \frac{\pi }{4}\left( {0 - 2\pi } \right){/tex} {tex} = 2\pi + \frac{{2{\pi ^2}}}{4}{/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi }{4}\left( {k - 2\pi r} \right) = 2\pi r{/tex} {tex}k - 2\pi r = 8r{/tex} {tex}k = 8r + 2\pi r{/tex} {tex}\frac{K}{{2(4 + \pi )}} = r{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{r^2}}} > 0{/tex} Area is minimum at {tex}r = \frac{k}{{2(4 + \pi )}}{/tex} {tex}2\pi r + 4x = k{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{k - 2\pi r}}{4}{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{k - 2\pi \frac{k}{{2(4 + \pi )}}}}{4}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4k + k\pi - k\pi }}{{4(4 + \pi )}} = \frac{{4k}}{{4(4 + \pi )}}{/tex} x = 2r
solution_html:
r is the radius of circle and x be side of sq.

{tex}2\pi r + 4x = K{/tex}

{tex}s = \pi {r^2} + {x^2}{/tex}

{tex}s = \pi {r^2} + {\left( {\frac{{K - 2\pi r}}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 2\pi r + 2\left( {\frac{{K - 2\pi r}}{4}} \right).\left( {0 - \frac{{2\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 2\pi r - \frac{\pi }{4}\left( {K - 2\pi r} \right){/tex}

{tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{r^2}}} = 2\pi - \frac{\pi }{4}\left( {0 - 2\pi } \right){/tex}

{tex} = 2\pi + \frac{{2{\pi ^2}}}{4}{/tex}

For maximum/minimum

{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\frac{\pi }{4}\left( {k - 2\pi r} \right) = 2\pi r{/tex}

{tex}k - 2\pi r = 8r{/tex}

{tex}k = 8r + 2\pi r{/tex}

{tex}\frac{K}{{2(4 + \pi )}} = r{/tex}

Now {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{r^2}}} > 0{/tex}

Area is minimum at  {tex}r = \frac{k}{{2(4 + \pi )}}{/tex}

{tex}2\pi r + 4x = k{/tex}

{tex}x = \frac{{k - 2\pi r}}{4}{/tex}

{tex}x = \frac{{k - 2\pi \frac{k}{{2(4 + \pi )}}}}{4}{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{4k + k\pi - k\pi }}{{4(4 + \pi )}} = \frac{{4k}}{{4(4 + \pi )}}{/tex}

x = 2r

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A window is in the form of a rectangle surmounted by a semicircular opening. The total perimeter of the window is 10 m. Find the dimensions of the window to admit maximum light through the whole opening.
question_html: A window is in the form of a rectangle surmounted by a semicircular opening. The total perimeter of the window is 10 m. Find the dimensions of the window to admit maximum light through the whole opening.
solution_text: Let P be the perimeter of window {tex}P = 2x + 2r + \frac{1}{2} \times 2\pi r{/tex} {tex}10 =2x + 2r + \pi r{/tex} [P = 10] {tex}x = \frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}{/tex} Let A be area of window {tex}A = 2rx + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex} = 2r\left[ {\frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}} \right] + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex} = 10r - 2{r^2} - \pi {r^2} + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex} = 10r - 2{r^2} - \frac{{\pi {r^2}}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dr}} = 10 - 4r - \pi r{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{r^2}}} = - \left( {\pi + 4} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex}r = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{r^2}}} < 0{/tex} maximum {tex}x = \frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} Length of rectangle = 2r {tex} = \frac{{20}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} width {tex} = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let P be the perimeter of window
{tex}P = 2x + 2r + \frac{1}{2} \times 2\pi r{/tex} 
{tex}10 =2x + 2r + \pi r{/tex} [P = 10]
{tex}x = \frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}{/tex} 
Let A be area of window
{tex}A = 2rx + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} 
{tex} = 2r\left[ {\frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}} \right] + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} 
{tex} = 10r - 2{r^2} - \pi {r^2} + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} 
{tex} = 10r - 2{r^2} - \frac{{\pi {r^2}}}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dr}} = 10 - 4r - \pi r{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{r^2}}} = - \left( {\pi + 4} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}r = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{r^2}}} < 0{/tex} maximum
{tex}x = \frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}x = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} 
Length of rectangle = 2r {tex} = \frac{{20}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} 
width {tex} = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A point on the hypotenuse of a triangle is at distance a and b from the sides of the triangle. Show that the minimum length of the hypotenuse is {tex}(a^{\frac{2}{3}} + b^{\frac{2}{3}})^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}
question_html: A point on the hypotenuse of a triangle is at distance a and b from the sides of the triangle. Show that the minimum length of the hypotenuse is{tex}(a^{\frac{2}{3}} + b^{\frac{2}{3}})^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}AP = a\cos ec\theta {/tex} {tex}BP = b\sec \theta {/tex} {tex}l = AP + BP{/tex} {tex}l = a\cos ec\theta + b\sec \theta {/tex} {tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = -a\cos ec\theta \cot \theta + b\sec \theta .\tan \theta {/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}l}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = a\cos e{c^2}\theta + a\cos ec\theta {\cot ^2}\theta + {b^2} - {\sec ^3}\theta + b\sec \theta .{\tan ^2}\theta {/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = -a\cos ec\theta \cot \theta + b\sec \theta .\tan \theta {/tex} {tex}\frac{acos\theta}{sin^2\theta}=\frac{bsin\theta}{cos^2\theta}{/tex} {tex}{\tan ^3}\theta = \frac{a}{b}{/tex} {tex}\tan \theta = {\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right)^{1/3}}{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta = \frac{{{a^{1/3}}}}{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }},\cos \theta = \frac{{{b^{1/3}}}}{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}l}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} < 0{/tex} for {tex}\tan \theta = {\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right)^{1/3}}{/tex} l is minimum and minimum value of l is given by, {tex}l = a\cos ec\theta + b\sec \theta {/tex} {tex}=a\sqrt{1+cot^2\theta}+b\sqrt{1+tan^2\theta}{/tex} {tex}=a\sqrt{1+(\frac{b}{a})^{2/3}}+b\sqrt{1+(\frac{a}{b})^{2/3}}{/tex} {tex}=a^{2/3}\sqrt{a^{2/3}+b^{2/3}}+b^{2/3}\sqrt{b^{2/3}+a^{2/3}}{/tex} {tex}l = {\left( {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} \right)^{3/2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}AP = a\cos ec\theta {/tex} 
{tex}BP = b\sec \theta {/tex} 
{tex}l = AP + BP{/tex} 
{tex}l = a\cos ec\theta + b\sec \theta {/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = -a\cos ec\theta \cot \theta + b\sec \theta .\tan \theta {/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}l}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = a\cos e{c^2}\theta + a\cos ec\theta {\cot ^2}\theta + {b^2} - {\sec ^3}\theta + b\sec \theta .{\tan ^2}\theta {/tex} 
For maximum/minimum
{tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = -a\cos ec\theta \cot \theta + b\sec \theta .\tan \theta {/tex} 
{tex}\frac{acos\theta}{sin^2\theta}=\frac{bsin\theta}{cos^2\theta}{/tex} 
{tex}{\tan ^3}\theta = \frac{a}{b}{/tex} 
{tex}\tan \theta = {\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right)^{1/3}}{/tex} 
{tex}\sin \theta = \frac{{{a^{1/3}}}}{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }},\cos \theta = \frac{{{b^{1/3}}}}{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}l}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} < 0{/tex} for {tex}\tan \theta = {\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right)^{1/3}}{/tex} 
l  is minimum and minimum value of l is given by,
{tex}l = a\cos ec\theta + b\sec \theta {/tex} 
{tex}=a\sqrt{1+cot^2\theta}+b\sqrt{1+tan^2\theta}{/tex} 
{tex}=a\sqrt{1+(\frac{b}{a})^{2/3}}+b\sqrt{1+(\frac{a}{b})^{2/3}}{/tex} 
{tex}=a^{2/3}\sqrt{a^{2/3}+b^{2/3}}+b^{2/3}\sqrt{b^{2/3}+a^{2/3}}{/tex} 
{tex}l = {\left( {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} \right)^{3/2}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the points at which the function f given by f(x) = (x - 2) 4 (x + 1) 3 has local maxima local minima point of inflexion
question_html:
Find the points at which the function f given by f(x) = (x - 2)4 (x + 1)3 has

  1. local maxima
  2. local minima
  3. point of inflexion
solution_text: It is given that function is f(x) = (x - 2) 4 (x + 1) 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 4(x - 2) 3 (x + 1) 3 + 3(x + 1) 2 (x - 2) 4 = (x - 2) 3 (x + 1) 2 [4(x + 1) + 3(x - 2)] = (x - 2) 3 (x + 1) 2 (7x - 2) Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 and x = {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} or x = 2 Now, for values of x close to {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} and to the left of {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} f'(x) > 0 Also, for values of x close to {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} and to the right of {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} , f'(x) < 0. Then, x = {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} is the point of local maxima. Now, for values of x close to 2 and to the left of 2, f'(x) < 0. Also, for values of x close to 2 and to the right of 2, f'(x) > 0. Then, x = 2 is the point of local minima. Now, as the value of x varies through -1, f'(x) does not changes its sign. Then, x = -1 is the point of inflexion.
solution_html:
It is given that function is f(x) = (x - 2)4 (x + 1)3 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 4(x - 2)3 (x + 1)3 + 3(x + 1)2 (x - 2)4 
= (x - 2)3(x + 1)2 [4(x + 1) + 3(x - 2)]
= (x - 2)3(x + 1)2 (7x - 2)
Now, f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 and x = {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} or x = 2
Now, for values of x close to {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} and to the left of {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex}
f'(x) > 0
Also, for values of x close to {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} and to the right of {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex}, f'(x) < 0.
Then, x = {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex} is the point of local maxima.
Now, for values of x close to 2 and to the left of 2, f'(x) < 0.
Also, for values of x close to 2 and to the right of 2, f'(x) > 0.
Then, x = 2 is the point of local minima.
Now, as the value of x varies through -1, f'(x) does not changes its sign.
Then, x = -1 is the point of inflexion.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the absolute maximum and minimum values of the function f given by f(x) = cos 2 x + sin x, x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ]
question_html: Find the absolute maximum and minimum values of the function f given by f(x) = cos2 x + sin x, x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex}]
solution_text: It is given that f(x) = cos 2 x + sin x, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) = 2 cos x (-sin x) + cos x = -2 sin x cos x + cos x Now, if {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 sin x cos x = cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x (2 sin x - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or cos x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6},\frac{5\pi}{6}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} as {tex}\mathrm{x} \in[0, \pi]{/tex} Next, evaluating the value of f at critical points {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} and at the end points of the interval {tex}[0, \pi]{/tex} , (i.e. at x = 0 and {tex}x=\pi{/tex} ), we get, {tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{6}+\sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex} {tex}f(\frac{5\pi}{6}) = cos^2(\frac{5\pi}{6})+ sin(\frac{5\pi}{6}) = cos^2(\pi-\frac\pi6)+sin(\pi-\frac\pi6)=cos^2\frac\pi6-sin\frac\pi6=\frac54{/tex} f(0) = cos 2 0 + sin 0 = 1 + 0 = 1 {tex}f(\pi)=\cos ^{2} \pi+\sin \pi{/tex} = (-1) 2 + 0 = 1 {tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=0+1=1{/tex} Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f is {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} occurring at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} and the absolute minimum value of f is 1 occurring at x = 1, {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\pi{/tex} .
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = cos2 x + sin x, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} 
{tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) = 2 cos x (-sin x) + cos x
= -2 sin x cos x + cos x
Now, if {tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 sin x cos x = cos x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x (2 sin x - 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or cos x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6},\frac{5\pi}{6}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} as {tex}\mathrm{x} \in[0, \pi]{/tex} 
Next, evaluating the value of f at critical points {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} and at the end points of the interval {tex}[0, \pi]{/tex}, (i.e. at x = 0 and {tex}x=\pi{/tex} ), we get,
{tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{6}+\sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}f(\frac{5\pi}{6}) = cos^2(\frac{5\pi}{6})+ sin(\frac{5\pi}{6}) = cos^2(\pi-\frac\pi6)+sin(\pi-\frac\pi6)=cos^2\frac\pi6-sin\frac\pi6=\frac54{/tex}
f(0) = cos2 0 + sin 0 = 1 + 0 = 1
{tex}f(\pi)=\cos ^{2} \pi+\sin \pi{/tex} = (-1)2 + 0 = 1
{tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=0+1=1{/tex} 
Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f is {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} occurring at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} and the absolute minimum value of f is 1 occurring at x = 1, {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\pi{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the altitude of the right circular cone of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius r is {tex}\frac{4r}{3}{/tex} .
question_html: Show that the altitude of the right circular cone of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius r is {tex}\frac{4r}{3}{/tex}.
solution_text: Let R and h be the radius and height of the cone. r be the radius of sphere. {tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {R^2}H{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {R^2}.\left( {r + x} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi .\left( {{r^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( {r + x} \right)\,\,\left[ {\because {R^2} = {r^2} - {x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{r^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {r + x} \right)\left( {0 - 2x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - {x^2} - 2rx - 2{x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - 2rx - 3{x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - 3rx + rx - 3{x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {r\left( {r - 3x} \right) + x\left( {r - 3x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {r - 3x} \right)\left( {r + x} \right)} \right]{/tex} For critical points {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}r -3x=0 \implies r=3x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{r}{3} = x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {0 - 2r - 6x} \right]{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{r}{3}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ { - 2r - 6 \times \frac{r}{3}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ { - 4r} \right]{/tex} = - ve therefore, maximum Altitude = r + x {tex}= \frac{r}{3} + r{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4r}}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let R and h be the radius and height of the cone.
r be the radius of sphere.
{tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {R^2}H{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {R^2}.\left( {r + x} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi .\left( {{r^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( {r + x} \right)\,\,\left[ {\because {R^2} = {r^2} - {x^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{r^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {r + x} \right)\left( {0 - 2x} \right)} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - {x^2} - 2rx - 2{x^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - 2rx - 3{x^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - 3rx + rx - 3{x^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {r\left( {r - 3x} \right) + x\left( {r - 3x} \right)} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {r - 3x} \right)\left( {r + x} \right)} \right]{/tex}
For critical points{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}
{tex}r -3x=0 \implies r=3x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{r}{3} = x{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {0 - 2r - 6x} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{r}{3}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ { - 2r - 6 \times \frac{r}{3}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ { - 4r} \right]{/tex} 
= - ve
therefore, maximum
Altitude = r + x
{tex}= \frac{r}{3} + r{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{{4r}}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Let f be a function defined on [a, b] such that f ′(x) > 0, for all x {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b). Then prove that f is an increasing function on (a, b).
question_html: Let f be a function defined on [a, b] such that f ′(x) > 0, for all x {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b). Then prove that f is an increasing function on (a, b).
solution_text: Since, f'(x) > 0 on (a, b) Then, f is a differentiable function on (a, b) Also, every differentiable function is continuous, Therefore, f is continuous on [a, b] Let x 1 , x 2 {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b) such that, x 2 > x 1 then by LMV theorem, there exists c {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b) s.t. {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(x_{2})-f(x_{1})}{x_{2}-x_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x 2 ) - f(x 1 ) = (x 2 - x 1 ) {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (c) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x 2 ) - f(x 1 ) > 0 as x 2 > x 1 and {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x 2 ) > f(x 1 ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} for x 1 < x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x 1 ) < f(x 2 ) Therefore, f is an increasing function.
solution_html:
Since, f'(x) > 0 on (a, b)
Then, f is a differentiable function on (a, b)
Also, every differentiable function is continuous,
Therefore, f is continuous on [a, b]
Let x1, x2 {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b) such that, x2 > x1 then by LMV theorem, there exists c {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b) s.t.
{tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(x_{2})-f(x_{1})}{x_{2}-x_{1}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x2) - f(x1) = (x2 - x1){tex}f^\prime{/tex}(c)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x2) - f(x1) > 0 as x2 > x1 and {tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x2) > f(x1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} for x1 < x2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x1) < f(x2)
Therefore, f is an increasing function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the height of the cylinder of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius R is {tex}\frac{{2R}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} Also find the maximum volume.
question_html: Show that the height of the cylinder of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius R is {tex}\frac{{2R}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} Also find the maximum volume.
solution_text: {tex}V = \pi {r^2}.2x{/tex} {tex} [\because OL = x , LM = 2x ]{/tex} {tex} = \pi .({a^2} - {x^2}).2x{/tex} {tex}V = 2\pi ({a^2}x - {x^3}){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 2\pi ({a^2} - 3{x^2}){/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2\pi \left[ {0 - 6x} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - 12\pi x{/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}2\pi [{a^2} - 3{x^2}] = 0{/tex} {tex}{a^2} = 3{x^2} \Rightarrow \sqrt {\frac{{{a^2}}}{3}} = x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = - 12\pi .\frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} = negative maximum Volume is maximum at {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} Height of cylinder of maximum volume is = 2x {tex} = 2 \times \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2a}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}V = \pi {r^2}.2x{/tex} {tex} [\because OL = x , LM = 2x ]{/tex} 
{tex} = \pi .({a^2} - {x^2}).2x{/tex} 
{tex}V = 2\pi ({a^2}x - {x^3}){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 2\pi ({a^2} - 3{x^2}){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2\pi \left[ {0 - 6x} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = - 12\pi x{/tex} 
For maximum/minimum
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}2\pi [{a^2} - 3{x^2}] = 0{/tex} 
{tex}{a^2} = 3{x^2} \Rightarrow \sqrt {\frac{{{a^2}}}{3}} = x{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} 
{tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = - 12\pi .\frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} 
= negative maximum
Volume is maximum at {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} 
Height of cylinder of maximum volume is
= 2x
{tex} = 2 \times \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{{2a}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that height of the cylinder of greatest volume which can be inscribed in a right circular cone of height h and semi vertical angle α is one-third that of the cone and the greatest volume of cylinder is {tex}\frac{4}{{27}}{\pi}h^3\tan^2 \alpha .{/tex}
question_html: Show that height of the cylinder of greatest volume which can be inscribed in a right circular cone of height h and semi vertical angle α is one-third that of the cone and the greatest volume of cylinder is {tex}\frac{4}{{27}}{\pi}h^3\tan^2 \alpha .{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\frac{{vo'}}{x} = \cot \alpha {/tex} {tex}vo' = x\cot \alpha {/tex} {tex}oo' = h - x\cot \alpha {/tex} {tex}V = \pi {x^2}.(h - x\cot \alpha ){/tex} {tex}V = \pi {x^2}h - \pi {x^3}\cot \alpha {/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 2\pi xh - 3\pi {x^2}\cot \alpha {/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}2\pi xh - 3\pi {x^2}\cot \alpha = 0{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{2h}}{3}\tan \alpha {/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2\pi h - 6\pi x\cot \alpha {/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{{2h}}{3}\tan \alpha }} = \pi (2h - 4h){/tex} Volume is maximum at {tex}x = \frac{{2h}}{3}\tan \alpha {/tex} Maximum volume is {tex}V = \pi .{x^2}(h - x\cot \alpha ){/tex} {tex} = \pi {\left( {\frac{{2h}}{3}\tan \alpha } \right)^2}\left[ {h - \frac{{2h}}{3}\tan \alpha \cot \alpha } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \pi .\frac{{4{h^2}}}{9}{\tan ^2}\alpha .\frac{h}{3}{/tex} {tex}V = \frac{4}{{27}}\pi {h^3}{\tan ^2}\alpha {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{{vo'}}{x} = \cot \alpha {/tex} 
{tex}vo' = x\cot \alpha {/tex} 
{tex}oo' = h - x\cot \alpha {/tex} 
{tex}V = \pi {x^2}.(h - x\cot \alpha ){/tex} 
{tex}V = \pi {x^2}h - \pi {x^3}\cot \alpha {/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 2\pi xh - 3\pi {x^2}\cot \alpha {/tex} 
For maximum/minimum
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}2\pi xh - 3\pi {x^2}\cot \alpha = 0{/tex} 
{tex}x = \frac{{2h}}{3}\tan \alpha {/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2\pi h - 6\pi x\cot \alpha {/tex} 
{tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{{2h}}{3}\tan \alpha }} = \pi (2h - 4h){/tex} 
Volume is maximum at  {tex}x = \frac{{2h}}{3}\tan \alpha {/tex} 
Maximum volume is 
{tex}V = \pi .{x^2}(h - x\cot \alpha ){/tex} 
{tex} = \pi {\left( {\frac{{2h}}{3}\tan \alpha } \right)^2}\left[ {h - \frac{{2h}}{3}\tan \alpha \cot \alpha } \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = \pi .\frac{{4{h^2}}}{9}{\tan ^2}\alpha .\frac{h}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}V = \frac{4}{{27}}\pi {h^3}{\tan ^2}\alpha {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A cylindrical tank of radius 10 m is being filled with wheat at the rate of 314 cubic metre per hour. Then the depth of the wheat is increasing at the rate of:
question_html: A cylindrical tank of radius 10 m is being filled with wheat at the rate of 314 cubic metre per hour. Then the depth of the wheat is increasing at the rate of:

options.explanation: Let V be the volume of the cylinder Then, {tex}{V}=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \mathrm{h}{/tex} = {tex}\pi(10)^{2} h{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = 100 {tex}\pi{/tex} h Differentiating w.r.t. t we get, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{dt}}=100 \pi \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} The tank is being filled with wheat at the rate of 314 cubic meters per hour. i.e, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{dt}}=314 ~~\mathrm{m}^{3} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} Then, we have {tex}314=100 \pi \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{314}{100 \times 3.14}=1{/tex} Therefore, the depth of wheat is increasing at the rate of 1 m/h.
